Brute force: 'for each bar, expand until shorter' — O(n²). Mention then move on.
Insight: 'for each bar as the SHORTEST, find left and right shorter bounds. Then area = h × (right - left - 1)'.
Pitch monotonic increasing stack: 'pop when incoming is shorter; the popped bar finds its right bound here, left bound is the new top'.

Code: stack = []. max_area = 0. for i, h in enumerate(heights): while stack and heights[stack[-1]] > h: top = stack.pop(); width = i if not stack else i - stack[-1] - 1; max_area = max(max_area, heights[top] * width). stack.append(i). Then drain at end with i = len(heights).
Walk [2,1,5,6,2,3]: detailed trace showing pops at i=1 (pops 2), at i=4 (pops 6 then 5), final drain pops 3, 2, 1.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 15-25 min. Follow-up: Maximal Rectangle (in a binary matrix — apply this algorithm to each row of cumulative heights).
dont_do: Don't write O(n²) — TLE.
Don't store heights on stack.
Don't forget the final drain.
Don't get the width formula wrong — it's (i - new_top - 1), not (i - popped).

approaches.name: Monotonic Increasing Stack
approaches.code:
def largestRectangleArea(heights):
    stack = []  # indices with strictly increasing heights
    max_area = 0
    n = len(heights)
    
    for i in range(n):
        while stack and heights[stack[-1]] > heights[i]:
            top = stack.pop()
            # Width is i - (new stack top) - 1, or i if stack now empty
            width = i if not stack else i - stack[-1] - 1
            max_area = max(max_area, heights[top] * width)
        stack.append(i)
    
    # Drain remaining bars with virtual right boundary at n
    while stack:
        top = stack.pop()
        width = n if not stack else n - stack[-1] - 1
        max_area = max(max_area, heights[top] * width)
    
    return max_area
approaches.explanation: Maintain a stack of indices where heights are strictly increasing. When a shorter bar appears, every taller bar still on the stack has found its 'next shorter to the right' (the current bar). Pop each, compute its rectangle with width = i - new_top - 1. After the loop, drain the stack as if a bar of height 0 sat at index n. Each index is pushed and popped at most once → O(n) total.

approaches.name: Brute Force (Don't ship this)
approaches.explanation: For each pair (i, j) compute the min height in [i..j] and area = min_h × (j - i + 1). Correct but O(n²) — TLEs on n = 10^5. Useful only as a conversational stepping stone.

title: Invert Binary Tree
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, invert the tree (mirror it left-to-right at every level), and return the root. (Here the function takes a level-order list and returns the inverted level-order list.)
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 100].

examples.explanation: Each node's left and right children are swapped at every level.
Root has left=1, right=3 → swap → left=3, right=1.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Simplest tree problem. At every node, swap its left and right children. Then recurse into both. Or invert by post-order: recurse first, then swap. Both work. Iterative BFS works too — process every node in a queue, swap its children, enqueue both.

clarifying_questions.question: Are we modifying in-place or returning a new tree?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says modify in place and return the (same) root. Building a new tree wastes memory and time.

clarifying_questions.question: What's an empty tree's inverse?
clarifying_questions.why: Empty. Trivial but worth confirming you handle root = None without crashing.

clarifying_questions.question: Iterative or recursive — preference?
clarifying_questions.why: Recursive is cleaner; iterative shows you can use a queue. Both are fine answers; ask if they have a preference.

clarifying_questions.question: Is the tree always a BST?
clarifying_questions.why: No — just a binary tree. (BST inversion would BREAK the BST property; this problem is about generic binary trees.)

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Recursive: at root, swap left and right; recurse into both. Base case: root is None — return None. Three lines of code. Iterative with BFS: queue starts with root; for each node, swap its children, enqueue them. Both are O(n) with O(h) or O(n) extra space respectively.

edge_cases.details: Return None. Recursive base case handles it.
No children to swap. Just return the node.
After inversion becomes right-only. Each node's right becomes left.
Every internal node swaps. The result is a mirror image.
Doesn't matter — inversion is positional, not value-based.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to recurse after swapping — only swaps top level.
Recursing BEFORE swapping in a pre-order... actually works! Just be consistent.
Returning the inverted subtree instead of mutating — wastes memory but correct.
Not handling None at root — crashes immediately.
Trying to use BST property — there's none here.

faang.introduction: The 'Max-Howell-couldn't-invert-a-binary-tree' question. Famous for being asked at Google and being trivially short — but you have to actually do it right.
faang.introduction_context: Phone screen across FAANG. Famously asked of Max Howell (Homebrew creator). Solving it in 30 seconds is the bar; longer than 2 minutes raises eyebrows.
really_testing: Basic comfort with recursive tree traversal.

rubric.criteria: Codes it in 30-60 seconds. Mentions both recursive and iterative as alternatives.
Codes it in 2-3 min, correctly.
Needs prompting to start. Or makes the None-handling mistake.
Cannot derive the recursion. Tries to build a new tree from scratch.

playbook: Restate: 'mirror every level — swap left and right at every node'.
Pitch: 'recurse, swap, return'. 3 lines.
Code: def invert(node): if not node: return None. node.left, node.right = invert(node.right), invert(node.left). return node.
Mention iterative alternative: BFS with queue, swap each node's children as we visit.
Edge: empty tree returns None. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 1-3 min. Sometimes the interviewer follows up with Same Tree or Symmetric Tree.
dont_do: Don't overthink it — 3 lines.
Don't build a new tree — mutate in place.
Don't forget the None base case.

approaches.name: Recursive (DFS)
approaches.explanation: Standard recursive answer. At every node, swap left and right (recursively-inverted subtrees). Base case: None. Three lines of work per node, O(n) total. Recursion depth is the tree height (worst case n for a skewed tree, log n for balanced).

approaches.name: Iterative BFS (queue)
approaches.explanation: BFS variant. Use a queue; pop a node, swap its children, enqueue both (now-swapped) children. Avoids recursion — useful if the interviewer asks for non-recursive, or if the tree is deeper than the call stack can handle.

title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth — the number of nodes along the longest path from the root to the deepest leaf.
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 10^4].

examples.explanation: Three levels: 3 → (9, 20) → (15, 7). Longest path has 3 nodes.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Trivial recursion. Empty tree: depth 0. Otherwise: 1 + max(depth of left subtree, depth of right subtree). Done. BFS works too — count levels.

clarifying_questions.question: Depth counted in nodes or edges?
clarifying_questions.why: Nodes. Single root = depth 1, not 0. Empty tree = 0. Clarifies the +1 vs +0.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty tree case?
clarifying_questions.why: Depth 0. Recursive base case.

clarifying_questions.question: Recursive or iterative?
clarifying_questions.why: Recursive is simpler. Iterative BFS counts levels. Either is fine — ask preference.

clarifying_questions.question: Bound on tree size?
clarifying_questions.why: Up to 10^4. Recursion depth could match — generally fine, but if you're worried about stack overflow, mention BFS.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Three-line recursive function. if not root: return 0; return 1 + max(maxDepth(root.left), maxDepth(root.right)). For BFS: queue with (node, depth) pairs, return the maximum depth seen.

edge_cases.details: Depth 0. Base case.
Depth 1. Both subtrees are empty (depth 0); 1 + max(0, 0) = 1.
Depth = number of nodes (worst case for balanced perception).
Depth = log2(n+1). Sanity check.
For n=10^4 skewed, recursion could overflow. Iterative BFS avoids it.

common_mistakes: Returning 0 for single root — off-by-one (forgot the +1).
Using max() without the +1 — counts levels minus 1.
Confusing depth (nodes) with height (edges) — interview spec varies.
Not handling None — crashes on root.left when root is None.
Iterative without tracking depth properly (e.g., BFS where you forget to count levels).

faang.introduction: Easiest tree problem. Tests basic recursive structure on a tree.
faang.introduction_context: Phone screen filler everywhere. If you can't solve this in 1 min, the rest of the interview gets harder. Often a warmup before a harder tree problem.

rubric.criteria: Codes the 3-line recursion in 30s. Mentions BFS as alternative.
Codes correctly in 2 min.
Off-by-one (returns nodes - 1 or levels - 1).
Cannot derive recursion.

playbook: Restate: 'count nodes on longest root-to-leaf path'.
Pitch: 'recursive — 1 + max(left depth, right depth), base case 0'.
Code: if not root: return 0. return 1 + max(maxDepth(root.left), maxDepth(root.right)).
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 1-2 min. Follow-up: Balanced Binary Tree (use depth and check |L-R| <= 1).
dont_do: Don't write 5+ lines for a 3-line recursion.
Don't forget the +1.
Don't return early without recursing into both subtrees.

approaches.name: Recursive (DFS)
approaches.explanation: The textbook 3-line recursion. Empty tree = 0; otherwise the depth is 1 (for the current node) plus the max depth of its two subtrees. Each node visited once → O(n). Recursion depth equals tree height.

approaches.name: Iterative BFS (level counting)
approaches.space: O(w) where w is the max width
approaches.explanation: BFS by levels. Each iteration of the outer loop processes exactly one level — increment the counter and drain. Avoids deep recursion stacks. Good answer when the tree might be skewed and the interviewer mentions stack-overflow concerns.

title: Same Tree
summary: Given the roots of two binary trees p and q, return True if they are the same tree — same structure AND same node values. (Here the function takes two level-order lists.)
constraints: The number of nodes in both trees is in the range [0, 100].

examples.explanation: Same values, different structure (left vs right child).

starter_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

def build_tree(values):
    if not values:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(values[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(values):
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

def isSameTree(p_values, q_values):
    # Pretend the signature is isSameTree(p: TreeNode, q: TreeNode) -> bool.
    p = build_tree(p_values)
    q = build_tree(q_values)
    
    # Your code: recursive structural+value comparison.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Recursive comparison. If both nodes are None → True (matching nulls). If one is None and the other isn't → False. If values differ → False. Otherwise recurse into both pairs of children. Three lines of logic, mirrored on left and right.

clarifying_questions.question: Are values guaranteed comparable (ints)?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per constraints. So == works directly.

clarifying_questions.question: Two empty trees — should they be considered same?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Both None → True is the natural base case.

clarifying_questions.question: Structural match — same parent-child relations?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — left children compare with left, right with right. NOT 'same set of values'.

clarifying_questions.question: Recursive or iterative?
clarifying_questions.why: Recursive is natural. Iterative (with two queues or a queue of pairs) works if recursion depth is a concern.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Recursion on the structure. Base cases: both None → True; exactly one None → False. Recursive case: same values AND recursively same-left AND recursively same-right. Iterate as a queue of (p, q) node pairs if avoiding recursion.

edge_cases.details: True. Base case fires immediately.
False. Caught by the 'one None' check.
True if values equal, recurse into (None, None) twice (both True).
False — returns False at the mismatching pair, short-circuits the rest.
False — caught by None vs non-None at a child position.

common_mistakes: Comparing values without comparing structure — e.g., tree-to-list comparison breaks because [1,None,2] and [1,2] both serialize to confusable forms.
Forgetting one of the base cases — usually 'both None'.
Returning False on (None, None) — should be True.
Comparing left to right (swap bug) — left must match left.
Using a queue but forgetting to enqueue both children's pairs in order.

faang.introduction: Foundational tree recursion. Common warm-up before harder tree problems.
faang.introduction_context: Phone screen at Amazon, Microsoft, Bloomberg. Often used as a sub-routine (e.g., in Subtree of Another Tree).
really_testing: Recursive tree traversal with two trees in lockstep.
Base case enumeration: both None, one None, values differ.

rubric.criteria: Codes the 4-line recursion in 1-2 min. Walks through (None, None) and (None, non-None) cases.
Solves correctly with one self-corrected bug.
Off-by-one on base cases (None handling).
Tries to serialize and compare lists — fails on ambiguity.

playbook: Restate: 'same structure and same values'.
Recursive pseudocode: if both None → True. If one None → False. If values differ → False. Else recurse on left+right.
Code: def same(p, q): if not p and not q: return True. if not p or not q: return False. return p.val == q.val and same(p.left, q.left) and same(p.right, q.right).
Walk identical [1,2,3] vs [1,2,3]: roots match (1==1); left subtrees (2,2) match; right subtrees (3,3) match. True. ✓
Walk [1,2] vs [1,None,2]: root 1==1; left (2 vs None) → False.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 2-4 min. Direct follow-up: Subtree of Another Tree, Symmetric Tree.
dont_do: Don't serialize trees and compare strings — ambiguous.
Don't forget the 'both None → True' base case.
Don't compare left to right.
Don't use BFS for elegance — DFS is shorter.

approaches.name: Recursive Comparison
approaches.explanation: Standard recursive answer. Base cases: both None (match), exactly one None (mismatch). Recursive case: values match AND left subtrees match AND right subtrees match. Short-circuits at the first mismatch — runs in O(min(|p|, |q|)) in the worst case but often much less.

approaches.name: Iterative BFS with Pairs
approaches.explanation: BFS with node pairs in the queue. Same logic as the recursive version, just expressed iteratively. Useful when deep recursion is a concern. Each pair is processed once.

title: Diameter of Binary Tree
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, return the length of the diameter — the longest path between any two nodes, measured in edges. The path may or may not pass through the root.
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [1, 10^4].

examples.explanation: Just one edge between the two nodes.

starter_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

def build_tree(values):
    if not values:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(values[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(values):
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

def diameterOfBinaryTree(values):
    # Pretend the signature is diameterOfBinaryTree(root: TreeNode) -> int.
    root = build_tree(values)
    
    # Your code: at each node, diameter through this node = left_depth + right_depth.
    # Track the max across the recursion.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Insight: the diameter through any node = depth(left) + depth(right) (in edges). The global diameter is the max over all nodes. So define a depth function that ALSO updates the diameter at each call. Returns depth (for the parent to use); side-effects the global max.

clarifying_questions.question: Diameter measured in nodes or edges?
clarifying_questions.why: Edges. So a single node has diameter 0. [1,2] has diameter 1. Critical to get right.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the longest path NOT pass through the root?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. The diameter is the max over all nodes of (left_depth + right_depth). Often the answer lives in a deep subtree.

clarifying_questions.question: Should I return the diameter, or both endpoints?
clarifying_questions.why: Just the count of edges. Endpoints would be more work.

clarifying_questions.question: What's the diameter of a single-node tree?
clarifying_questions.why: 0 — no edges to traverse.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the tree be empty?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints, n >= 1, so no. Defensive 0 for empty is cheap.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Define depth(node) returning the depth in edges. As a side effect, at every node compute left_depth + right_depth and update a 'max' variable. The trick: depth(None) = -1 makes the arithmetic clean. OR depth(None) = 0 and then a leaf has depth 0; through-leaf diameter is 0+0=0; through-leaf-with-two-children is 1+1=2 edges. Both conventions work — pick one and be consistent.

edge_cases.details: Diameter 0 — no edges. depth(node) = 0 if both children None.
Diameter 1 — one edge.
Common case. left_depth + right_depth at root is the answer.
Need to consider every node. That's why we update max at EVERY node, not just root.
Diameter = n - 1 (height - 1). All edges form the path.

common_mistakes: Returning depth + 1 instead of just depth — confuses diameter with depth.
Counting in nodes instead of edges (off-by-one).
Only checking diameter at the root — misses deep-subtree maxima.
Computing depth in O(n) for each node → O(n²) total. Use the single-pass max-tracking trick.
Forgetting to update max at every recursive call — only checks root's diameter.

faang.introduction: Classic 'compute X at every node and take the max' pattern. The optimal solution combines a depth helper with an outer-scope counter.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Microsoft, Bloomberg. Quick interview question if you've seen the pattern; tricky if you haven't.
really_testing: Spotting that diameter through a node = left_depth + right_depth.
Combining recursion's return value (depth) with a side effect (max update).
Edge count vs node count discipline.
Avoiding O(n²) by not recomputing depth from scratch.

rubric.criteria: States the 'depth + side-effect max' pattern. Codes in 5-7 min. Walks through diameter-not-through-root case.
Solves with O(n²) (recompute depth at every node), recognizes inefficiency, refactors.
Counts in nodes not edges. Or only checks diameter at root.
Cannot derive any traversal-based approach.

playbook: Restate: 'longest path between any two nodes, in edges'.
Insight: 'diameter through a node = depth(left) + depth(right)'.
Trick: 'compute depth in one DFS, side-effecting a max counter'.

Code: diameter = 0. def depth(node): nonlocal diameter; if not node: return 0; L = depth(node.left); R = depth(node.right); diameter = max(diameter, L + R); return 1 + max(L, R). depth(root). return diameter.
Walk [1,2,3,4,5]: at root, L=2, R=1, diameter = 3. ✓
Walk diameter-not-through-root: subtree with deeper branches has larger L+R at its root. Max captures it.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 6-10 min. Follow-up: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum (Hard) — same pattern with values instead of depth.
dont_do: Don't compute depth at every node from scratch — O(n²).
Don't only check the root's diameter.
Don't count in nodes when spec says edges.
Don't forget the +1 in `1 + max(L, R)` when returning depth.

approaches.name: DFS with Side-Effect Max
approaches.explanation: Single DFS pass. The depth helper returns the depth of the subtree (for parents to use) and as a side effect updates the global maximum diameter — measured as left + right depths at every node. Each node visited once → O(n). Outer-scope `nonlocal diameter` keeps the count simple.

title: Balanced Binary Tree
summary: Given a binary tree, determine if it is height-balanced — for every node, the heights of its left and right subtrees differ by at most 1.
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 5000].

examples.explanation: Every node's subtrees differ in height by at most 1.
Subtree at root has left height 4 and right height 2 — differ by 2.

starter_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

def build_tree(values):
    if not values:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(values[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(values):
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

def isBalanced(values):
    # Pretend the signature is isBalanced(root: TreeNode) -> bool.
    root = build_tree(values)
    
    # Your code: compute height bottom-up, propagate -1 if any subtree is unbalanced.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: The naive O(n²): for every node, compute heights of both subtrees and check the difference. Better: a bottom-up DFS that returns height and short-circuits with a sentinel (-1) if any subtree is unbalanced. Single pass, O(n).

clarifying_questions.question: Height definition — nodes or edges?
clarifying_questions.why: Conventionally edges in LC, but it doesn't matter for THIS problem since we only compare differences. Either works.

clarifying_questions.question: Is an empty tree balanced?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — trivially. Height 0 (or -1), no imbalance possible.

clarifying_questions.question: Difference of 1 allowed at EVERY node, or only at root?
clarifying_questions.why: Every node. Even if root's two subtrees differ by 0, a deep subtree could still be unbalanced. Must check recursively.

clarifying_questions.question: Can I do it in O(n)?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — bottom-up DFS that returns 'height OR sentinel -1'. The naïve top-down version is O(n²).

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Two approaches. Top-down: for every node, compute heights of children — O(n) per node × n nodes = O(n²). Bottom-up: DFS returns height of subtree, or -1 if subtree is unbalanced. At each node, recurse left and right; if either returned -1 or |L - R| > 1, return -1. Else return 1 + max(L, R). Final check: is the returned value != -1? O(n).

edge_cases.details: True (height 0, no imbalance).
True. Both subtrees empty, heights 0, diff 0.
True. One child empty (height 0), other height 1. Diff 1 → OK.
False. Long chain creates a height diff > 1 somewhere.
False. The sentinel -1 propagates up; the entire tree is reported unbalanced.

common_mistakes: Top-down O(n²) — fine for small inputs, but the interviewer wants O(n).
Only checking root's balance — misses deep imbalances.
Comparing heights without taking abs — directional difference can be negative.
Forgetting to propagate the -1 sentinel — algorithm keeps computing even after detecting imbalance.
Off-by-one in 'differ by AT MOST 1' (must be ≤, not <).

faang.introduction: Tree DP / bottom-up DFS classic. Tests whether you can avoid the O(n²) top-down trap.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Microsoft. Also pairs well with Diameter as 'both use the same pattern' question.
really_testing: Bottom-up DFS with a sentinel for early exit.
Returning 'height OR fail' from a single recursive call.
Checking balance AT EVERY NODE, not just root.

rubric.criteria: Codes the O(n) bottom-up version directly. Clean sentinel logic. 5-7 min.
Codes O(n²) first, refactors to O(n) when prompted.
Only checks root's balance. Or buggy sentinel propagation.
Cannot derive any working recursion.

playbook: Restate: 'every node's two subtrees differ in height by at most 1'.
Naïve: 'compute height at every node and check' — O(n²).
Optimization: 'one DFS, returns height OR -1 if unbalanced'.

Code: def check(node): if not node: return 0. L = check(node.left); if L == -1: return -1. R = check(node.right); if R == -1: return -1. if abs(L-R) > 1: return -1. return 1 + max(L, R). return check(root) != -1.
Walk balanced [3,9,20,N,N,15,7]: heights at every node fit; returns 3.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 5-8 min. Follow-up: 'redo for AVL balance (factor ±1)' — same algorithm.
dont_do: Don't ship O(n²) without mentioning the optimization.
Don't only check root.
Don't forget to propagate -1.
Don't compare heights with strict < — use abs(L - R) > 1.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up DFS with Sentinel
approaches.explanation: Single DFS pass. Each call returns either the subtree's height OR -1 if the subtree (or any deeper subtree) is unbalanced. Once a -1 appears, it propagates all the way up — we never compute past it. O(n) total work.

approaches.name: Top-Down (Don't ship this)
approaches.explanation: Top-down: at every node, recompute heights of both subtrees and check. Correct but O(n²) — each node's height is computed many times. Useful only as a stepping stone in conversation.

title: Subtree of Another Tree
summary: Given the roots of two binary trees root and subRoot, return True if there is a subtree of root with the same structure and node values as subRoot.
constraints: Number of nodes in root is in [1, 2000].
Number of nodes in subRoot is in [1, 1000].

examples.explanation: The left subtree of root matches subRoot exactly.
The subtree at node 4 has an extra '0' below it, so it doesn't match subRoot.

starter_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

def build_tree(values):
    if not values:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(values[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(values):
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

def isSubtree(root_values, sub_values):
    # Pretend the signature is isSubtree(root: TreeNode, subRoot: TreeNode) -> bool.
    root = build_tree(root_values)
    sub = build_tree(sub_values)
    
    # Your code: walk root, and at each node check if subtree there is identical to sub.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Two-step recursion. Outer recursion walks every node of root, asking 'does the subtree rooted here equal subRoot?'. Inner check is the standard Same Tree comparison. O(m × n) in the worst case where m = |root|, n = |subRoot|. For competitive scenarios there's an O(m + n) trick using KMP on a serialization, but the O(mn) DFS is what's expected.

clarifying_questions.question: What if subRoot is empty?
clarifying_questions.why: By convention an empty tree is a subtree of any tree. Return True. Worth confirming.

clarifying_questions.question: What if root is empty but subRoot isn't?
clarifying_questions.why: False. Recursion's base case (root None, sub not None) handles it.

clarifying_questions.question: Match means structure AND values exact?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Same Tree semantics — every position must match, including None positions.

clarifying_questions.question: Can root and subRoot be the same tree?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Trivially True. Top-level Same Tree call returns immediately.

clarifying_questions.question: Any performance concern at n=2000, m=1000?
clarifying_questions.why: O(2 * 10^6) — fine. No need for the KMP optimization in a basic interview.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Two passes work together: walk every node of root, and at each one ask 'is the subtree rooted here Same as subRoot?'. The Same Tree comparison is a separate helper. Recursion: isSubtree(root, sub) = same(root, sub) OR isSubtree(root.left, sub) OR isSubtree(root.right, sub). Base: if root is None return sub is None.

edge_cases.details: True — empty matches everywhere.
False.
True at the outermost Same Tree call.
Outer recursion descends into both children until Same Tree matches.
False — Same Tree catches the extra null vs node.

common_mistakes: Comparing by values only — misses structural differences.
Skipping the base case for Same Tree (both None case).
Trying to find subRoot using just values — fails when values repeat.
Forgetting to recurse into BOTH children of root (only checking left).
Confusing 'subtree' with 'sub-set of nodes' — they're different (subtree must be a full subtree).

faang.introduction: Tree recursion with a helper. Tests composing two recursive functions cleanly.
faang.introduction_context: Phone screen at Amazon, Bloomberg. Often follows Same Tree directly — interviewer says 'and now find this as a subtree'.
really_testing: Composing isSameTree as a helper inside a larger recursion.
Understanding 'subtree' means structure AND values match exactly.
Base case discipline (both None, one None, etc.).

rubric.criteria: Reaches for Same Tree as helper instantly. Codes both in 5-7 min.
Solves correctly with one base-case bug self-fixed.
Compares by values only or forgets one base case.
Cannot derive the two-recursion structure.

playbook: Restate: 'is subRoot identical to some subtree of root?'
Decompose: 'helper isSame(a, b); main isSubtree(root, sub) = isSame OR recurse left OR recurse right'.

Code: def isSame(a, b): if not a and not b: return True. if not a or not b: return False. return a.val == b.val and isSame(a.left, b.left) and isSame(a.right, b.right). def isSubtree(root, sub): if not sub: return True. if not root: return False. return isSame(root, sub) or isSubtree(root.left, sub) or isSubtree(root.right, sub).
Walk root=[3,4,5,1,2] sub=[4,1,2]: at root.left=4, isSame(4-subtree, sub) → True. Return True. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 6-10 min. Follow-up: 'O(m+n)?' — serialize and use KMP.
dont_do: Don't compare by values only.
Don't forget either base case.
Don't recurse only on left or only on right.
Don't write a 'maybe a subtree' check that returns True for partial matches.

approaches.name: Two-Recursion DFS
approaches.explanation: Two recursions working together. isSame is the standard Same Tree check. check walks every node of root, asking if the subtree there matches sub. Worst case O(m × n) because we might do a full Same Tree comparison at every node of root, but usually much less due to short-circuiting on first mismatch.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree
summary: Given a binary search tree (BST) and two nodes p and q (given by value), return the lowest common ancestor (LCA) of those two nodes — the deepest node that is an ancestor of both.
constraints: p != q. Both p and q exist in the BST.

examples.explanation: Root 6 is the LCA — 2 is in the left subtree, 8 in the right.
2 is an ancestor of 4 (and trivially of itself). LCA = 2.

starter_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

def build_tree(values):
    if not values:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(values[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(values):
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

def lowestCommonAncestor(values, p, q):
    # Pretend the signature is lowestCommonAncestor(root, p: TreeNode, q: TreeNode) -> TreeNode.
    # Here p and q are passed by their values.
    # Return the LCA node's value.
    root = build_tree(values)
    
    # Your code: navigate the BST. If p and q both < node, go left. If both > node, go right.
    # Otherwise this node is the LCA.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: BST property is the trick. Walk from root. If both p.val and q.val are LESS than current node, LCA is in the left subtree → go left. If both GREATER, go right. Otherwise — they split here, so this node IS the LCA. O(h) time, O(1) extra space iteratively.

clarifying_questions.question: Is the tree guaranteed to be a BST?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — this is the BST variant. Don't confuse with the general binary tree version (LC 236), which can't use the ordering trick.

clarifying_questions.question: Are p and q guaranteed to exist?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes, per spec. So we don't have to handle 'not found'.

clarifying_questions.question: Can a node be an ancestor of itself?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. If p == q's ancestor, LCA = p. If p == q (not in this problem but worth confirming), LCA = p.

clarifying_questions.question: Recursive or iterative?
clarifying_questions.why: Iterative is cleaner (no stack), but recursive is fine. Both are O(h) time.

clarifying_questions.question: Why is this Medium, not Easy?
clarifying_questions.why: Because students who don't know the BST trick try to do the general algorithm (track paths, etc.), which is much more complex. Recognizing the BST shortcut is the test.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: BST navigation. Compare both target values against the current node. If both smaller → answer is to the left. If both larger → answer is to the right. If they straddle (one smaller, one larger, or one equals current) → current node is the LCA (the split point). Iterative implementation: while node: if both < val: node = node.left. elif both > val: node = node.right. else: return node.

edge_cases.category: One of p, q is an ancestor of the other
edge_cases.details: Algorithm returns the ancestor. The split happens exactly there.
Root is the LCA. Algorithm returns root immediately.
Walk left. Recurses until split.
Walk right. Recurses until split.
Root is LCA of root and its only child.

common_mistakes: Forgetting the BST property and writing the general (LC 236) algorithm — works but overkill.
Using > and < without handling equality — equals means 'this is the ancestor itself'.
Not normalizing (min, max) of p and q — easy to handle both orderings with comparisons directly.
Recursing past the LCA — failing to stop when the values straddle.
Comparing nodes by identity when the problem gives values — make sure to compare values.

faang.introduction: BST navigation classic. The interviewer is watching for whether you exploit the BST ordering — solving this without it is much harder.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Meta, Microsoft. Often a warm-up before LCA of a general binary tree (LC 236).
really_testing: Recognizing BST property as the shortcut.
Clean iterative loop with three branches (both <, both >, otherwise).
Comfort comparing values, not node identities.

rubric.criteria: Uses BST property in 1 min, codes the iterative loop in 3-5 min.
Recursive solution; correct but doesn't initially exploit BST. Refactors when prompted.
Writes general LCA algorithm; can't simplify even with hints.
Cannot derive any working LCA. Or tries to walk from leaves up.

playbook: Restate: 'lowest common ancestor in a BST'.
Insight: 'BST split point = LCA'.
Code: node = root. while node: if p < node.val and q < node.val: node = node.left. elif p > node.val and q > node.val: node = node.right. else: return node.val.
Walk [6,2,8,...] with p=2, q=8: 2 < 6 and 8 > 6 → split at root. Return 6. ✓
Walk p=7, q=9: both > 6 → go right to 8. Both > 8? 7 < 8, so split. Return 8. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 3-5 min. Follow-up: LCA in a general binary tree (LC 236) — completely different algorithm (post-order traversal, return non-None on either side).
dont_do: Don't write the general algorithm — BST shortcut is the point.
Don't forget to handle 'p == current' (use OR equals).
Don't compare nodes by identity — use values.
Don't ascend (walk up) — that requires parent pointers.

approaches.name: Iterative BST Navigation
approaches.explanation: The canonical BST solution. Walk down from root. If both targets are smaller than the current node, the LCA must be in the left subtree. If both are larger, go right. Otherwise the targets split at this node (or one equals it) — this is the LCA. O(h) time, O(1) extra space. Iterative beats recursive because there's no recursion stack.

approaches.name: Recursive
approaches.explanation: Same idea recursively. Cleaner to read but uses O(h) stack space. Equivalent correctness.

title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal of its nodes' values — i.e., a list of lists, one per level from top to bottom.

examples.explanation: Three levels: root, then 9 and 20, then 15 and 7.

starter_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

def build_tree(values):
    if not values:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(values[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(values):
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

def levelOrder(values):
    # Pretend the signature is levelOrder(root: TreeNode) -> List[List[int]].
    root = build_tree(values)
    
    # Your code: BFS by level. For each level, collect values; descend together.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Classic BFS. Use a queue (deque). On each outer-loop iteration, snapshot the current queue size — that's how many nodes are at this level. Pop exactly that many, collect their values into a level list, enqueue their children. Repeat until queue is empty.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty tree — return empty list or list with empty inner list?
clarifying_questions.why: Empty list []. Trivial case but worth confirming.

clarifying_questions.question: Level ordering — top to bottom or bottom to top?
clarifying_questions.why: Top to bottom (spec). LC 107 asks for bottom to top with the same algorithm + reverse.

clarifying_questions.question: Left to right within each level?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Standard BFS order — pop in FIFO order naturally produces left-to-right.

clarifying_questions.question: Can I use DFS with a level counter?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — recurse, passing the level depth, append to result[level]. Slightly more code but works and impresses with the alternative.

clarifying_questions.question: Space concerns?
clarifying_questions.why: Queue can hold up to a full level — width of the widest level. Worst case n/2 for a balanced tree.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Standard BFS by levels. Three-line trick: 'for _ in range(len(queue)):' processes exactly one level. Each iteration pops a node, appends its value to the current level's list, enqueues children. Outer loop continues while queue is non-empty.

edge_cases.details: Return []. Outer loop never enters.
[[root.val]]. One level.
n levels, each with 1 node. Algorithm still works.
Few levels, many nodes per level. Queue grows to max level width.
Just skip enqueueing None children. Levels naturally contain only existing nodes.

common_mistakes: Not snapshotting queue size before the inner loop — children get added and confused with current level.
Using a regular list (pop(0)) — O(n) per pop = O(n²) total. Use deque.popleft().
Enqueueing None children — extra checks needed; cleaner to filter at enqueue time.
Forgetting the outer 'while queue' termination.
Returning a flat list instead of list-of-lists.

faang.introduction: The canonical BFS-on-a-tree problem. Essential pattern — many tree problems reduce to this.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Meta, Google. Solving it cleanly is the foundation for related problems: Right Side View, Zigzag Order, Average of Levels.
really_testing: Using deque, not list, for O(1) pops.

rubric.criteria: Codes the 7-line BFS in 4-5 min. Uses deque correctly.
Correct with minor cleanup (uses list.pop(0), refactors when noted).
Buggy queue management. Or DFS without proper level tracking.
Cannot implement BFS at all.

playbook: Restate: 'list of lists, top-to-bottom, left-to-right'.
Pitch BFS with deque.

Code: from collections import deque. if not root: return []. queue = deque([root]); result = []. while queue: level = []. for _ in range(len(queue)): node = queue.popleft(); level.append(node.val); if node.left: queue.append(node.left); if node.right: queue.append(node.right). result.append(level). return result.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 4-7 min. Follow-up: Zigzag (LC 103), Right Side View (LC 199), Average of Levels (LC 637) — all use this same pattern.
dont_do: Don't use list.pop(0) — quadratic.
Don't enqueue None children.
Don't snapshot len(queue) inside the loop where it changes.
Don't write recursive DFS unless asked — BFS is the natural answer.

approaches.name: BFS with Queue (snapshot size)
approaches.explanation: The canonical BFS-by-level pattern. The for-loop with len(queue) snapshot ensures we process exactly one level per outer iteration. Each node is enqueued and dequeued once → O(n). Queue grows up to the widest level.

approaches.name: DFS with Level Counter
approaches.explanation: Alternative DFS approach. Recurse with the current depth as an extra argument. The result list grows as new depths are encountered. Same O(n) complexity, less natural for level-order but valid. Worth mentioning as an option.

title: Binary Tree Right Side View
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, imagine standing on the right side of it — return the values of the nodes you can see, ordered from top to bottom.

examples.explanation: From the right: see 1 (root), 3 (right child), 4 (5's right is None, but 3 has right=4).

starter_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

def build_tree(values):
    if not values:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(values[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(values):
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

def rightSideView(values):
    # Pretend the signature is rightSideView(root: TreeNode) -> List[int].
    root = build_tree(values)
    
    # Your code: for each level, return the rightmost node's value.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: BFS by level, take the LAST node's value from each level. That's the rightmost node visible. Trivial variation on standard BFS — same queue pattern, just append the last popped value per level instead of all of them.

clarifying_questions.question: What if a level has no right-side node? (left-only tree)
clarifying_questions.why: Then the leftmost node IS the rightmost — it's the only one. The 'rightmost per level' rule still works.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty tree → empty list?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Confirms the trivial case.

clarifying_questions.question: Is left-side view the symmetric problem?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes (LC 199 has a companion). Same algorithm, take first instead of last per level.

clarifying_questions.question: Can I do it with DFS?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — preorder visit right first, track max depth seen; first node at each depth is the answer. Slightly trickier but valid.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: BFS by level. For each level, the last node popped is the rightmost. Append its value to the result. Done. DFS alternative: traverse right children first (right-preorder); at each node, if its depth exceeds max-depth-seen, append its value. Both O(n).

edge_cases.details: Return []. BFS loop never enters.
[root.val].
All single-node levels — the only nodes are the rightmost. Return the chain in order.
Right chain — same chain returned.
Rightmost of each level. Mixes left and right children.

common_mistakes: Returning only nodes that have NO sibling — wrong (rightmost includes nodes with left siblings).
Returning rightmost of subtree — different problem.
DFS without right-first ordering — first hit at each depth becomes leftmost, not rightmost.
Forgetting to snapshot queue size — adds children to current level.
Off-by-one when picking the last node (use index len-1).

faang.introduction: Variant of Level Order Traversal. Tests whether you can adapt the BFS-by-level pattern to a slightly different output.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Meta, Bloomberg. Often paired with Level Order as the second half of a tree round.
really_testing: Adapting BFS-by-level to produce one value per level.
Or: DFS right-first with depth tracking.

rubric.criteria: Codes BFS variant in 3-5 min, or DFS variant; explains why both work.
Solves correctly, mentions one alternative.
Buggy queue management or wrong ordering.
Cannot derive any working approach.

playbook: Restate: 'rightmost node at each level, top to bottom'.
Pitch BFS: 'standard level traversal, but only keep the last node value per level'.

Code: queue = deque([root]) if root else deque(). result = []. while queue: size = len(queue). for i in range(size): node = queue.popleft(). if i == size - 1: result.append(node.val). if node.left: queue.append(node.left). if node.right: queue.append(node.right). return result.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 3-5 min. Follow-up: 'left side view' (take first instead of last). Or 'top view' / 'bottom view' (harder; needs column tracking).
dont_do: Don't return only nodes without left siblings.
Don't forget to handle empty root.
Don't enqueue None children.
Don't sort — natural BFS order is already correct.

approaches.name: BFS, Rightmost per Level
approaches.explanation: Standard BFS by level. We process each level fully, then add the LAST node's value to the result — that's the rightmost visible from outside. Each node touched once → O(n).

approaches.name: DFS Right-First with Depth
approaches.explanation: DFS variant. Visit right children FIRST. The first time we reach a given depth, that node must be the rightmost (because of right-first ordering). Append it. Slightly trickier than BFS, but uses less queue memory.

title: Validate Binary Search Tree
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid BST. A valid BST has: (1) the left subtree contains only nodes with values STRICTLY LESS than the node's value, (2) the right subtree contains only nodes with values STRICTLY GREATER than the node's value, (3) both subtrees are themselves BSTs.

examples.explanation: Root is 5, right child is 4. But the right SUBTREE contains 3, which is less than root 5 — invalid. The local parent-child check alone misses this.

starter_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

def build_tree(values):
    if not values:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(values[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(values):
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

def isValidBST(values):
    # Pretend the signature is isValidBST(root: TreeNode) -> bool.
    root = build_tree(values)
    
    # Your code: every node must be strictly within (lower, upper) bounds.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: The trap: checking only that each node satisfies node.left.val < node.val < node.right.val is WRONG. You can have a left subtree where the immediate left child is fine but a deeper RIGHT descendant violates the constraint against an ancestor. Solution: pass down (lower, upper) bounds. Every node must be strictly within (lower, upper); update bounds when recursing.

clarifying_questions.question: Strict or non-strict inequality?
clarifying_questions.why: Strict. Duplicates are NOT allowed (left < node < right, not ≤). Easy to get wrong.

clarifying_questions.question: What's the initial range for root?
clarifying_questions.why: (-infinity, infinity). Use float('-inf') and float('inf') as bounds.

clarifying_questions.question: Could in-order traversal work?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — for a valid BST, in-order yields strictly increasing values. Walk in-order, check each value > previous. Equally valid alternative.

clarifying_questions.question: Why is the 'check immediate children only' approach wrong?
clarifying_questions.why: Because the BST property is GLOBAL — every node in left subtree must be less than the ancestor, not just the immediate parent. Critical insight to vocalize.

clarifying_questions.question: Is empty tree a valid BST?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes by convention. Constraints say n ≥ 1, so moot in practice.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Two approaches. (1) Bounds: pass (lo, hi) down. Root starts at (-inf, +inf). For node, check lo < node.val < hi. Recurse left with (lo, node.val); recurse right with (node.val, hi). (2) In-order traversal: a valid BST yields strictly increasing values in order. Walk in-order, track previous; if ever prev >= current, invalid.

edge_cases.details: Trivially valid.
Invalid (strict inequality). [2,1,2] returns False.
The classic gotcha — local checks pass but global fails. Example [5,1,4,N,N,3,6].
Use float('-inf') / float('inf') to safely bound.
Algorithm still works; recursion depth equals tree height.

common_mistakes: Checking only immediate child relationships — fails on global violations.
Using ≤/≥ instead of strict < / > — accepts duplicates incorrectly.
Forgetting to update bounds when recursing — every recursion uses the same (-inf, +inf).
Trying to use INT_MIN/MAX bounds and hitting them on nodes with those values — use ±inf.
In-order approach: forgetting to update prev or using ≤ instead of <.

faang.introduction: The classic 'local check isn't enough' tree problem. Tests whether you can think globally about a recursive structure.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta, Google, Microsoft. The 'why not just check immediate children?' question is the hook — naive candidates fall into it.
really_testing: Recognizing the global vs local distinction.
Passing bounds through recursion (or doing in-order).

rubric.criteria: Calls out the local-check trap upfront, codes bounds-based DFS in 5-7 min, mentions in-order as alternative.
Falls into local-check; when test case shows failure, refactors to bounds correctly.
Local check; defends it as correct or gets stuck refactoring.
Cannot derive any working algorithm.

playbook: Restate: 'strict BST — left subtree all <, right subtree all >'.
Pitch bounds approach: 'every node sees a (lo, hi) range; recurse with updated bounds'.

Code: def valid(node, lo, hi): if not node: return True. if not (lo < node.val < hi): return False. return valid(node.left, lo, node.val) and valid(node.right, node.val, hi). return valid(root, float('-inf'), float('inf')).
Walk [5,1,4,N,N,3,6]: root 5 OK in (-inf, inf). Right child 4 must be > 5 — fail. False. ✓
Walk [2,1,3]: all in bounds at each level. True. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 6-10 min. Follow-up: BST iterator, Recover BST (swap two misplaced nodes).
dont_do: Don't only check immediate children.
Don't use ≤ or ≥ (must be strict).
Don't use INT_MIN/MAX — use ±inf.
Don't forget to update bounds when recursing.

approaches.name: DFS with Bounds
approaches.explanation: The canonical answer. Each node must lie strictly within (lo, hi). Recurse left with hi = node.val (everything left must be smaller); recurse right with lo = node.val (everything right must be larger). Bounds propagate down the tree, catching any global violation. O(n) time, O(h) recursion stack.

approaches.name: In-Order Traversal Check
approaches.code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

def build_tree(values):
    if not values:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(values[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(values):
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

def isValidBST(values):
    root = build_tree(values)
    self_prev = [float('-inf')]  # using a mutable container to share state across recursion
    valid = [True]
    
    def inorder(node):
        if not node or not valid[0]:
            return
        inorder(node.left)
        if node.val <= self_prev[0]:
            valid[0] = False
            return
        self_prev[0] = node.val
        inorder(node.right)
    
    inorder(root)
    return valid[0]
approaches.explanation: Alternative using the fact that in-order traversal of a valid BST produces strictly increasing values. Walk in-order, track the previous value; if any current ≤ previous, invalid. Equivalent O(n) complexity. Cleaner with nonlocal in Python 3.

title: Count Good Nodes in Binary Tree
summary: Given a binary tree, a 'good' node X is one where on the path from root to X there is NO node with value greater than X. Return the number of good nodes.

examples.explanation: Root 3 (good). Subtree at 4: 4 (good — max in path was 3), 5 (good — max was 4). Left subtree's 3 (good — equal to root max). Total 4.
Root 3 (good). 3 (good — equal to max). 4 (good — beats max 3). 2 (NOT good — 3 in path). Total 3.

starter_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

def build_tree(values):
    if not values:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(values[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(values):
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

def goodNodes(values):
    # Pretend the signature is goodNodes(root: TreeNode) -> int.
    root = build_tree(values)
    
    # Your code: DFS passing 'max value seen along the path so far'.
    # If node.val >= path_max, this node is good. Update path_max and recurse.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: DFS passing 'max value seen along the root-to-here path' as a parameter. Node is good iff node.val >= path_max. Update path_max = max(path_max, node.val) and recurse into both children. Sum the good counts.

clarifying_questions.question: Is the root always considered good?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Trivially — its path is just itself. Initial path_max = -inf or root.val both work.

clarifying_questions.question: Strict greater or greater-or-equal for 'no greater in path'?
clarifying_questions.why: Strict. 'No node with value GREATER than X' means equal-valued ancestors don't disqualify. So check node.val >= path_max.

clarifying_questions.question: Negative values possible?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes, per constraints. Use float('-inf') as initial path_max to avoid issues.

clarifying_questions.question: Could I do this iteratively?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — DFS with explicit stack of (node, path_max) tuples. Recursive is more natural; iterative useful for deep trees.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Single DFS pass. For each node, the rule depends only on the maximum value on the path from root to here. So I'll pass that max as a parameter. Base case: None → 0. Otherwise: count = 1 if node.val >= path_max else 0. Update path_max. Recurse into both children, accumulate. Returns total good count.

edge_cases.details: Root is good. Return 1.
Every node is good (equal counts). Return n.
Only root is good — every child is smaller, so blocked by max=root.val.
Every node beats path_max. All good. Return n.
Each subtree has its own path_max accumulated independently.

common_mistakes: Using > instead of >= — spec says NO greater, equal is fine.
Forgetting to update path_max — uses stale value.
Tracking path_max with mutable state shared across branches — sibling subtrees should see independent maxes from their common ancestor down.
Returning a bool instead of an int (count).
Not handling root specially when initial path_max is root.val (causes off-by-one in some impls).

faang.introduction: Tree DFS with state propagation. Tests the 'pass extra info down the recursion' technique.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Bloomberg. Easier than Validate BST but uses similar 'state-down recursion' pattern.
really_testing: DFS with a parameter tracking root-to-here state.
Understanding that branches share ancestor state but evolve independently.

rubric.criteria: Codes 7-line recursion in 4-6 min. Walks through an example.
Correct after one small bug self-fixed.
Forgets to update path_max or uses > instead of >=.
Can't structure the recursion.

playbook: Restate: 'count nodes where the max value on the root-to-node path is the node itself (or tied)'.
Pitch: 'DFS with path_max as a parameter'.

Code: def dfs(node, path_max): if not node: return 0. count = 1 if node.val >= path_max else 0. new_max = max(path_max, node.val). return count + dfs(node.left, new_max) + dfs(node.right, new_max). return dfs(root, float('-inf')).
Walk [3,3,N,4,2]: dfs(3, -inf) good=1, new_max=3. dfs(3 left, 3): good=1 (3>=3), new_max=3. dfs(4, 3): good=1 (4>=3), new_max=4. dfs(2, 4): good=0 (2<4). Sums: 1+1+1+0=3. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 4-7 min. Follow-up: Path Sum III (count paths with given sum).
dont_do: Don't use > (must be >=).
Don't forget to update path_max.
Don't use shared mutable state across siblings.
Don't return bool — return int count.

approaches.name: DFS with Path-Max
approaches.explanation: Pass the running max of root-to-here path as a parameter. At each node, contribute 1 if node.val >= path_max (it's the new high water mark, or tied). Recurse with updated max into both children, sum the counts. Path_max naturally splits independently into each subtree.

title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
summary: Given the root of a BST and an integer k, return the kth smallest value (1-indexed) among all node values.
starter_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

def build_tree(values):
    if not values:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(values[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(values):
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

def kthSmallest(values, k):
    # Pretend the signature is kthSmallest(root: TreeNode, k: int) -> int.
    root = build_tree(values)
    
    # Your code: in-order traversal of a BST visits values in sorted order.
    # Stop at the kth visit and return that value.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Key insight: in-order traversal of a BST visits node values in sorted ascending order. So do an in-order walk and stop at the kth value. Iterative version uses a stack and short-circuits at k — O(h + k) time, O(h) space.

clarifying_questions.question: Is k 1-indexed or 0-indexed?
clarifying_questions.why: 1-indexed per spec (k=1 means smallest, k=n means largest). Critical detail.

clarifying_questions.question: Tree always valid BST?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Don't have to handle bad input.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the tree be modified?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec doesn't say not to, but the in-order approach doesn't need to.

clarifying_questions.question: What if we need to do many queries? (LC follow-up)
clarifying_questions.why: Augment nodes with subtree sizes; each query becomes O(h). Mention as a follow-up if the interviewer asks.

clarifying_questions.question: Recursive in-order or iterative?
clarifying_questions.why: Iterative is preferable — short-circuits at k. Recursive must traverse fully unless you use a counter outside scope.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: BST property + in-order = sorted sequence. Iterative in-order with a stack: push left descendants, pop when no more left, that's the next smallest value; then move to right subtree of popped node. Decrement k on each pop; return when k hits 0.

edge_cases.details: Leftmost node in the BST. Walk down left until null.
Rightmost node. Walk through all.
Root is the only value. Return root.val.
In-order still works; recursion depth is n.
Per spec, no duplicates. But algorithm handles them by including each in the count.

common_mistakes: Recursive without early termination — O(n) when k is small.
0-indexed vs 1-indexed confusion — off by one.
Forgetting to decrement k after the in-order visit.
Using a min-heap (overkill — that's for unsorted data; here BST gives us sorting for free).
Visiting left then current then right but stopping after current (BFS-like) — misses the recursion structure.

faang.introduction: BST property exploitation. Tests whether you know that in-order traversal of a BST is sorted.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Microsoft. Quick win if you've seen it; struggle if not.
really_testing: Knowing in-order traversal of a BST yields sorted values.

rubric.criteria: States in-order insight immediately, codes iterative version with early exit in 5-7 min.
Recursive in-order solution, correct; doesn't add early-exit until prompted.
Off-by-one on k. Or sorts all values into a list.
Cannot derive the in-order insight; tries random traversals.

playbook: Restate: 'kth smallest in BST, 1-indexed'.
Insight: 'in-order traversal of BST = sorted sequence'.
Iterative: 'stack-based in-order, count down k'.
Code: stack = []; node = root. while stack or node: while node: stack.append(node); node = node.left. node = stack.pop(); k -= 1. if k == 0: return node.val. node = node.right.
Walk [5,3,6,2,4,N,N,1] k=3: visits 1, 2, 3 → return 3. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 5-8 min. Follow-up: 'augment nodes with subtree sizes for O(h) queries'.
dont_do: Don't sort all values — O(n log n) when O(h + k) is achievable.
Don't use BFS/level-order — wrong order.
Don't forget early termination.
Don't off-by-one on 1-indexed k.

approaches.name: Iterative In-Order with Early Exit
approaches.explanation: Iterative in-order traversal. Push left descendants until you can't; pop (this is the next smallest); count down k. When k hits 0, return. Continue into right subtree if not done. O(h + k) — short-circuits without visiting larger values.

approaches.name: Recursive In-Order
approaches.explanation: Recursive in-order collects values up to k. Equivalent correctness. The early-exit check inside inorder() keeps it from traversing more than needed. Less elegant than iterative for showing the early exit, but more readable.

title: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
summary: Given two arrays preorder and inorder representing the same binary tree, reconstruct the tree. (Test runner returns the level-order serialization of the reconstructed tree.)
constraints: preorder and inorder consist of unique values.
Both arrays are valid traversals of the same tree.

examples.explanation: preorder[0]=3 is root. inorder splits [9 | 15,20,7] — left subtree has 1 node, right has 3. Recurse.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: preorder[0] is the root (preorder visits root first). Find that root's index in inorder — left of it is the left subtree's in-order, right of it is the right subtree's in-order. The sizes determine how to split preorder for the recursive calls. Use a hash map from value to inorder-index for O(1) lookup.

clarifying_questions.question: Are all values unique?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Required for the lookup-by-value to be unambiguous.

clarifying_questions.question: Are preorder and inorder valid for the same tree?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. So we don't have to detect inconsistency.

clarifying_questions.question: Can we do this in O(n) or is O(n²) okay?
clarifying_questions.why: O(n) with a hash map for inorder lookups. O(n²) is the naive approach (scanning inorder each time). Hash map is the bar.

clarifying_questions.question: What about constructing from inorder + postorder?
clarifying_questions.why: Same trick, just postorder[-1] is root, and split inorder around it. Worth knowing the pattern generalizes.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty arrays?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, length >= 1. Empty case returns None — defensive.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: preorder gives me the root (first element). inorder, split at the root's position, gives me the SIZES of left and right subtrees. So I can split preorder into [root, left_preorder, right_preorder] where left_preorder has the same size as the left part of inorder. Recurse on each half.

edge_cases.details: preorder = inorder = [x]. Build root with no children. Recursion bottoms out immediately.
preorder = [1,2,3], inorder = [3,2,1]. Each split has empty right.
preorder = inorder = [1,2,3]. Each split has empty left.
Splits evenly. Classic divide-and-conquer.
Would break the lookup. Spec guarantees uniqueness.

common_mistakes: Linear search inorder each call — O(n²). Use hash map.
Off-by-one in slicing — confusing inclusive/exclusive indices.
Not advancing the preorder pointer — gets stuck on root.
Re-slicing arrays instead of passing indices — O(n²) memory and time.
Confusing preorder/inorder/postorder relationships.

faang.introduction: Classic divide-and-conquer on trees. The technique generalizes to other traversal-pair constructions.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Google, Meta. The 'root from preorder, sizes from inorder' insight is the test.
really_testing: Knowing how preorder and inorder identify structure.
Using a hash map for O(1) value-to-index lookup.
Passing indices, not slicing, for O(n) overall.

rubric.criteria: States 'root from pre[0], split inorder' instantly. Uses hash map. Codes in 12-15 min.
Solves correctly, possibly with O(n²) slicing/scanning. Refactors to O(n) when prompted.
Confuses preorder and inorder roles. Off-by-one issues.
Cannot articulate how the two traversals relate to structure.

playbook: Restate: 'reconstruct tree from preorder + inorder'.
Insight: 'preorder[0] is root. In inorder, root's index splits left | right subtree.'
Pitch O(n): 'hash map from value to inorder index'.

Code: idx_map = {v: i for i, v in enumerate(inorder)}. pre_idx = [0]. def build(lo, hi): if lo > hi: return None. root_val = preorder[pre_idx[0]]; pre_idx[0] += 1. node = TreeNode(root_val); mid = idx_map[root_val]. node.left = build(lo, mid - 1); node.right = build(mid + 1, hi). return node. return build(0, len(inorder) - 1).
Walk preorder=[3,9,20,15,7] inorder=[9,3,15,20,7]: root 3, inorder mid=1. Left build(0,0): root 9. Right build(2,4): root 20, mid=3. Left build(2,2): root 15. Right build(4,4): root 7. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 12-18 min. Follow-up: 'from inorder + postorder' — same algorithm, postorder[-1] is root.
dont_do: Don't scan inorder linearly each call.
Don't slice the arrays — pass indices.
Don't forget to advance the preorder pointer.
Don't confuse which traversal is root-first vs root-middle.

approaches.name: Recursive Divide with Hash Map
approaches.explanation: Classic divide-and-conquer. preorder gives us roots in pre-order (root, left, right). For each subtree, take the next preorder value as root, look up its position in inorder, recurse on left and right halves of the inorder range. The hash map gives O(1) lookups; the preorder cursor advances naturally as we visit. O(n) total.

title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, return the maximum path sum of any non-empty path. A path is a sequence of nodes where each consecutive pair shares an edge; a path doesn't need to pass through the root. Each node can be used at most once.

examples.explanation: Path 15 → 20 → 7 sums to 42. Root -10 hurts; the best path stays in the right subtree.

starter_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

def build_tree(values):
    if not values:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(values[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(values):
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

def maxPathSum(values):
    # Pretend the signature is maxPathSum(root: TreeNode) -> int.
    root = build_tree(values)
    
    # Your code: at each node, the best 'through-this-node' path uses left arm + this + right arm.
    # But the return value to parent is the best single-arm extension (left OR right, not both).
    # Track the global max separately.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Two values at every node. (1) 'max path sum THROUGH this node' = left_arm + node.val + right_arm. Use this to update the global max. (2) 'max ARM extending up from this node to its parent' = node.val + max(left_arm, right_arm). Return THIS to the parent. The arm uses only one side (left or right) because the parent will use the other side or extend further.

clarifying_questions.question: Path defined as nodes with shared edges — same node only once, right?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. So a path can be a single node, a straight line, or an inverted-V through a top node. Critical for the 'inverted-V vs arm' distinction.

clarifying_questions.question: Can values be negative?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Critical — if a subtree's max arm is negative, we should treat it as 0 (just don't include it). Use max(arm, 0).

clarifying_questions.question: Can the path be empty?
clarifying_questions.why: No. Minimum 1 node. So single-negative-node answers are still that negative value (per all-negatives example).

clarifying_questions.question: Why is this Hard?
clarifying_questions.why: The dual-return pattern (use one thing locally, return another to parent) is the trick. Without seeing it, candidates flounder.

clarifying_questions.question: Does the path have to pass through root?
clarifying_questions.why: No. The optimal path could be entirely in a subtree. That's why we track the max globally, not at root.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Classic dual-purpose recursion. At each node, compute the through-path-sum (use both arms) for updating the global max, and the arm-extension (one arm only) to return to the parent. Crucially: clip negative arms to 0 — if a subtree contributes negatively, omit it.

edge_cases.details: Answer is that negative value (can't omit nodes — path must be non-empty).
Use all branches — through-path sums work out.
Optimal might not include root. Updating global max at every node catches this.
Sum of positives along the chain. Negative segments get clipped to 0.
Both arms positive → through-sum at root is the answer.

common_mistakes: Returning the through-sum to parent — parent can't use both arms simultaneously.
Not clipping negative arms to 0 — wrong answer when subtrees hurt.
Initializing max to 0 instead of -inf — wrong on all-negative trees.
Only updating max at root — misses paths deeper in the tree.
Confusing 'depth' with 'sum' — the recursion looks like depth recursion but the math is different.

faang.introduction: The textbook 'dual-return tree DP' problem. Tests whether you can split 'what to use locally' from 'what to return to parent'.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta, Google. Often the hardest tree question of the round. Even strong candidates need 15-20 min.
really_testing: Recognizing two distinct quantities per node (through-sum vs arm).
Initializing max to -infinity for all-negative cases.
Updating global max at every node, not just root.

rubric.criteria: States the dual-return idea in <2 min. Codes cleanly with negative-arm clipping. 15-18 min.
Gets there with one hint on the dual-return idea. Correct otherwise.
Wrong return value (sum instead of arm), or forgets to clip negatives.
Cannot derive the dual quantity. Tries to enumerate all paths.

playbook: Restate: 'max path sum, path is any node sequence with shared edges, non-empty'.
Insight: 'at each node, compute through-path (use both arms) and arm-extension (use one)'.
Critical: 'clip negative arms to 0'.

Code: max_sum = [float('-inf')]. def arm(node): if not node: return 0. left = max(arm(node.left), 0). right = max(arm(node.right), 0). max_sum[0] = max(max_sum[0], node.val + left + right). return node.val + max(left, right). arm(root). return max_sum[0].
Walk [-10, 9, 20, N, N, 15, 7]: at node 20, left=15, right=7, through=42 → max. arm of 20 = 20 + 15 = 35 returned to -10. At root, left=9, right=35; through=-10+9+35=34. Max remains 42. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 15-22 min. Follow-up: Path Sum III (count paths with given sum).
dont_do: Don't return through-sum to parent.
Don't forget negative-arm clipping.
Don't initialize max to 0 (fails on negatives).
Don't try to enumerate all paths.
Don't update max only at root.

approaches.name: Dual-Return DFS (arm + global max)
approaches.code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

def build_tree(values):
    if not values:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(values[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(values):
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(values) and values[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(values[i])
            queue.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

def maxPathSum(values):
    root = build_tree(values)
    max_sum = [float('-inf')]
    
    def arm(node):
        if not node:
            return 0
        left = max(arm(node.left), 0)   # clip negative
        right = max(arm(node.right), 0) # clip negative
        # Path THROUGH this node (uses both arms)
        through = node.val + left + right
        max_sum[0] = max(max_sum[0], through)
        # Return: ARM extending up (only one side)
        return node.val + max(left, right)
    
    arm(root)
    return max_sum[0]
approaches.explanation: The canonical hard-tree trick. The recursive function returns the best arm (single-side extension) to the parent, while side-effecting a global max with the best through-sum at every node. Clipping negative arms to 0 means we just don't include hurt subtrees. Each node visited once — O(n).

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
summary: Design an algorithm to serialize a binary tree to a string AND deserialize that string back to the original tree. The roundtrip must reconstruct an identical tree. (For testability, the function serializes then deserializes and returns the result tree's level-order list — should equal the input.)

examples.explanation: Empty tree serializes to a sentinel, deserializes back to empty.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Two approaches: BFS level-order (LeetCode's format) or DFS preorder with null sentinels. DFS preorder is simpler to code: walk the tree, emit each value (or '#' for None), comma-separated. Deserialize by splitting and consuming via a queue, recursively rebuilding.

clarifying_questions.question: What format should the serialized string be?
clarifying_questions.why: Up to me — any format that's unambiguous. Comma-separated preorder with '#' for null is concise and easy. LC's BFS level-order is also fine.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty tree — serialize to empty string or sentinel?
clarifying_questions.why: Either, as long as deserialize round-trips. I'll use '#' for null, so an empty tree serializes to '#'.

clarifying_questions.question: Can values be negative?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes (constraints say -1000 ≤ val). Use comma separator so '-1' isn't confused with anything.

clarifying_questions.question: Should structure or values be the focus of the roundtrip?
clarifying_questions.why: Both. The deserialized tree must be identical to the original — same structure AND same values.

clarifying_questions.question: BFS or DFS — preference?
clarifying_questions.why: DFS preorder is shorter. BFS matches LeetCode's display format. Mention both; pick one for the implementation.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: DFS preorder: serialize visits root, recurse left, recurse right, joining with commas. Use '#' (or 'null') for None children. Deserialize: split the string, use a queue (or iterator); pop one token at a time; if it's '#' return None; otherwise build the node and recursively build left and right. The preorder traversal naturally matches the recursive deserialize.

edge_cases.details: Serializes to a single sentinel '#'. Deserialize sees '#', returns None.
'5,#,#' (preorder). Deserialize builds 5 with two null children.
Each leaf emits two '#' tokens. The format makes this unambiguous.
Just store as string. Re-parse with int(). Comma separator avoids ambiguity.
