Recursion depth = h. For n=10^4 skewed, watch stack overflow — iterative version useful.

common_mistakes: Using a delimiter that appears in values (e.g., spaces with negative values).
Forgetting null sentinels — structure becomes ambiguous.
Using only inorder OR postorder traversal — cannot reconstruct without both (or null markers).
Forgetting to consume the queue/iterator after a None — desyncs the parse.
Serializing with BFS but deserializing with DFS (or vice versa) — must match.

faang.introduction: Classic tree serialization design problem. Tests whether you can choose a format that round-trips correctly, and implement both directions cleanly.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Google, Amazon, Meta. Often paired with 'design a tree storage system' open-ended questions.
really_testing: Choosing a format with null markers (preorder + '#' or BFS + 'null').
Pairing serialize and deserialize so they round-trip.
Using a queue/iterator to consume tokens during deserialize.
Handling negatives, empty trees, and deeply nested cases.

rubric.criteria: Picks preorder + '#' format in <1 min. Codes both directions in 15 min. Walks through serialize/deserialize of an example.
Picks a working format; needs help getting one direction right.
Confused by null sentinels. Inconsistent formats between sides.
Cannot articulate a format that round-trips.

playbook: Restate: 'tree → string → tree, identical'.
Pitch: 'preorder with # for null, comma-separated'.
Serialize: def s(node): if not node: return '#'. return f'{node.val},{s(node.left)},{s(node.right)}'.
Deserialize: tokens = iter(data.split(',')). def d(): t = next(tokens). if t == '#': return None. node = TreeNode(int(t)). node.left = d(); node.right = d(). return node.
Walk [1,2,3,None,None,4,5]: serialize → '1,2,#,#,3,4,#,#,5,#,#'. Deserialize consumes in order. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 15-20 min. Follow-up: Serialize a BST in less space (don't need nulls — use just preorder).
dont_do: Don't use only inorder — ambiguous.
Don't skip null markers — structure is lost.
Don't pick a delimiter that values can contain.
Don't write one half and assume the other will work — test the roundtrip mentally.

approaches.name: Preorder DFS with Null Sentinel
approaches.space: O(n) for the string + O(h) recursion
approaches.explanation: DFS preorder with '#' for null. Serialize walks the tree, recording each value and a sentinel for each null. Deserialize uses an iterator over the comma-split tokens; reading one token at a time naturally matches the preorder order. Each direction is O(n).

title: Kth Largest Element in a Stream
summary: Design a class to find the kth largest element in a stream. Implement add(val) which returns the kth largest element after adding val.

examples.input: k = 3, nums = [4,5,8,2]; calls: add(3), add(5), add(10), add(9), add(4)
examples.explanation: After each add, the 3rd largest element in the running stream.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Min-heap of size k. The kth largest is always the heap's minimum (heap[0]). On add: push the new value; if heap size > k, pop the smallest. The remaining k elements are the top k largest seen so far, and the min of those is the kth largest.

clarifying_questions.question: k is fixed at construction time?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. So we can size the heap once.

clarifying_questions.question: What if fewer than k elements have been seen?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints, when add() is called there will always be >= k total elements. Otherwise the answer would be undefined.

clarifying_questions.question: Why min-heap not max-heap?
clarifying_questions.why: We want fast access to the SMALLEST of the top k — that's the kth largest. Min-heap's root is exactly that.

clarifying_questions.question: What if duplicates push the heap above k?
clarifying_questions.why: Push-then-pop-if-bigger handles it. Heap stays at size k always.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: We need the kth largest after every add. Sorting the full stream each time is O(n log n) per add. Better: a min-heap holding the TOP k values seen so far. The smallest of those (heap[0]) is the kth largest. Add: heappush; if size > k, heappop (drops the smallest). O(log k) per add.

edge_cases.details: Heap starts empty. First few adds just fill it.
Heap of size 1; tracks the maximum.
Duplicates are allowed — heap handles them fine.
No issue — heap orders by comparison.

edge_cases.category: Adding a value smaller than current kth
edge_cases.details: Pushed onto heap, immediately popped (it's the new smallest). Heap unchanged.

common_mistakes: Using a max-heap and removing all but k — wrong abstraction.
Sorting the array each add — O(n log n) per call, TLE.
Not heapifying nums in __init__ — start with no order, slow first adds.
Mixing up 'kth largest' (positional rank) with 'k largest' (set).
Using a list and bisect — O(n) inserts.

faang.introduction: Heap design problem. Tests whether you reach for the right data structure for 'track top-k under updates'.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Bloomberg. Often a phone-screen intro to the heap pattern.
really_testing: Recognizing 'top-k under updates' = min-heap of size k.
O(log k) per add — not O(n).

rubric.criteria: Pitches min-heap immediately. Codes in 3-5 min. Mentions heapify for init.
Correct heap solution but doesn't heapify in __init__ — slow first adds.
Uses sorted list and bisect — works but O(n) per add.
Sorts the entire array on every add.

playbook: Restate: 'kth largest under streaming adds, O(log k) per operation'.
Pitch: 'min-heap of size k, root is the answer'.
Code __init__: self.k = k; self.heap = nums; heapq.heapify(self.heap); while len(self.heap) > k: heapq.heappop(self.heap).
Code add: heapq.heappush(self.heap, val); if len(self.heap) > self.k: heapq.heappop(self.heap). return self.heap[0].
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 5-8 min. Follow-up: Kth Largest in Array (LC 215), Top K Frequent.
dont_do: Don't sort each add.
Don't use max-heap.
Don't forget to trim to size k in __init__.
Don't use a sorted list.

approaches.name: Min-Heap of Size K
approaches.time: O(log k) per add; O(n log k) init
approaches.explanation: Min-heap holds the top k largest values seen so far. The root (heap[0]) is the smallest of those — i.e., the kth largest overall. Each add: push, then pop if oversized. O(log k) per call. Initialization heapifies nums then pops the smallest until len == k.

title: Last Stone Weight
summary: You have stones with positive integer weights. On each turn, take the two heaviest stones and smash them together. If equal, both destroyed. If different, the smaller is destroyed and the larger becomes (heavier - smaller). Return the weight of the last remaining stone, or 0 if none.

examples.explanation: Smash 8&7 → 1, stones=[2,4,1,1,1]. Smash 4&2 → 2, stones=[2,1,1,1]. Smash 2&1 → 1, stones=[1,1,1]. Smash 1&1 → 0, stones=[1]. Return 1.
Only one stone — return its weight.

starter_code:
import heapq

def lastStoneWeight(stones):
    # Return the weight of the last remaining stone (or 0).
    # Hint: max-heap. Python's heapq is min-heap; negate the values to simulate max-heap.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Repeatedly extract the two largest values, smash them, push back the difference (if any). That's a max-heap simulation. Python's heapq is min-heap, so negate values on push and pop.

clarifying_questions.question: What if all stones are destroyed?
clarifying_questions.why: Return 0. Spec says 'last remaining or 0 if none'. The algorithm naturally hits empty heap and returns 0.

clarifying_questions.question: What if only one stone?
clarifying_questions.why: Return its weight directly. Algorithm pops only one, no smash; returns it.

clarifying_questions.question: Why max-heap?
clarifying_questions.why: Always smashing the TWO HEAVIEST. Max-heap gives both in O(log n).

clarifying_questions.question: Why not sort each time?
clarifying_questions.why: O(n log n) per round → O(n² log n) total. Heap is O(n log n) total.

clarifying_questions.question: Why negate for Python?
clarifying_questions.why: heapq is a min-heap. Negating values converts it to behave as a max-heap. Standard trick.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Simulation with max-heap. Negate to use Python's heapq as a max-heap. Loop: while len(heap) >= 2: pop two (heaviest), compute diff = -y - (-x) = x - y if x >= y. If diff != 0, push -diff back. End when 0 or 1 stones remain. Return -heap[0] if heap else 0.

edge_cases.details: Return it. While loop never enters.
Both destroyed. Heap empty. Return 0.
One round, return the difference.
Pairs cancel. Heap empties to 0.
Pairs cancel until 1 stone remains. Return that weight.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to negate values when pushing/popping with heapq.
Pushing 0 back onto the heap when smash result is 0 — extra work, sometimes correct but messy.
Sorting each iteration — O(n² log n).
Using a list and finding max each round — O(n) per max = O(n²).
Forgetting the final negate when returning the answer.

faang.introduction: Beginner-level heap problem. Tests whether you reach for a heap and remember the negate-for-max-heap trick in Python.
faang.introduction_context: Common phone screen at Amazon, Microsoft. Quick warmup that filters out candidates who don't know the heap pattern.
really_testing: Recognizing repeated 'get max two' as a heap pattern.

rubric.criteria: Codes the max-heap simulation in 3-5 min. Handles edge cases.
Solves correctly, possibly with sorting-based O(n² log n) version first.
Forgets to negate; gets min-heap behavior and wrong answers.
Cannot derive simulation. Or scans for max each round in O(n²).

playbook: Restate: 'smash heaviest two, repeat, return last (or 0)'.
Pitch max-heap.
Code: heap = [-s for s in stones]. heapify(heap). while len(heap) >= 2: y = -heappop(heap); x = -heappop(heap); if y != x: heappush(heap, -(y - x)). return -heap[0] if heap else 0.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 3-5 min. Follow-up: 'what if there are 10^6 stones' — same algorithm, O(n log n) handles it.
dont_do: Don't sort each iteration.
Don't scan for max linearly.
Don't forget the negate trick in Python.
Don't push 0 onto the heap (waste).

approaches.name: Max-Heap Simulation
approaches.code:
import heapq

def lastStoneWeight(stones):
    # Python's heapq is min-heap; negate values to simulate max-heap
    heap = [-s for s in stones]
    heapq.heapify(heap)
    
    while len(heap) >= 2:
        y = -heapq.heappop(heap)  # heaviest
        x = -heapq.heappop(heap)  # second heaviest
        if y != x:
            heapq.heappush(heap, -(y - x))
    
    return -heap[0] if heap else 0
approaches.explanation: Max-heap simulation. Each round, pop the two largest, smash them, push the difference back if non-zero. Heap operations are O(log n); total work is O(n log n). Python's heapq is a min-heap, so we negate values on the way in and out.

title: K Closest Points to Origin
summary: Given an array of points where points[i] = [xi, yi] represents a point on the X-Y plane and an integer k, return the k closest points to the origin (0, 0). Output order doesn't matter.

examples.explanation: Distance(1,3) = sqrt(10) > Distance(-2,2) = sqrt(8). [-2,2] is closer.

starter_code:
import heapq

def kClosest(points, k):
    # Return the k closest points to origin. Order doesn't matter.
    # Hint: max-heap of size k (or min-heap of all + take top k).
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Compare points by squared distance (no need to sqrt — comparison is monotonic). Two approaches: (1) min-heap of all + take k smallest — O(n + k log n); (2) max-heap of size k, push and pop when size > k — O(n log k). Approach 2 is better when k << n.

clarifying_questions.question: Output order — does it matter?
clarifying_questions.why: No per spec. Saves work. Output in heap order is fine.

clarifying_questions.question: Are the k closest unique, or could there be ties?
clarifying_questions.why: Could be ties. Either tied point is valid — spec just wants k closest by distance.

clarifying_questions.question: Should I compute actual distance or use squared?
clarifying_questions.why: Squared distance is enough (sqrt is monotonic). Cheaper and avoids floating point.

clarifying_questions.question: k vs n — should I optimize for k << n?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — max-heap of size k is O(n log k) which beats O(n log n) when k is small.

clarifying_questions.question: Quickselect is even faster — O(n) average. Worth it?
clarifying_questions.why: Mention as the optimal solution. Harder to code under pressure; heap is the safe answer.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Distance: x² + y². No sqrt. Heap-of-k approach: max-heap. For each point: push -d² (since Python is min-heap). If size > k: pop. The remaining k entries are the closest. Return their points.

edge_cases.details: Just the single closest. Heap with 1 element.
Return all points. No work to do.
Point (0,0) is closest possible. Heap handles it.
Either tied point is valid output. Spec doesn't enforce specific tie-break.
Doesn't matter — squared distance is positive.

common_mistakes: Computing sqrt — wastes work and risks floating-point.
Using a min-heap of size k — wrong direction (drops large distances instead of dropping small).
Sorting all points — O(n log n), wasteful when k << n.
Forgetting to negate for Python's heapq when using as max-heap.
Comparing tuples (-d², point) — fine, but watch for tuple comparison on list-typed point if equal d².

faang.introduction: Classic top-k problem. Multiple acceptable solutions; the interviewer wants to see which tradeoffs you understand.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Meta, Google. The basic heap version is the expected answer; quickselect is impressive bonus.

rubric.criteria: Max-heap of size k in 5-7 min. Mentions quickselect as O(n) alternative.
Sorts all points by distance — works but suboptimal. Refactors to heap when prompted.
Buggy heap direction or computes sqrt.
Cannot derive any working solution.

playbook: Restate: 'k points closest to origin'.
Distance: 'use x²+y², no sqrt needed'.
Pitch max-heap of size k.
Code: heap = []. for x, y in points: d = -(x*x + y*y); heappush(heap, (d, x, y)); if len(heap) > k: heappop(heap). return [[x, y] for _, x, y in heap].
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 6-10 min. Follow-up: quickselect for O(n).
dont_do: Don't compute sqrt.
Don't sort all points if k << n.
Don't use min-heap of size k (wrong direction).
Don't return distances — return the original points.

approaches.name: Sort by Distance (deterministic order)
approaches.explanation: Sort all points by squared distance, take the first k. Simplest correct answer. O(n log n) — fine when k is close to n. Returns points in ascending-distance order (deterministic), which is what the test runner compares against.

approaches.name: Max-Heap of Size K (optimal when k << n)
approaches.code:
import heapq

def kClosest(points, k):
    heap = []  # max-heap: store (-distance, x, y)
    for x, y in points:
        d2 = x * x + y * y
        heapq.heappush(heap, (-d2, x, y))
        if len(heap) > k:
            heapq.heappop(heap)
    # Sort output for deterministic order (algorithm itself returns the k closest in any order)
    result = sorted([[x, y] for _, x, y in heap], key=lambda p: p[0] * p[0] + p[1] * p[1])
    return result
approaches.explanation: Max-heap of size k. Negate distance so Python's min-heap behaves as max-heap on distance. For each point: push, then pop if oversized. O(n log k) — efficient when k << n. The final sort adds O(k log k); skip it if order doesn't matter to your caller.

title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
summary: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the kth largest element in the array. Note that it is the kth largest in sorted order, not the kth distinct element.
starter_code:
import heapq

def findKthLargest(nums, k):
    # Return the kth largest (1-indexed).
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Three options: sort O(n log n); min-heap of size k O(n log k); quickselect O(n) average. Min-heap is the safe interview answer. Quickselect impresses but is harder to code under pressure.

clarifying_questions.question: kth largest in sorted order, including duplicates?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — not distinct. [3,3,3] k=2 returns 3, not 'the 2nd unique value'.

clarifying_questions.question: k always valid (1 ≤ k ≤ n)?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. No need to handle k = 0 or k > n.

clarifying_questions.question: Should I avoid the obvious 'sort and index' answer?
clarifying_questions.why: Mention it as the easy answer. The interviewer probably wants O(n log k) or O(n) — sort is the floor.

clarifying_questions.question: What's quickselect's worst case?
clarifying_questions.why: O(n²) with bad pivots. Random pivot or median-of-medians keeps it O(n) average / worst. Mention if pushed.

clarifying_questions.question: Negatives?
clarifying_questions.why: Just compare numerically — algorithm doesn't care.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Three solutions, escalating difficulty. (1) sorted(nums)[-k] — O(n log n). (2) Min-heap of size k: push each, pop when size > k; root is the kth largest. O(n log k). (3) Quickselect partition: pivot, place smaller left and larger right; recurse into the side that contains the kth largest. O(n) average.

edge_cases.details: Return max.
Return min.
Count them — [3,3] k=2 returns 3.
k must be 1; return that element.
No special handling needed — algorithm doesn't depend on order.

common_mistakes: Treating 'kth largest' as 'kth distinct largest' — wrong on duplicates.
Using a max-heap of size n (overkill).
Buggy quickselect — recursing into wrong side.
Off-by-one with k (1-indexed vs 0-indexed).
Using sorted(nums, reverse=True)[k] instead of [k-1].

faang.introduction: Top-k classic. Almost always paired with a discussion of multiple approaches and their tradeoffs.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta, Google. The full conversation includes sort → heap → quickselect; the interviewer probes how deep your understanding goes.
really_testing: Knowing multiple approaches with their complexities.
Min-heap of size k (the safe answer).

rubric.criteria: Min-heap in 4 min. Mentions quickselect with complexity. Codes either correctly.
Solves with sort or heap; mentions one other approach.
Sort-and-index only; can't discuss optimization.
Off-by-one. Or treats duplicates as one.

playbook: Restate: 'kth largest in sorted order (with duplicates)'.
Mention sorted()[-k] as O(n log n).
Pitch min-heap: 'size k. Push each; pop when oversized. Root is the kth largest.'
Code: heap = []. for num in nums: heappush(heap, num); if len(heap) > k: heappop(heap). return heap[0].
Walk [3,2,1,5,6,4] k=2: heap grows to size 2 with smallest popped: ends [5, 6]. Return 5. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 5-10 min. Bonus: quickselect for O(n) expected — write the partition function.
dont_do: Don't treat duplicates as one.
Don't off-by-one on k.
Don't ship sort-only if you know heap/quickselect.
Don't quickselect with deterministic pivot — O(n²) on sorted input.

approaches.name: Min-Heap of Size K
approaches.explanation: Maintain a min-heap of the top k values. When a new value comes in, push it; if size exceeds k, pop the smallest. After processing all n values, the heap contains the k largest, and the root is the smallest of those — i.e., the kth largest overall. O(n log k).

approaches.name: Sort (simple baseline)
approaches.explanation: Sort then index from the end. O(n log n). Acceptable baseline; mention before pitching the optimization.

approaches.name: Quickselect (O(n) average)
approaches.explanation: Quickselect with 3-way partition for robustness on duplicates. Random pivot keeps expected O(n) and avoids the O(n²) worst case on sorted input. Recurse into the half containing the target index. Significantly faster than heap when n is huge.

title: Task Scheduler
summary: Given a list of tasks (capital letters), each takes 1 unit of time. The CPU can either run a task or idle. The SAME task must be separated by at least n cooldown intervals. Return the minimum total time to finish all tasks.

examples.explanation: Pattern: A B idle A B idle A B → 8 units.
Pattern: A B A B C D → 6 units. Enough variety to fill cooldowns.
No cooldown — just run them in any order.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Two approaches. (1) Greedy formula: answer = max(len(tasks), (max_count - 1) * (n + 1) + tied_max). Most-frequent task dominates the schedule; others fill cooldowns. (2) Simulation with max-heap: pick the most-frequent available task at each step, decrement, push to cooldown queue. Both give the same answer.

clarifying_questions.question: Cooldown n — units of time, including the task itself?
clarifying_questions.why: n cooldown units BETWEEN occurrences of the same task. So A . . A means n=2. Clarify the off-by-one.

clarifying_questions.question: Can n be 0?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. No cooldown → answer is just len(tasks).

clarifying_questions.question: Are tasks always uppercase letters?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Max 26 distinct task types.

clarifying_questions.question: Order of tasks in input matters?
clarifying_questions.why: No — only counts matter (we can rearrange freely).

clarifying_questions.question: Greedy or simulation — which to ship?
clarifying_questions.why: Formula is cleaner and O(n). Simulation more intuitive but harder to reason about. Mention both.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: The most-frequent task sets the schedule's skeleton. If A appears 3 times with n=2: A _ _ A _ _ A — that's (3-1)*(2+1) + 1 = 7 slots. But the _ slots can be filled by OTHER tasks. If we have enough variety, the formula's answer (≥len(tasks)) is achievable. Otherwise the formula gives the right bound. Formula: (max_count - 1) * (n + 1) + (# of tasks tied at max_count). Answer = max(formula, len(tasks)).

edge_cases.details: Answer = len(tasks). No idle time needed.
AAAA n=2 → 4 + 3*2 = 10 slots (formula). Yep — A . . A . . A . . A.
Answer = len(tasks). No cooldowns needed.
Multiple tasks at max count → tied count in the formula.
Variety fills cooldowns → answer = len(tasks).

common_mistakes: Off-by-one on cooldown definition.
Forgetting the max(formula, len(tasks)) cap — formula UNDERESTIMATES when variety is high.
Not handling ties at max_count.
Simulating without a max-heap — O(n²) instead of O(n log 26).
Treating the cooldown as 'including' the task itself.

faang.introduction: Greedy scheduling with a clever formula. Tests whether you can derive the formula or simulate cleanly with a heap.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Microsoft. The formula version is impressive; simulation is the safer route.
really_testing: Recognizing 'most frequent task is the bottleneck'.
Or simulating with max-heap + cooldown queue.

rubric.criteria: Derives the formula or codes simulation in 8-10 min. Walks through example.
Solves with simulation; doesn't derive formula but understands when prompted.
Off-by-one on cooldown. Or forgets the max(formula, len) cap.
Cannot articulate any algorithm.

playbook: Restate: 'min time with cooldown between same tasks'.
Counts: 'Counter, find max_count and tied_max'.
Formula: '(max_count - 1) * (n + 1) + tied_max, but at least len(tasks)'.

Code: from collections import Counter. counts = Counter(tasks). max_count = max(counts.values()). tied = sum(1 for c in counts.values() if c == max_count). return max(len(tasks), (max_count - 1) * (n + 1) + tied).
Walk AAABBB n=2: max=3, tied=2 (A and B). Formula: 2*3 + 2 = 8. len=6. Max(8, 6) = 8. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min. Follow-up: 'output the actual schedule' (use the simulation version).
dont_do: Don't ignore tied max counts.
Don't forget the len(tasks) lower bound.
Don't off-by-one on (n+1) vs (n).
Don't simulate without a heap (slow).

approaches.name: Greedy Formula
approaches.code:
from collections import Counter

def leastInterval(tasks, n):
    counts = Counter(tasks)
    max_count = max(counts.values())
    # Number of tasks tied at the maximum frequency
    tied = sum(1 for c in counts.values() if c == max_count)
    # Formula: most-frequent task creates (max_count - 1) blocks of size (n + 1).
    # Final block holds all tied-max tasks (no cooldown after the last one).
    formula = (max_count - 1) * (n + 1) + tied
    return max(formula, len(tasks))
approaches.explanation: The clever O(n) answer. The most-frequent task dictates the structure: (max_count - 1) gaps of width (n + 1) plus the final tied-max-count tasks. Other tasks slot into the gaps. If variety is high enough, len(tasks) wins — take the max. Pure math, no simulation.

approaches.name: Max-Heap Simulation
approaches.code:
from collections import Counter, deque
import heapq

def leastInterval(tasks, n):
    counts = Counter(tasks)
    heap = [-c for c in counts.values()]
    heapq.heapify(heap)
    
    time = 0
    cooldown = deque()  # (count_remaining, time_available)
    
    while heap or cooldown:
        time += 1
        if heap:
            count = -heapq.heappop(heap) - 1  # ran one
            if count > 0:
                cooldown.append((count, time + n))
        if cooldown and cooldown[0][1] == time:
            count, _ = cooldown.popleft()
            heapq.heappush(heap, -count)
    
    return time
approaches.explanation: Simulation. Max-heap holds available task counts. Each tick: pop the most-frequent, decrement, push to cooldown queue with the time it becomes available again. When cooldown's front matches current time, push it back to the heap. Loop until both are empty. Slightly slower in constant factor but more intuitive for explaining.

title: Find Median from Data Stream
summary: Design a data structure that supports addNum(num) to add an integer to a stream and findMedian() to return the median of all elements so far. Both operations should be efficient under repeated calls.
constraints: At most 5 * 10^4 calls to addNum and findMedian.
There will be at least one element in the data structure before findMedian is called.

examples.input: ops: addNum(1), addNum(2), findMedian(), addNum(3), findMedian()
examples.explanation: After [1, 2] median is 1.5. After [1, 2, 3] median is 2.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Two heaps. Lower half is a MAX-heap (Python: negate values), upper half is a MIN-heap. Their sizes differ by at most 1. When adding: push to lower if num ≤ lower's max, else upper; then rebalance. Median: if sizes equal → average of both roots. Else: root of the larger heap.

clarifying_questions.question: What's the time bound per operation?
clarifying_questions.why: O(log n) for addNum, O(1) for findMedian. The two-heaps approach hits this; sorted-list-with-insert is O(n) per add.

clarifying_questions.question: What's an empty-data findMedian — does that need handling?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, at least one element before findMedian. So no. But defensive return of 0 or raising is acceptable.

clarifying_questions.question: Return float or int?
clarifying_questions.why: Float — median of two integers can be x.5. Always return float.

clarifying_questions.question: Could I use a sorted list with bisect?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — O(log n) insertion lookup + O(n) shift. Asymptotically worse but possible. Two heaps is better.

clarifying_questions.question: Why max-heap on the low side?
clarifying_questions.why: So its root is the LARGEST of the lower half — adjacent to the median. Similarly min-heap on the high side gives the SMALLEST of the upper half.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Two heaps maintained at balanced sizes. Lower = max-heap (Python: store negative). Upper = min-heap. Invariant: sizes differ by at most 1. To add: push to one (decide by value), then if sizes are unbalanced, move root from larger to smaller. Median: if equal sizes, average of both roots. Else: root of the larger heap.

edge_cases.details: Goes to lower. Median is that number (as float).
Sizes equal. Median = (lower_root + upper_root) / 2.
Larger heap (by 1) has the median at its root.
Algorithm handles them — they just land in the appropriate heap.
Works the same. Just comparing values.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to negate when using heapq as max-heap.
Returning int division instead of float — must be float.
Not rebalancing after each insert — heaps drift apart, median is wrong.
Pushing to wrong heap initially — fixable by an immediate rebalance step.
Using a single sorted list and not seeing O(n)-per-insert problem.

faang.introduction: Two-heaps design problem. The canonical 'maintain median under streaming inserts' challenge.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta, Google. The two-heaps insight separates strong-hire from average candidates.
really_testing: Recognizing 'median = element adjacent to two halves'.
Distinguishing odd vs even counts for findMedian.

rubric.criteria: Pitches two heaps in 1-2 min. Codes it in 10-15 min. Handles balancing correctly.
Solves with sorted list (O(n) insert); refactors to two heaps with prompting.
Two-heap idea but buggy balancing. Or off-by-one on size invariants.
Cannot derive any sublinear-per-add algorithm.

playbook: Restate: 'addNum + findMedian, frequent both, want efficient'.
Pitch two heaps: 'max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half, sizes balanced'.
addNum: push to lower if num <= lower.peek() else upper. Then rebalance: if size diff > 1, move root from larger to smaller.
findMedian: if sizes equal → (max-of-lower + min-of-upper) / 2. Else → root of larger heap.
Walk addNum(1), addNum(2), findMedian: lower=[1], upper=[]. After 2: 2 > 1 → upper=[2]. Sizes equal: (1+2)/2 = 1.5. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 12-18 min. Follow-up: 'streaming median in a sliding window' (combine with sorted-list/multiset).
dont_do: Don't sort each findMedian.
Don't forget to rebalance.
Don't return int — must be float.
Don't forget to negate when using heapq as max-heap.

approaches.name: Two Heaps (Max + Min)
approaches.explanation: Two heaps with size invariant: |lower| - |upper| ∈ {0, 1}. Lower is a max-heap (negate values for Python's min-heap); upper is a min-heap. addNum places into the appropriate heap based on value, then rebalances. findMedian returns the larger heap's root (odd case) or the average of both roots (even case). O(log n) per add, O(1) per median.

title: Number of Islands
summary: Given an m × n 2D grid of '1's (land) and '0's (water), return the number of islands. An island is surrounded by water and formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically. You may assume the grid is surrounded by water.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Connected components on a grid. For each cell that's '1' and unvisited, start DFS/BFS, marking all connected '1' cells visited. Increment island count once per start. Mark visited by mutating to '0' (saves space) or with a separate visited set.

clarifying_questions.question: 4-directional or 8-directional?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, 4-directional (horizontal + vertical only). Diagonal '1's are separate islands. Critical to confirm.

clarifying_questions.question: Can I mutate the input grid?
clarifying_questions.why: Usually yes — saves the visited set. Confirm with interviewer. Otherwise use a separate set.

clarifying_questions.question: Recursive or iterative?
clarifying_questions.why: DFS recursive is shortest. BFS iterative with a queue is safer for deep grids (stack overflow concerns).

clarifying_questions.question: Cells are strings or ints?
clarifying_questions.why: LC has strings '1'/'0'. Easy to mistake for ints. Use exact comparison.

clarifying_questions.question: Worst case grid size?
clarifying_questions.why: 300x300 = 90000 cells. O(m*n) handles it easily.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Iterate every cell. If it's land ('1'), increment count and flood-fill DFS to mark the connected component as visited (or sink to '0'). Each cell visited at most once across all DFS calls → O(m*n) total.

edge_cases.details: Return 0. No DFS calls.
One island. First DFS fills everything.
Either 0 or 1.
Separate islands (4-direction only). [[1,0],[0,1]] = 2.
m != n is fine. Algorithm doesn't assume square.

common_mistakes: 8-directional traversal — wrong by spec.
Not marking visited — infinite loop.
Mutating grid when caller didn't authorize — bad practice.
Off-by-one on bounds (m vs m-1).
Comparing to int 1 instead of string '1'.

faang.introduction: Classic flood-fill / connected components problem. Foundation for many grid problems.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at virtually every FAANG. Often a warm-up before a harder grid problem (rotting oranges, surrounded regions, etc.).
really_testing: DFS or BFS on a grid.

rubric.criteria: Codes DFS/BFS flood-fill in 5-8 min. Confirms 4-direction. Handles edges.
Solves correctly with one self-corrected bug.
Forgets to mark visited, gets infinite loop or wrong count.
Cannot derive any flood-fill approach.

playbook: Restate: 'count 4-connected components of '1' cells'.
Pitch: 'iterate every cell; if it's land, DFS-mark and increment count'.

Code: m, n = len(grid), len(grid[0]). def dfs(r, c): if r<0 or r>=m or c<0 or c>=n or grid[r][c] != '1': return. grid[r][c] = '0'. dfs(r+1, c); dfs(r-1, c); dfs(r, c+1); dfs(r, c-1). count = 0. for r in range(m): for c in range(n): if grid[r][c] == '1': dfs(r, c); count += 1. return count.
Walk on example 1: DFS from (0,0) sinks all connected '1's. Count = 1. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 6-10 min. Follow-ups: Number of Connected Components (graph), Surrounded Regions, Pacific Atlantic Water Flow.
dont_do: Don't traverse diagonally.
Don't forget to mark visited.
Don't check bounds at the call site instead of at function start (cluttered).
Don't use Union-Find unless asked — DFS is shorter and faster.

approaches.name: DFS Flood-Fill (mutate grid)
approaches.explanation: Classic flood-fill. Iterate every cell; when we find a land cell, increment count and DFS-mark all connected land cells (sink them to '0' so they're not counted again). Each cell visited at most once → O(m * n). Mutates input — call out in the interview.

approaches.name: BFS Flood-Fill (queue, no mutation)
approaches.explanation: BFS variant. Use a queue to flood-fill from each starting land cell. Maintain a visited set to avoid revisits and avoid mutating the grid. Same O(m * n) complexity; safer for deep grids that would blow the recursion stack in DFS.

title: Clone Graph
summary: Given a reference of a node in a connected undirected graph, return a DEEP COPY (clone) of the graph. Each node has an integer val and a list of its neighbors. (Here the input is an adjacency list and we return the cloned adjacency list.)
constraints: The number of nodes in the graph is in [0, 100].
Node.val is unique for each node.
There are no repeated edges and no self-loops.

examples.explanation: Each index represents node val = index+1. Node 1's neighbors are 2 and 4; node 2's are 1 and 3; etc. After cloning the adjacency list should match exactly.
Single isolated node with no neighbors.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: DFS or BFS with a hash map old_node → new_node. As you walk the original graph, create a new node for each original you encounter (if not already cloned), then recursively clone each neighbor and append it to the new node's neighbors list. The map prevents infinite loops on cycles.

clarifying_questions.question: Connected graph?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, yes (you're given one node). A DFS/BFS from it reaches everything.

clarifying_questions.question: Undirected (each edge appears twice in adjacency lists)?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Algorithm handles both — the visited map prevents double-cloning.

clarifying_questions.question: Self-loops or duplicate edges?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, no. Simplifies — no need to handle node.neighbors containing duplicates.

clarifying_questions.question: DFS or BFS?
clarifying_questions.why: Either works. DFS recursive is shortest. BFS iterative avoids deep recursion. Pick one and execute well.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the graph be empty?
clarifying_questions.why: If yes (no nodes), return None / empty. Defensive but easy.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Need to clone every node AND preserve the edge structure. Use a hash map from original-node to its clone. DFS: if node already in map, return its clone (handles cycles). Otherwise create clone, register in map, then for each neighbor recursively clone and append to the new clone's neighbors. Return the clone.

edge_cases.details: Return None / []. No nodes to clone.
Clone with empty neighbors list.
Visited map prevents infinite recursion.
Per spec, graph is connected. Single DFS reaches all.
Algorithm complexity O(N + E) handles both.

common_mistakes: Forgetting the visited map — infinite recursion on cycles.
Returning the original node instead of the clone.
Copying val but not neighbors (shallow copy).
Pointing clone's neighbors to ORIGINAL nodes (must point to clones).
Trying to use copy.deepcopy — works but defeats the algorithm interview.

faang.introduction: Classic graph DFS/BFS with state. Tests the 'visited map' technique that's foundational for many graph problems.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta, Google. Often paired with Number of Islands as a graph-traversal round.
really_testing: Recognizing 'clone with cycles' as a visited-map problem.
DFS/BFS with state passed across calls.
Avoiding both infinite recursion AND duplicate cloning.
Connecting new edges to NEW nodes, not original.

rubric.criteria: Pitches visited-map approach in <2 min. Codes DFS in 10 min.
Solves correctly with one bug fixed mid-flight.
Forgets visited map, hits infinite recursion. Or copies neighbors as original nodes.
Cannot articulate the cycle problem or any traversal.

playbook: Restate: 'deep copy a connected undirected graph'.
Insight: 'cycles → need visited map old → clone'.
DFS: def clone(node): if node in seen: return seen[node]. copy = Node(node.val). seen[node] = copy. for nb in node.neighbors: copy.neighbors.append(clone(nb)). return copy.

Walk on a 2-node graph A-B: clone(A) creates A', registers A→A', recurses clone(B) → creates B', recurses clone(A): already in seen, returns A'. So B'.neighbors = [A']. Back to A: A'.neighbors = [B']. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-15 min. Follow-up: 'Clone with random pointers' (LC 138).
dont_do: Don't forget the visited map.
Don't connect new edges to original nodes.
Don't use deepcopy.
Don't return the original.

approaches.name: DFS with Visited Map
approaches.explanation: Classic DFS clone. The visited map old→clone serves two purposes: prevents infinite recursion on cycles, and ensures we only create each clone once. Crucially, register the clone in the map BEFORE recursing on neighbors — otherwise the first cycle returns to an unregistered node and creates a second clone. O(N + E) total work.

title: Course Schedule
summary: There are numCourses courses labeled 0 to numCourses - 1. Given prerequisites[i] = [a, b] meaning you must take b before a, return True if you can finish all courses (no cycles in the prerequisite graph).

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Cycle detection in a directed graph. Two standard approaches: (1) DFS with 3-color (white/gray/black) — finding gray during DFS means cycle. (2) Kahn's algorithm (BFS): peel off zero-in-degree nodes; if you can't peel everything, there's a cycle. Both O(V + E).

clarifying_questions.question: Edge direction: [a, b] means 'b is prereq of a'?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. So the edge in the graph goes b → a (b must come first). Confirm orientation.

clarifying_questions.question: Self-loops or duplicate edges?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, no duplicate pairs. But a self-loop [a, a] is technically distinct from a duplicate — algorithm catches it as a cycle.

clarifying_questions.question: Can numCourses be 1 with no prereqs?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — trivially True. Empty prereqs always returns True.

clarifying_questions.question: DFS or BFS approach?
clarifying_questions.why: Both work. DFS 3-color is shorter. Kahn's BFS produces a topological order as a bonus.

clarifying_questions.question: What about course scheduling (LC 210) follow-up?
clarifying_questions.why: Returns the actual order, not just feasibility. Kahn's algorithm extends naturally.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Build adjacency list from prerequisites. Use DFS with 3 states: WHITE = unvisited, GRAY = in current path, BLACK = fully explored. If DFS hits a GRAY node, cycle exists → return False. BLACK nodes can be skipped (already fully checked, no cycle through them). After all DFS calls, return True.

edge_cases.details: No edges. Trivially True.
[a, a] → cycle of length 1. DFS catches: WHITE → GRAY → sees GRAY → False.
Loop over all nodes, run DFS from each unvisited. Catches cycles in any component.
No cycle. DFS unrolls; everything goes BLACK.
Any single cycle is enough to return False. Don't keep searching once found.

common_mistakes: Confusing edge direction in [a, b]. Read spec carefully.
Treating BLACK nodes as visited equivalent to GRAY — leads to false-positive cycles. Use 3 states, not 2.
Forgetting to short-circuit on cycle detection.
Building wrong adjacency list (forward vs reverse).
Not handling disconnected components — only DFS from node 0.

faang.introduction: Cycle detection / topological sort classic. Foundation for build systems, task scheduling, dependency resolution.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta, Google, Microsoft. The 3-color DFS or Kahn's BFS are the two canonical solutions; knowing either is fine.
really_testing: Recognizing 'can topological order be built?' = 'is the graph acyclic?'.

rubric.criteria: Pitches DFS 3-color or Kahn's in 2 min. Codes in 8-10 min. Walks through cycle example.
Solves correctly; might need to switch from 2-color to 3-color when prompted.
Wrong edge direction or 2-state false positives.
Cannot articulate cycle detection. Tries to enumerate paths.

playbook: Restate: 'finish all courses iff no cycle in prereq graph'.
Build graph: 'edge from prereq to course'.
DFS 3-color: WHITE/GRAY/BLACK arrays. For each WHITE node: dfs(node). dfs marks GRAY, recurses on neighbors; if neighbor is GRAY → cycle. Mark BLACK on return.

Code outline. Adjacency list from prereqs. state = [0]*n. def dfs(u): if state[u] == 1: return False (cycle). if state[u] == 2: return True. state[u] = 1. for v in graph[u]: if not dfs(v): return False. state[u] = 2. return True. for i in range(n): if not dfs(i): return False. return True.
Walk simple cycle [[1,0],[0,1]]: graph[0]=[1], graph[1]=[0]. DFS(0): state[0]=1; DFS(1): state[1]=1; DFS(0): state[0]==1 → cycle. Return False. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-15 min. Follow-up: Course Schedule II (return order — Kahn's gives it for free).
dont_do: Don't use 2-color (false cycles).
Don't skip the multi-component loop.
Don't enumerate all paths — exponential.
Don't get edge direction wrong.

approaches.name: DFS with 3-Color (WHITE/GRAY/BLACK)
approaches.explanation: Standard cycle detection. GRAY means 'currently exploring this node's subtree' — if we encounter another GRAY, there's a back edge → cycle. BLACK means 'fully explored, no cycles found through here' — skip. The outer loop handles disconnected components. O(V + E).

approaches.name: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS by in-degree)
approaches.code:
from collections import deque

def canFinish(numCourses, prerequisites):
    graph = [[] for _ in range(numCourses)]
    in_degree = [0] * numCourses
    for a, b in prerequisites:
        graph[b].append(a)
        in_degree[a] += 1
    
    queue = deque(i for i in range(numCourses) if in_degree[i] == 0)
    processed = 0
    
    while queue:
        u = queue.popleft()
        processed += 1
        for v in graph[u]:
            in_degree[v] -= 1
            if in_degree[v] == 0:
                queue.append(v)
    
    return processed == numCourses
approaches.explanation: Kahn's algorithm. Compute in-degrees; start with all zero-in-degree nodes. Repeatedly remove a node, decrement neighbors' in-degrees; new zeros enter the queue. If we process all numCourses nodes, no cycle. If some remain unprocessed, there's a cycle blocking them. Bonus: this produces a topological order if you record the dequeue order.

title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
summary: Given an m × n matrix heights where heights[r][c] is the elevation, return all cells where water can flow to both the Pacific (top + left edges) and Atlantic (bottom + right edges) oceans. Water flows from a cell to neighbors with equal or lower height.

examples.explanation: Each listed cell can drain to both oceans.
Single cell touches both oceans trivially.

starter_code:
def pacificAtlantic(heights):
    # Return list of [r,c] cells from which water reaches both Pacific and Atlantic oceans.
    # Hint: reverse the problem — start from ocean edges and walk UPHILL.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Forward simulation (from each cell, can we reach both oceans?) is wasteful. Reverse: from each ocean's edge, walk UPHILL (≥ height) and mark reachable cells. The intersection of Pacific-reachable and Atlantic-reachable sets is the answer.

clarifying_questions.question: Water flows to equal or LOWER, right?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, equal or lower (>=). Critical — affects the inequality.

clarifying_questions.question: Pacific is top + left edges; Atlantic is bottom + right?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Both diagonals are edges of one ocean (corner cells touch both).

clarifying_questions.question: Are corner cells always in the answer?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — they touch both oceans directly. (0,0) is Pacific; (0,0) is also... well, (0,0) is top-left → Pacific. But (m-1, 0) is bottom-left → both Pacific (left) and Atlantic (bottom).

clarifying_questions.question: Could the grid be 1xN or Nx1?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes, per constraints. Algorithm handles it — every cell on a 1x1 grid touches both oceans.

clarifying_questions.question: Why reverse the problem?
clarifying_questions.why: Going forward: m*n cells × DFS each = O((mn)²). Reverse: 2 BFS/DFS from O(m+n) starting points, total O(mn). Much faster.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Reverse simulation. Two DFS/BFS: from Pacific edges walking UPHILL (visit any neighbor with height >= current); from Atlantic edges similarly. The cells reachable in BOTH walks are the answer. Two visited sets, intersect at the end.

edge_cases.details: Touches both oceans → answer is [[0,0]].
Every cell touches at least one ocean from each edge — algorithm handles.
All cells reachable from both oceans (since we use >= for uphill). Answer is every cell.
Cells at peak can drain in any direction if they touch an ocean; reverse DFS reaches them from both.
Only reachable if uphill chain to an ocean exists.

common_mistakes: Forward simulation per cell — O((mn)²), TLE.
Using > instead of >= for uphill — misses equal-height plateaus.
Starting only from corners, not full edges — misses interior ocean access.
Not separating Pacific and Atlantic reachable sets — confusion.
Forgetting to handle the two oceans' edges separately.

faang.introduction: BFS/DFS variant with the reverse-the-problem insight. Tests both the algorithm and the meta-observation.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Google. The 'why reverse?' question separates strong from average — even good candidates initially try forward.
really_testing: Reversing to walk uphill from oceans.
Grid BFS/DFS with multiple starting points.

rubric.criteria: Mentions reverse approach within 2 min. Codes both BFS/DFS in 12-15 min.
Forward first, refactors to reverse when prompted. Correct.
Forward only, can't optimize. Or uses > instead of >=.
Cannot derive any working approach.

playbook: Restate: 'cells from which water reaches BOTH oceans'.
Insight: 'reverse — from ocean edges, walk uphill'.
Pacific starting points: row 0 + column 0.
Atlantic starting points: row m-1 + column n-1.
DFS from each starting cell, walking to neighbors with >= height; record visited.
Return [r, c] for cells in both visited sets.

dont_do: Don't forward simulate per cell.
Don't use > for uphill.
Don't forget to include all edge cells as starts.
Don't combine the two visited sets into one.

approaches.name: Reverse DFS from Ocean Edges
approaches.explanation: Reverse simulation. Two DFS walks: one from every Pacific-edge cell (top row + left column), one from every Atlantic-edge cell (bottom row + right column). At each step, walk to neighbors with height >= current (uphill in reverse). Mark visited. Cells in both sets can drain to both oceans → answer. Each cell visited O(1) times per DFS → O(m * n) total.

title: Max Area of Island
summary: Given an m × n binary matrix grid, an island is a group of 1's (representing land) connected 4-directionally. The area of an island is the number of cells with value 1. Return the maximum area of an island. If there is no island, return 0.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Same skeleton as Number of Islands, but instead of just counting islands we ALSO count the area of each. For every '1' cell that isn't visited, start DFS and count cells. Track max area. Mutate to 0 to mark visited (or use a set).

clarifying_questions.question: 4-directional only?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Diagonal '1's are separate islands.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty grid possible?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints, m, n >= 1. Defensive check is cheap.

clarifying_questions.question: Should I mutate input?
clarifying_questions.why: Usually fine. Confirm with interviewer. Otherwise use a visited set.

clarifying_questions.question: Return 0 if no islands?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Initial max_area = 0 handles this naturally.

clarifying_questions.question: Recursive or iterative?
clarifying_questions.why: Recursive is shorter. Iterative BFS safer for deep grids.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Standard flood-fill with a count. DFS returns the size of the connected component. For each unvisited '1', call DFS and update max. Mutate visited cells to 0 to avoid revisits.

edge_cases.details: Return 0. max_area stays at initial value.
DFS returns 1. max_area = 1.
DFS reaches all cells once. Returns total count.
Algorithm tracks max; ties are fine.
Separate islands due to 4-directional rule.

common_mistakes: Not mutating (or marking visited) — infinite recursion.
DFS returning bool instead of int — can't get area.
Forgetting to update max_area for each DFS call.
Off-by-one on bounds.
8-directional traversal.

faang.introduction: Variant of Number of Islands. Tests adapting a familiar pattern to slightly different output.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Microsoft. Often used as a Number of Islands follow-up — 'now return the largest area'.
really_testing: Adapting DFS to return cell count.
Tracking max across multiple DFS calls.

rubric.criteria: Codes DFS returning area in 5-7 min. Handles edges.
Correct with one self-fix.
Confused between count and area. Or returns wrong thing from DFS.
Cannot derive flood-fill structure.

playbook: Restate: 'largest 4-connected component of 1s'.
Pitch DFS returning count.

Code: def dfs(r, c): if bounds_check_fail or grid[r][c] != 1: return 0. grid[r][c] = 0. return 1 + dfs(r+1,c) + dfs(r-1,c) + dfs(r,c+1) + dfs(r,c-1). max_area = 0. for r,c: if grid[r][c] == 1: max_area = max(max_area, dfs(r, c)). return max_area.
Walk small example: each DFS returns area; track max. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 6-8 min. Follow-up: 'island perimeter' (LC 463) — count edges between land and water.
dont_do: Don't forget visited marking.
Don't return bool from DFS — need int.
Don't 8-directional.
Don't reset max_area mid-loop.

approaches.name: DFS Flood-Fill Returning Area
approaches.explanation: DFS variant of flood-fill that returns the count of cells visited. For each unvisited '1' cell, run DFS — its return value is the area of that island. Track the maximum. Mutate cells to 0 to mark visited and avoid double-counting. O(m * n) — each cell visited once.

title: Rotting Oranges
summary: You are given an m × n grid where each cell is 0 (empty), 1 (fresh orange), or 2 (rotten orange). Every minute, fresh oranges 4-directionally adjacent to a rotten one become rotten. Return the minimum minutes until no fresh oranges remain, or -1 if impossible.

examples.explanation: After 4 minutes all fresh oranges are rotten.
The fresh orange at (2,0) can never be reached by the rot. Return -1.

starter_code:
from collections import deque

def orangesRotting(grid):
    # Multi-source BFS. Start from all rotten oranges simultaneously.
    # Return minutes until no fresh remain, or -1 if some fresh are unreachable.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Multi-source BFS. Push ALL initial rotten oranges into the queue simultaneously. BFS level-by-level — each level is a 'minute'. Count levels until queue is empty. Track fresh count; if any remain at the end, return -1.

clarifying_questions.question: All rotten oranges rot adjacent fresh ones at the SAME time?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — simultaneous spread. That's why we need multi-source BFS, not separate BFS per rotten orange.

clarifying_questions.question: What if there are no fresh oranges to start?
clarifying_questions.why: Return 0 — nothing to rot, no minutes needed.

clarifying_questions.question: What if there are no rotten oranges but fresh exist?
clarifying_questions.why: Return -1 — no source to spread the rot.

clarifying_questions.question: 4-directional only?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Diagonal is not contagious.

clarifying_questions.question: Do empty cells (0s) block the rot?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — 0 means no orange there at all. Rot can't bridge through an empty cell.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Standard multi-source BFS. Initial queue: all rotten oranges. Count fresh. While queue and fresh > 0: process one level (current queue size), spreading rot to neighbors. Increment minute counter. At the end: return minutes if fresh == 0, else -1.

edge_cases.details: 0 minutes needed.
If any fresh exist, return -1.
Walled off by 0s. Fresh count > 0 at end → -1.
Trivial cases — return 0 or -1 based on contents.
Return 0 immediately.

common_mistakes: Single-source BFS per rotten orange — wrong semantics (rotting is simultaneous).
Forgetting to count fresh oranges and return -1 for unreachable.
Incrementing minute counter even when no rotting happened in that step (off-by-one at the end).
Not handling empty grid or all-empty grid.
Returning the count of levels processed instead of (levels - 1) if you count the initial state.

faang.introduction: Multi-source BFS. Tests recognizing that simultaneous spread needs all sources started together.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Google. Variants include 'walls and gates', 'shortest distance to all 0s'.

rubric.criteria: Codes multi-source BFS in 8-10 min. Handles -1 and 0 cases.
Solves correctly; minor off-by-one fixed.
Single-source BFS; can't unify when prompted.
Cannot derive any BFS.

playbook: Restate: 'min minutes to rot all fresh, or -1'.
Insight: 'multi-source BFS from all initial rotten oranges'.

Code: queue = deque(). fresh = 0. for r,c: if grid[r][c]==2: queue.append((r,c)). elif grid[r][c]==1: fresh += 1. minutes = 0. while queue and fresh > 0: minutes += 1. for _ in range(len(queue)): r,c = queue.popleft(). for nr,nc in 4 dirs: if 0<=nr<m and 0<=nc<n and grid[nr][nc]==1: grid[nr][nc] = 2; fresh -= 1; queue.append((nr,nc)). return minutes if fresh == 0 else -1.
Walk classic 3x3: initial queue [(0,0)]. Min 1: rot (0,1) and (1,0). Min 2: rot (0,2) and (1,1). Min 3: rot (2,1). Min 4: rot (2,2). Fresh = 0. Return 4. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min. Follow-up: Walls and Gates (LC 286).
dont_do: Don't do single-source BFS per rotten — wrong.
Don't increment minutes when nothing rots.
Don't skip the fresh count.
Don't forget the no-fresh case (return 0, not minutes-after-loop).

approaches.name: Multi-Source BFS
approaches.explanation: Multi-source BFS. Push every initial rotten orange into the queue at once. Process the queue one level at a time (each level = one minute of simultaneous spread). Decrement the fresh counter as oranges rot. Exit when no fresh remain OR queue empties. If fresh remain at the end, some were unreachable → -1.

title: Walls and Gates
summary: You are given an m × n grid of rooms with: -1 (wall/obstacle), 0 (gate), INF=2^31-1 (empty room). Fill each empty room with the distance to its nearest gate. If unreachable, leave it as INF.

examples.explanation: Distance from each empty cell to nearest gate, 4-directional.

starter_code:
from collections import deque

INF = 2147483647

def wallsAndGates(rooms):
    # Multi-source BFS from all gates. Mutate the grid in place, return it.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Multi-source BFS from all gates simultaneously. Each empty room's BFS distance from the nearest gate is the answer. Push all gates into the queue at level 0. BFS expands; each visited empty room is exactly 'distance from gate' away.

clarifying_questions.question: BFS from each gate would be O(gates * m * n) — can we do better?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Multi-source BFS from ALL gates simultaneously: O(m * n) total. Each cell visited once.

clarifying_questions.question: What if a room is unreachable from any gate?
clarifying_questions.why: Leave it as INF. Don't modify.

clarifying_questions.question: Can a cell be both wall and gate?
clarifying_questions.why: No — exclusive types: -1 (wall), 0 (gate), or INF (empty). Algorithm assumes this.

clarifying_questions.question: Is INF specifically 2^31 - 1?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, yes. Used as a sentinel for 'empty room'.

clarifying_questions.question: Mutate the input grid?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec — answer is written in-place. Confirm before coding.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Multi-source BFS exactly like Rotting Oranges. Initial queue has all gates. For each, visit neighbors at distance 1, 2, 3... Mark visited by writing the distance (replaces INF). Empty rooms unreachable from any gate stay as INF.

edge_cases.details: All cells stay INF. No work.
No changes.
BFS from it, filling reachable empties.
Those cells stay INF.
BFS naturally picks the closest gate — first visit wins.

common_mistakes: Single-source BFS per gate — O(g * m * n).
Forgetting to skip walls (-1) in BFS expansion.
Overwriting INF on cells that are already non-INF (already visited closer) — first visit wins.
Off-by-one on distance counter.
Not pushing all gates initially — defeats the multi-source trick.

faang.introduction: Multi-source BFS variant — direct cousin of Rotting Oranges. Often the next problem after that.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Google, Amazon. Premium LC problem (286) but reproduced everywhere.

rubric.criteria: Multi-source BFS in 5-8 min. Walks through example.
Solves correctly, possibly with one bug fixed.
Per-gate BFS; can't unify.
Cannot derive any working BFS.

playbook: Restate: 'distance to nearest gate for each empty room'.
Insight: 'multi-source BFS from all gates'.

Code: queue = deque(). for r,c: if rooms[r][c]==0: queue.append((r,c)). while queue: r,c = popleft(). for nr,nc in 4-dirs: if in bounds and rooms[nr][nc]==INF: rooms[nr][nc] = rooms[r][c] + 1; queue.append((nr,nc)).
First-visit wins because BFS visits cells in increasing distance order. Walls (-1) naturally skipped (we check == INF).
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 6-10 min. Follow-up: '01 Matrix' (LC 542) — same algorithm.
dont_do: Don't BFS from each gate separately.
Don't overwrite already-visited cells.
Don't treat walls as empty rooms.
Don't forget the in-place mutation.

approaches.name: Multi-Source BFS from All Gates
approaches.explanation: Push all gates onto the queue. BFS expands outward in lockstep — every cell's first visit is its shortest distance from some gate. Walls are -1 (not INF) so they're naturally skipped by the `== INF` check. Each cell visited at most once → O(m * n).

title: Surrounded Regions
summary: Given an m × n board containing 'X' and 'O', capture all 'O' regions that are SURROUNDED by 'X'. A region is captured by flipping all its 'O's to 'X's. 'O' cells connected to a border 'O' cannot be captured.

examples.explanation: Center O's are surrounded → flipped. Bottom-left O touches the border → preserved.

starter_code:
def solve(board):
    # Capture surrounded 'O's. Mutate in place, return the board.
    # Trick: flood-fill from BORDER 'O's to mark survivors, then flip the rest.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Reverse the problem. Instead of identifying surrounded regions, identify 'safe' O's — those connected to a border O. Flood-fill from every border O, marking them with a temporary marker (e.g., '#'). Then walk the grid: anything still 'O' is surrounded (flip to X); anything '#' is safe (restore to O).

clarifying_questions.question: What counts as 'border'?
clarifying_questions.why: Any cell on row 0, row m-1, column 0, or column n-1.

clarifying_questions.question: Mutate in place?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Final board is the modified input.

clarifying_questions.question: 4-directional only?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Standard grid connectivity.

clarifying_questions.question: Why reverse the problem?
clarifying_questions.why: Easier to identify 'safe' O's (connected to border) than 'surrounded' ones. Surrounded = NOT safe. Saves logic.

clarifying_questions.question: Single-cell or all-border O's?
clarifying_questions.why: Trivially: nothing to flip. The reverse approach handles it without special-casing.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Flood-fill marker pattern. Walk the border; for each 'O', DFS/BFS marking all connected 'O's with '#'. After pass 1, the grid has 'X', 'O' (surrounded), and '#' (safe). Pass 2: 'O' → 'X', '#' → 'O'. Done.

edge_cases.details: No work. Return unchanged.
Safe. Stays O.
All connected to border. All safe. Stays unchanged.
Not connected to border → all flipped to X.
Standard algorithm handles correctly.

common_mistakes: Forward approach (flood-fill surrounded regions, check 'does any border O touch?') — tangled.
Forgetting one of the four borders — leaks safe regions.
Using only the four corners as starting points — misses non-corner border O's.
Forgetting to restore '#' to 'O' in pass 2.
Using BFS/DFS without bounds checks.

faang.introduction: Reverse-the-problem flood-fill. Tests recognizing that 'safe' is easier to identify than 'surrounded'.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Microsoft. Often follows Number of Islands.

rubric.criteria: Pitches the reverse approach in 2 min. Codes in 8-10 min.
Tries forward, refactors when stuck. Correct in 12-15 min.
Forward approach with bugs; tangled logic.
Cannot derive any working algorithm.

playbook: Restate: 'flip surrounded O regions to X; border-connected stay'.
Reverse insight: 'mark safe O's (border-connected), then flip everything else'.
DFS from every border O, mark with '#'.
Walk grid: 'O' → 'X' (surrounded); '#' → 'O' (restored).
Walk classic 4x4: DFS from (3,1) (only border O), no neighbors are 'O'. So (3,1) becomes '#'. Pass 2: center O's → X, (3,1) restored. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min. Follow-up: Use union-find — same logic, group border O's together.
dont_do: Don't go forward — tangled.
Don't skip any of the 4 borders.
Don't forget to restore '#' → 'O'.
Don't 8-directional.

approaches.name: Border DFS + Mark + Flip
approaches.explanation: Two-pass algorithm. Pass 1: DFS from every border cell that's 'O', marking connected 'O's with '#' (safe). Pass 2: any remaining 'O' is surrounded → flip to 'X'; any '#' is safe → restore to 'O'. O(m * n) total — each cell touched a constant number of times.

title: Course Schedule II
summary: There are numCourses courses labeled 0 to numCourses - 1. Given prerequisites[i] = [a, b] meaning b must be taken before a, return a valid order in which to take all courses. If impossible (cycle), return an empty array.

examples.explanation: 0 first; then 1 and 2 (both need 0); then 3 (needs both). Multiple valid orders exist; any is acceptable.

starter_code:
from collections import deque

def findOrder(numCourses, prerequisites):
    # Return a valid topological order of courses, or [] if impossible.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Kahn's algorithm (BFS topological sort). Build graph + in-degree array. Start queue with all zero-in-degree nodes. Repeatedly pop a node, record in order; decrement in-degrees of its neighbors; push any new zeros. If we record all numCourses, return order; else there's a cycle, return [].

clarifying_questions.question: Edge direction in [a, b]?
clarifying_questions.why: b must come before a. So edge b → a in the graph.

clarifying_questions.question: Multiple valid orders — which to return?
clarifying_questions.why: Any valid one. Kahn's natural order works.

clarifying_questions.question: What if cycle?
clarifying_questions.why: Return empty array per spec.

clarifying_questions.question: DFS post-order vs Kahn's BFS — preference?
clarifying_questions.why: Both work. Kahn's BFS more natural for this output (record in dequeue order). DFS post-order reverses to get topo order.

clarifying_questions.question: Single course with no prereqs?
clarifying_questions.why: Return [0]. Kahn's handles it — in-degree 0 from start.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Topological sort with Kahn's algorithm. Build adjacency list. Compute in-degrees. Queue all zero-in-degree nodes. Dequeue → record → decrement neighbors → enqueue new zeros. If recorded count == numCourses, return; else cycle, return [].

edge_cases.details: Some courses never reach in-degree 0. Recorded count < numCourses → return [].
In-degree 0 from start. Return [0].
All in-degree 0. All in queue at start. Order is dequeue order.
Course 0 has in-degree 1 from itself. Never reaches 0. Return [].
Only 0 in initial queue. Each release unlocks the next.

common_mistakes: Wrong edge direction.
Forgetting to handle cycle case (return [] when recorded < numCourses).
Using DFS without proper post-order reversal.
Building wrong adjacency from prerequisites.
Not initializing queue with ALL zero-in-degree nodes.

faang.introduction: Topological sort classic. Output the order, not just feasibility — Course Schedule's natural follow-up.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta. Often follows Course Schedule directly in the same round.

rubric.criteria: Codes Kahn's in 8-10 min. Handles cycle case. Walks through diamond.
Solves correctly, possibly with edge-direction confusion fixed.
Wrong edge direction or missing cycle handling.
Cannot articulate topological sort.

playbook: Restate: 'topological order, or [] if cycle'.
Pitch Kahn's: 'queue of zero-in-degree, process one at a time, decrement neighbors'.

Code: graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]. indeg = [0]*n. for a,b in pre: graph[b].append(a); indeg[a]+=1. q = deque(i for i in range(n) if indeg[i]==0). order = []. while q: u = q.popleft(); order.append(u). for v in graph[u]: indeg[v]-=1. if indeg[v]==0: q.append(v). return order if len(order)==n else [].
Walk diamond: indeg = [0, 1, 1, 2]. Queue [0]. Pop 0 → 1, 2 become 0. Queue [1, 2]. Pop 1 → 3 becomes 1. Pop 2 → 3 becomes 0. Queue [3]. Pop 3. Order [0,1,2,3]. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-15 min. Direct extension of Course Schedule.
dont_do: Don't forget to return [] on cycle.
Don't enqueue only one initial zero-indegree.
Don't get edge direction wrong.
Don't use DFS without reversing the post-order.

approaches.name: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS topological sort)
approaches.code:
from collections import deque

def findOrder(numCourses, prerequisites):
    graph = [[] for _ in range(numCourses)]
    in_degree = [0] * numCourses
    for a, b in prerequisites:
        graph[b].append(a)
        in_degree[a] += 1
    
    queue = deque(i for i in range(numCourses) if in_degree[i] == 0)
    order = []
    
    while queue:
        u = queue.popleft()
        order.append(u)
        for v in graph[u]:
            in_degree[v] -= 1
            if in_degree[v] == 0:
                queue.append(v)
    
    return order if len(order) == numCourses else []
approaches.explanation: Kahn's algorithm directly produces the topological order. We dequeue nodes in increasing dependency-resolved order. If we manage to record all numCourses, the graph is a DAG. Otherwise some nodes are stuck in a cycle and never enter the queue — return []. O(V + E).

title: Redundant Connection
summary: Given a graph that started as a tree of n nodes (so n-1 edges) and had one extra edge added (now n edges), find that redundant edge. If multiple are valid, return the one that appears LAST in the input.
constraints: There are no repeated edges; the input is guaranteed to be such an edge list.

examples.explanation: Adding 2-3 creates a cycle (1-2-3-1). The redundant edge is the last one.
Adding 1-4 creates a cycle 1-2-3-4-1. The redundant edge.

starter_code:
def findRedundantConnection(edges):
    # Process edges in order; the one that creates a cycle is redundant.
    # Use Union-Find: if endpoints share a root before union, this edge is redundant.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Classic Union-Find application. Iterate edges in order. For each edge (a, b): if find(a) == find(b), they're already in the same component — this edge is the redundant one. Otherwise union(a, b) and continue.

clarifying_questions.question: Multiple cycles — which edge to return?
clarifying_questions.why: The LAST one in input order that creates a cycle. Union-Find naturally produces this since we iterate forward and return the first edge to detect a cycle (which by the problem's guarantee is the redundant one).

clarifying_questions.question: Are nodes 1-indexed?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Size the parent array as n+1.

clarifying_questions.question: Could we use DFS instead?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — incrementally add edges and DFS check for path. But Union-Find is much cleaner and faster (nearly O(α(n)) per operation).

clarifying_questions.question: Path compression and union by rank?
clarifying_questions.why: Both optional but standard. They give effectively O(α(n)) per operation. Without them, worst case is O(n) per find.

clarifying_questions.question: Is the input guaranteed to have exactly one redundant edge?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — spec says 'started as a tree with one extra edge added'. So exactly one edge creates a cycle.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Union-Find. Walk edges in order. For each (a, b): if they share a root, return this edge — it's the redundant one. Else union them. Path compression + union by rank for efficiency.

edge_cases.details: Third edge creates cycle. Return it.
The edge that closes the loop is detected via find(a) == find(b).
Per spec, exactly one redundant. So first cycle detected = answer.
Standard handling — Union-Find catches it.
Nodes are 1-indexed; size parent array as n+1.

common_mistakes: 0-indexed parent array on 1-indexed nodes — IndexError or wrong unions.
Skipping path compression — O(n²) worst case.
Returning the WRONG edge when multiple cycles exist (spec says one, but bugs could create multiple).
Using DFS without optimization — slow.
Modifying input edges array.

faang.introduction: Canonical Union-Find application. Tests whether you reach for the right data structure.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Google. The Union-Find pattern shows up in many graph problems; this is a natural intro.
really_testing: Recognizing 'detect cycle when adding edge one-by-one' = Union-Find.
Implementing union by rank or size.

rubric.criteria: Union-Find with optimizations in 8-12 min. Walks through example.
Solves with basic Union-Find; doesn't add optimizations unless prompted.
DFS-based solution; works but slow.
Cannot derive cycle detection. Or buggy Union-Find.

playbook: Restate: 'find the edge that creates a cycle, given exactly one extra'.
Pitch Union-Find.

Code: parent = list(range(n+1)). def find(x): while parent[x] != x: parent[x] = parent[parent[x]] (compression). x = parent[x]. return x. for a, b in edges: ra, rb = find(a), find(b). if ra == rb: return [a, b]. parent[ra] = rb.
Walk triangle: edge (1,2): roots 1,2 → union. edge (1,3): roots 1,3 → union. edge (2,3): roots now find(2) = root, find(3) = same root → return [2,3]. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-12 min. Follow-up: Number of Connected Components (LC 323).
dont_do: Don't use 0-indexed parent.
Don't skip path compression.
Don't DFS from scratch each iteration.
Don't pre-build full graph — process edges incrementally.

approaches.name: Union-Find (DSU) with Path Compression
approaches.explanation: Classic Union-Find application. Process edges one at a time. For each edge (a, b): find the roots of a and b. If they're equal, a and b are already connected — this edge would create a cycle, so it's the redundant one. Otherwise, union them. Path compression keeps operations near O(1) amortized.

title: Word Ladder
summary: Given two words beginWord and endWord and a dictionary wordList, return the length of the shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord. Each adjacent pair differs by exactly one letter, and every intermediate word must be in wordList. If no such sequence exists, return 0. The sequence length counts both endpoints.
constraints: All words are lowercase English letters.

examples.explanation: hit → hot → dot → dog → cog. 5 words.
endWord 'cog' not in wordList. No valid sequence.

starter_code:
from collections import deque, defaultdict

def ladderLength(beginWord, endWord, wordList):
    # BFS over word transformations. Use a 'pattern' index for fast neighbors.
    # e.g., 'hot' has patterns '*ot', 'h*t', 'ho*'. Words sharing a pattern are one-letter neighbors.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Shortest transformation = BFS over a graph where words are nodes and edges connect words differing by one letter. Naïve: build the graph by O(n²) pairwise comparison. Better: pattern index — for each word, generate L 'patterns' (one wildcard each position); words sharing a pattern are neighbors. BFS from beginWord; first time we reach endWord is the answer.

clarifying_questions.question: Sequence length counts both endpoints?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. hit→hot→cog is length 3.

clarifying_questions.question: Must intermediate words be in wordList?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. beginWord doesn't need to be.

clarifying_questions.question: Case-sensitive?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, all lowercase. No need to normalize.

clarifying_questions.question: endWord must be in wordList?
clarifying_questions.why: Per problem, if endWord not in wordList, return 0. Catches a fast early exit.

clarifying_questions.question: Pattern index — what's the gain?
clarifying_questions.why: O(n²) pairwise comparison vs O(n*L²) pattern build + O(n*L) BFS. For L=10 and n=5000, ~5x faster.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Convert to BFS. Build pattern index: for each word, store it under each of its L pattern keys. BFS from beginWord: queue has (word, distance). At each pop, generate the word's patterns, look up all words sharing them, enqueue unvisited ones. First time we pop endWord, return distance. If queue empties, return 0.

edge_cases.details: Return 0. Early exit before BFS.
Return 1. Single-word sequence.
Return 2.
Return 0 after BFS exhausts.
Works — pattern '*' is the single pattern.

common_mistakes: O(n²) pairwise comparison — too slow on large lists.
BFS without tracking visited — infinite loops on cycles.
Counting edges instead of nodes — off-by-one (length is node count).
Not returning 0 when endWord absent.
Building the pattern index incorrectly (wrong wildcard position).

faang.introduction: Shortest-path BFS over a word-transformation graph. Tests both BFS and the optimization of building the implicit graph efficiently.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta, Google. The pattern-index trick is the discriminator between Hire and Strong Hire.
really_testing: Recognizing word transformation as graph BFS.

rubric.criteria: Pattern-index + BFS in 15-18 min. Walks through small example.
BFS with O(n*L) per-step pairwise check (no pattern index). Correct but slower.
DFS instead of BFS (returns A path, not shortest). Or visited bugs.
Cannot articulate the graph reduction.

playbook: Restate: 'shortest transformation length, one-letter diff per step'.
Insight: 'BFS on a word graph'.
Optimization: 'pattern index — *ot groups hot, lot, dot, etc.'

Code: build defaultdict mapping pattern → [words]. BFS queue: (word, dist) starting (beginWord, 1). visited = {beginWord}. pop word: if endWord, return dist. For each i: pattern = word[:i] + '*' + word[i+1:]; for nb in patterns[pattern]: if nb not in visited: visited.add(nb); queue.append((nb, dist+1)).
Walk 'hit' → 'cog': patterns for hit: *it, h*t, hi*. Find 'hot' under 'h*t'. Continue BFS until cog reached. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 15-22 min. Follow-up: 'return one valid sequence' or 'return all shortest sequences' (Word Ladder II).
dont_do: Don't O(n²) compare every word pair.
Don't DFS — returns any path, not shortest.
Don't forget visited set.
Don't count edges; count nodes (length = nodes).

approaches.name: BFS with Pattern Index
approaches.time: O(n * L²) build + O(n * L²) BFS
approaches.explanation: BFS over a word graph, with an O(1) per-step neighbor lookup via the pattern index. Each word generates L patterns (one wildcard each position). Words sharing a pattern are one-letter neighbors. BFS guarantees shortest path. Clearing each pattern after use prevents re-traversal of the same edges — keeps it linear in n + edges.

title: Number of Connected Components in an Undirected Graph
summary: You have a graph of n nodes labeled 0 to n-1. Given an integer n and a list of undirected edges where each edges[i] = [ai, bi], return the number of connected components.
starter_code:
def countComponents(n, edges):
    # Count the number of connected components in the undirected graph.
    # Approach: Union-Find. Each union of distinct roots reduces component count by 1.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Union-Find. Start with n components. For each edge: if endpoints are in different components, union them and decrement the count. If they're already in the same component, skip (redundant edge). Final count is the answer.

clarifying_questions.question: Undirected edges?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Direction doesn't matter for connectivity.

clarifying_questions.question: 0-indexed nodes?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Size parent array as n.

clarifying_questions.question: Self-loops?
clarifying_questions.why: Probably not per spec ('ai != bi'). Defensive code handles them (no union, no count change).

clarifying_questions.question: Can I use DFS instead?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Build adjacency, then DFS from unvisited node, mark all reachable, count++. Same O(V+E). Union-Find is slightly more elegant.

clarifying_questions.question: Why decrement on union?
clarifying_questions.why: Each successful union merges two components into one — net change of -1.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Two clean options. (1) Union-Find: start with count = n. For each edge, find roots; if different, union and count--. Return count. (2) DFS: build adjacency. Iterate nodes; for each unvisited, DFS-mark all reachable, count++. Both O(V+E) with proper UF optimizations.
