edge_cases.details: n isolated components.
1 component.
1 component, regardless of edge count.
Spec says no duplicates. But UF handles them gracefully (skip).
Same component; union skipped; count unchanged.

common_mistakes: Counting initial components as 0 — start with n.
Decrementing on EVERY edge — only when union actually merges.
Skipping path compression — slow on adversarial inputs.
Wrong indexing (off-by-one).
Using DFS without visited set — infinite loops.

faang.introduction: Union-Find application. Tests the canonical connectivity pattern.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Bloomberg. Often used as a Union-Find practice problem.

rubric.criteria: Union-Find with optimizations in 5-8 min.
Solves with DFS or basic UF. Correct.
Buggy count logic (decrement on every edge).
Cannot derive connectivity logic.

playbook: Restate: 'count connected components in undirected graph'.
Pitch UF: 'start with n components; union merges; count decreases each successful union'.

Code: parent = list(range(n)). count = n. def find(x): while parent[x] != x: parent[x] = parent[parent[x]]; x = parent[x]; return x. for a, b in edges: ra, rb = find(a), find(b). if ra != rb: parent[ra] = rb; count -= 1. return count.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 5-8 min. Follow-up: Redundant Connection (when union fails, that edge creates cycle).
dont_do: Don't decrement on every edge — only on real merges.
Don't skip path compression.
Don't start count at 0.
Don't forget that disconnected nodes count as components.

approaches.name: Union-Find (DSU)
approaches.explanation: Start with n components (every node alone). For each edge, find both endpoints' roots. If they're different, this edge connects two distinct components — union them and decrement the count. If they're the same root, the edge is redundant. Final count = number of components. With path compression, effectively O(n + E).

approaches.name: DFS (alternative)
approaches.explanation: Build adjacency. For each unvisited node, DFS to mark all reachable; increment component count. Equivalent correctness, slightly more code than UF.

title: Graph Valid Tree
summary: Given n nodes labeled 0 to n-1 and a list of undirected edges, return True if the edges form a valid TREE. A valid tree has exactly n-1 edges, no cycles, and is fully connected.

examples.explanation: 4 edges, connected, no cycles. Valid tree.
5 edges on 5 nodes — guaranteed cycle. Not a tree.

starter_code:
def validTree(n, edges):
    # Valid tree iff exactly n-1 edges, connected, no cycles.
    # Shortcut: if edges count != n-1, return False. Then just check connectivity (no cycle check needed).
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: A tree on n nodes has exactly n-1 edges AND is connected AND has no cycles. Key insight: if edge count = n-1, then 'connected' and 'no cycles' are equivalent — checking either is sufficient. So just verify: len(edges) == n - 1 AND graph is connected (DFS reaches all nodes from node 0).

clarifying_questions.question: Definition of tree — connected + acyclic + n-1 edges?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. All three properties are necessary. But any two imply the third (given the right count of nodes).

clarifying_questions.question: Empty edges, n=1?
clarifying_questions.why: Single node is a valid tree (trivially). 0 edges, 1 node — n-1 = 0 edges matches.

clarifying_questions.question: Self-loops?
clarifying_questions.why: Not per spec (ai != bi). Defensive code can guard.

clarifying_questions.question: Why is the edge-count shortcut valid?
clarifying_questions.why: On n nodes: connected + n-1 edges → tree (proves acyclic). Acyclic + n-1 edges → tree (proves connected, by counting). Either property + edge count = sufficient.

clarifying_questions.question: Union-Find or DFS?
clarifying_questions.why: UF: cycle detected when union fails. Edge count check + UF that never fails = tree. DFS: build adjacency, DFS from 0, must visit all n nodes.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Fast check: if len(edges) != n - 1, False. Otherwise build adjacency, DFS from 0, count visited. If visited == n, return True. Or use UF: walk edges; if union fails (cycle), False; if it succeeds for all and we end with one component, True.

edge_cases.details: 0 edges. Trivially a tree. Return True.
Implies cycle (impossible with n-1 edges)? Actually possible: 4 nodes, edges [0-1, 2-3] = 2 edges = n-1 but disconnected. Return False.
len(edges) > n - 1 → must have cycle. Return False.
len(edges) < n - 1 → disconnected. Return False.
Return True.

common_mistakes: Not checking edge count — leads to inefficient cycle detection.
Assuming n-1 edges implies tree — must also verify connectivity (or absence of cycle).
DFS without visited tracking — infinite loops.
Treating directed edges (when input is undirected) — miss half the neighbors.
Off-by-one on n vs n-1.

faang.introduction: Connectivity + acyclic check. Tests deriving the n-1 shortcut.
faang.introduction_context: Premium LC problem but very common in interviews. Onsite at Amazon, Microsoft, Google.
really_testing: Tree properties: connected + acyclic + n-1 edges.

rubric.criteria: Edge-count shortcut + DFS/UF in 6-8 min. Explains the property.
Doesn't use shortcut; runs full connectivity + cycle check. Correct.
Forgets one of the conditions; passes some tests, fails others.
Cannot articulate tree properties.

playbook: Restate: 'tree = connected + acyclic + exactly n-1 edges'.
Insight: 'check edge count first; if n-1, then connected iff acyclic'.

Code: if len(edges) != n - 1: return False. graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]. for a,b in edges: graph[a].append(b); graph[b].append(a). visited = set(). def dfs(u): visited.add(u). for v in graph[u]: if v not in visited: dfs(v). dfs(0). return len(visited) == n.
Walk valid tree: n=5, 4 edges, DFS from 0 reaches all 5. True. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 5-8 min. Premium-LC follow-up: Find Smallest Set of Vertices to Reach All Nodes.
dont_do: Don't skip the edge-count check (wastes time).
Don't check only connectivity OR only cycle without edge count.
Don't treat as directed.
Don't forget the n=1 case.

approaches.name: Edge-Count + DFS for Connectivity
approaches.explanation: First check the edge count: a tree on n nodes must have exactly n-1 edges. If yes, just verify connectivity — DFS from node 0 should reach all n nodes. Since we've ensured the right edge count, connectivity alone is sufficient (no separate cycle check needed). O(n + E).

approaches.name: Union-Find with Cycle Detection
approaches.explanation: Edge-count check first. Then Union-Find: for each edge, if endpoints share a root → cycle → False. If all unions succeed and edge count is n-1, the graph is a tree (one component, no cycles).

title: Network Delay Time
summary: Given n nodes labeled 1 to n and a list of directed weighted edges times[i] = [ui, vi, wi] meaning a signal from ui takes wi time to reach vi, send a signal from node k. Return the minimum time for ALL n nodes to receive the signal, or -1 if impossible.

examples.explanation: From 2: reaches 1 in 1, reaches 3 in 1, then 4 in 2. Max = 2.
From node 2, can't reach node 1 (directed edges only).

starter_code:
import heapq
from collections import defaultdict

def networkDelayTime(times, n, k):
    # Dijkstra's algorithm. Min-heap of (distance, node). Track shortest distance to each.
    # Return max distance, or -1 if any node unreachable.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Single-source shortest path in a directed weighted graph → Dijkstra. Min-heap of (distance, node). Pop the node with smallest tentative distance, relax its neighbors. Answer is the max of the final shortest distances (or -1 if any node was never reached).

clarifying_questions.question: Are weights non-negative?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec (0 ≤ wi ≤ 100). So Dijkstra applies. Negative weights would need Bellman-Ford.

clarifying_questions.question: Directed edges?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Build adjacency only from u to v.

clarifying_questions.question: Multiple edges between same pair?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, unique (u, v) pairs. So no need to dedupe.

clarifying_questions.question: Answer is the MAX over all destination delays?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — signal reaches all when even the last node has gotten it. So max of shortest distances.

clarifying_questions.question: Unreachable node?
clarifying_questions.why: Return -1. Any node with infinite distance after Dijkstra means -1.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Classic Dijkstra. Build adjacency. Min-heap starts with (0, k). dist = {}. Pop (d, u). If u already settled, skip. Settle u with distance d. Push neighbors with (d + w, v). At the end: if len(dist) < n, return -1. Else max(dist.values()).

edge_cases.details: Impossible — distance to k is 0.
If any node never reached, return -1.
n=1, k=1 — answer is 0.
Algorithm handles. Just means equal-distance neighbors.
Dijkstra would fail. Need Bellman-Ford.

common_mistakes: Treating edges as undirected.
Not skipping already-settled nodes (revisits give wrong answer).
Using BFS without weights — wrong distances.
Forgetting the unreachable case (return -1).
Initializing distances incorrectly.

faang.introduction: Dijkstra's algorithm classic. Tests whether you can implement shortest-path with a priority queue.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Google, Meta. Often the entry point to shortest-path discussions.
really_testing: Reading 'time for all to receive' = max shortest distance.

rubric.criteria: Dijkstra in 10-12 min. Clean adjacency, heap, settle logic.
Solves correctly, possibly Bellman-Ford if unfamiliar with Dijkstra.
BFS without weights, or buggy heap state management.
Cannot derive any shortest-path algorithm.

playbook: Restate: 'shortest delay to each node from k; return max'.
Pitch Dijkstra.

Code: graph = defaultdict(list). for u,v,w in times: graph[u].append((v, w)). heap = [(0, k)]. dist = {}. while heap: d, u = heappop(heap). if u in dist: continue. dist[u] = d. for v, w in graph[u]: if v not in dist: heappush(heap, (d + w, v)). return max(dist.values()) if len(dist) == n else -1.
Walk chain: from 2: heap=[(0,2)]. Settle 2 dist=0. Push (1,1), (1,3). Settle 1 dist=1. Settle 3 dist=1. Push (2,4). Settle 4 dist=2. Max = 2. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-15 min. Follow-up: Cheapest Flights Within K Stops (Dijkstra variant with hop limit).
dont_do: Don't BFS — ignores weights.
Don't revisit settled nodes.
Don't forget the unreachable case.
Don't treat edges as undirected.

approaches.name: Dijkstra with Min-Heap
approaches.explanation: Dijkstra's algorithm. Min-heap holds (tentative_distance, node) tuples. Each pop yields the node with smallest pending distance — settle it once, then relax its neighbors. The 'already settled' skip ensures O((V + E) log V). At the end, max of distances is the time for all nodes to receive the signal; if any node missing from dist, return -1.

title: Climbing Stairs
summary: You are climbing a staircase with n steps. Each time you can climb 1 or 2 steps. How many distinct ways can you reach the top?

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Fibonacci in disguise. ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2) (last step was 1 or 2). Base: ways(1) = 1, ways(2) = 2. Bottom-up with two rolling variables → O(n) time, O(1) space.

clarifying_questions.question: Can I climb 3 steps at a time?
clarifying_questions.why: No per spec — only 1 or 2. Generalizes easily (just add more terms in the recurrence).

clarifying_questions.question: n bounded?
clarifying_questions.why: n ≤ 45. Big enough to need DP, small enough that O(n) is fine.

clarifying_questions.question: Recursive memoization or iterative DP?
clarifying_questions.why: Either works. Iterative bottom-up is shorter; recursive with memo is more general. Bottom-up wins for clarity.

clarifying_questions.question: Can I do O(log n) with matrix exponentiation?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — Fibonacci's closed form. Mention as the optimal asymptotic; rarely required.

clarifying_questions.question: Closed form (Binet's)?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes for Fibonacci but uses sqrt(5); float precision issues. Avoid in production.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Standard 1D DP. State: dp[i] = ways to reach step i. Transition: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] (last move was 1 or 2). Base: dp[1] = 1, dp[2] = 2. Roll with two variables to save space.

edge_cases.details: 1 way.
2 ways.
~1.8 billion ways. Int is fine in Python.
Defensive: 1 (empty path). Spec says n >= 1.
Not in spec. Don't need to handle.

common_mistakes: Allocating O(n) array when O(1) suffices.
Off-by-one on initialization (dp[0] vs dp[1]).
Pure recursion without memoization — exponential.
Returning dp[n-1] instead of dp[n] (off-by-one).
Trying to enumerate all paths — n! style explosion.

faang.introduction: The simplest 1D DP problem. The classic 'recognize Fibonacci structure' interview filter.
faang.introduction_context: Phone screen at virtually every FAANG. The bar: solve in 1-2 minutes. Slower = caution.

rubric.criteria: Codes in 1-2 min. O(1) space. Mentions Fibonacci.
Memoization first, then iterative. Correct in 5 min.
Off-by-one or doesn't reach iterative.
Tries pure recursion; says it's optimal.

playbook: Restate: 'count ways to reach step n, taking 1 or 2 steps'.
Insight: 'dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]'.
Bottom-up with two vars: if n <= 2: return n. prev2, prev1 = 1, 2. for i in range(3, n+1): cur = prev1 + prev2; prev2, prev1 = prev1, cur. return prev1.
Walk n=5: 1,2,3,5,8. Return 8. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 2-4 min. Generalization: 'climb 1, 2, or 3 steps' — sum of three previous.
dont_do: Don't use O(n) array.
Don't recurse without memo.
Don't off-by-one initial values.

approaches.name: Iterative DP, O(1) Space
approaches.explanation: Bottom-up Fibonacci with two rolling variables. dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. Initialize prev2=ways(1)=1, prev1=ways(2)=2. Iterate from 3 to n, computing the new value as the sum of the previous two. O(n) time, O(1) extra space.

approaches.name: Recursive with Memoization
approaches.explanation: Top-down with memoization. Equivalent correctness. More natural to read for some; uses O(n) stack and dict space. Useful pattern to teach DP, but iterative is preferred.

title: House Robber
summary: You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has some amount of money. The only constraint is adjacent houses have connected security systems — you cannot rob two adjacent houses. Given an integer array nums representing the amount of money in each house, return the maximum amount you can rob without alerting police.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: At each house i, choose: rob it (dp[i-2] + nums[i]) or skip it (dp[i-1]). dp[i] is the max of those two. Bottom-up with two rolling variables.

clarifying_questions.question: Strictly adjacent houses forbidden together?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — robbing house i means not robbing i-1 or i+1. Spec only says 'adjacent'.

clarifying_questions.question: Can I skip many houses in a row?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — no requirement to rob. Sometimes skipping is best.

clarifying_questions.question: Can amounts be zero or negative?
clarifying_questions.why: Zero per spec (≥ 0). Algorithm still handles negatives — skip them if worse than skipping.

clarifying_questions.question: Single house?
clarifying_questions.why: Rob it. Return nums[0].

clarifying_questions.question: Can the houses be in a circle? (LC 213)
clarifying_questions.why: Different problem. Solve linear first, then handle circular by splitting.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Classic 1D DP. Two choices at each house: rob (and skip neighbor) or skip. dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]). Base: dp[0] = nums[0], dp[1] = max(nums[0], nums[1]). Roll with prev2, prev1.

edge_cases.details: Rob it. Return nums[0].
Return max(nums[0], nums[1]).
Return 0.
Skipping low ones to reach high ones may be optimal.
Take every other → ⌈n/2⌉ * v.

common_mistakes: Greedy: 'always rob the bigger of i and i+1' — fails on [2,7,9,3,1].
Allocating O(n) array — O(1) with two vars.
Off-by-one initialization for dp[1].
Trying to rob every other house starting from 0 — fails when nums[1] is huge.
Forgetting the option to skip both at start.

faang.introduction: Classic 1D DP. The 'two choices per position' template.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta, Microsoft. Quick after Climbing Stairs.
really_testing: Recognizing 'take or skip' as a DP state.

rubric.criteria: DP in 4-5 min. O(1) space. Walks through [2,7,9,3,1].
Memoization solution; converts to iterative when asked.
Greedy approach; can't fix when it fails.
Cannot derive any working DP.

playbook: Restate: 'max sum non-adjacent subset'.
Pitch: 'at each house, rob (dp[i-2] + nums[i]) or skip (dp[i-1])'.
Code: if not nums: return 0. if len(nums) == 1: return nums[0]. prev2, prev1 = 0, nums[0]. for i in range(1, len(nums)): cur = max(prev1, prev2 + nums[i]); prev2, prev1 = prev1, cur. return prev1.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 5-8 min. Follow-up: House Robber II (circular), House Robber III (tree).
dont_do: Don't greedy — fails on adversarial.
Don't allocate O(n) array.
Don't off-by-one on base.
Don't always rob every other house.

approaches.name: Iterative DP, O(1) Space
approaches.explanation: Bottom-up DP with two rolling variables. At each house i, either rob (prev2 + nums[i]) or skip (prev1). The maximum becomes the new prev1; the old prev1 shifts to prev2. O(n) time, O(1) space.

title: Coin Change
summary: Given an integer array coins representing coins of different denominations and an integer amount, return the fewest number of coins needed to make up that amount. If impossible, return -1. You can use each coin an unlimited number of times.
starter_code:
def coinChange(coins, amount):
    # dp[i] = min coins to make amount i. Initialize dp[0] = 0, rest = infinity.
    # Transition: dp[i] = 1 + min(dp[i - coin]) over all coins where i - coin >= 0 and dp[i-coin] reachable.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Unbounded knapsack DP. dp[i] = min coins to make amount i. Initialize dp[0] = 0, dp[i] = infinity for i > 0. For each i from 1 to amount: dp[i] = 1 + min(dp[i - c]) over all coins c where i - c >= 0 and dp[i - c] != infinity. Return dp[amount] if reachable, else -1.

clarifying_questions.question: Coins are reusable?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — unlimited supply per spec. That makes it unbounded knapsack.

clarifying_questions.question: What if amount = 0?
clarifying_questions.why: Return 0 — zero coins needed. dp[0] = 0 handles.

clarifying_questions.question: Can coins have duplicate denominations?
clarifying_questions.why: Doesn't matter — duplicates don't change the DP.

clarifying_questions.question: Greedy doesn't work?
clarifying_questions.why: Correct — greedy fails on denominations like [1, 3, 4] for amount 6: greedy says 4+1+1, but 3+3 is optimal.

clarifying_questions.question: BFS as an alternative?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — BFS from 0, each layer adds a coin. First time we reach amount is the answer. Same complexity, different framing.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Bottom-up DP. For each amount i from 1 to target, try every coin. The fewest coins to make i is 1 + fewest to make (i - coin). Use a sentinel (amount + 1) for 'infinity' to keep arithmetic simple. Return dp[amount] if it's still ≤ amount, else -1.

edge_cases.details: Return 0.
Return -1. Common if all coins > amount.
Return 1.
Reachable iff amount % coin == 0.
Real coin denominations (US) are greedy-safe; arbitrary denominations are not.

common_mistakes: Greedy — fails on [1,3,4] amount 6.
Not handling unreachable (return -1).
Initializing dp[0] != 0.
Using actual float('inf') and doing arithmetic — sometimes overflows or compares poorly. Use amount + 1 as sentinel.
Trying recursion without memo — exponential.

faang.introduction: Unbounded knapsack — one of the most common DP problems. Tests whether you can resist greedy.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Google, Meta. The 'why doesn't greedy work?' question is a frequent follow-up.

rubric.criteria: DP in 8-10 min. Walks through example. Argues greedy fails.
Memoization version; convert to bottom-up when prompted.
Greedy first; can't fix even with counterexample.
Cannot derive any sub-exponential algorithm.

playbook: Restate: 'min coins to make amount; -1 if impossible'.
Insight: 'DP — dp[i] = min over coins c of (1 + dp[i - c])'.
Code: dp = [amount + 1] * (amount + 1); dp[0] = 0. for i in range(1, amount + 1): for c in coins: if c <= i: dp[i] = min(dp[i], 1 + dp[i - c]). return dp[amount] if dp[amount] <= amount else -1.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min. Follow-up: Coin Change II (count ways, not min coins).
dont_do: Don't greedy.
Don't recurse without memo.
Don't forget the -1 case.
Don't use float('inf') for sentinel — use amount + 1.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up DP
approaches.explanation: dp[i] = min coins to make amount i. Initialize dp[0]=0, rest to sentinel (amount + 1, larger than any valid answer). For each amount i, try every coin: if c fits, candidate is 1 + dp[i - c]. After filling, check if dp[amount] is still sentinel (unreachable) and return -1, else return dp[amount].

approaches.name: BFS (alternative framing)
approaches.explanation: BFS variant. Each level adds one more coin. First time we reach `amount` is the minimum coin count. Same complexity as DP but different mental model — useful when the interviewer asks 'can you frame this as graph search?'.

title: Min Cost Climbing Stairs
summary: You are given an integer array cost where cost[i] is the cost of stepping on step i. Once you pay the cost, you can climb one or two steps. You can start from index 0 or index 1. Return the minimum cost to reach the TOP (beyond the last step).

examples.explanation: Start at index 1 (pay 15), jump 2 to past the top. Total 15.

starter_code:
def minCostClimbingStairs(cost):
    # dp[i] = min cost to REACH step i (paying as you step ON it).
    # Top is index n (past the last). dp[n] = min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]).
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Variant of Climbing Stairs: instead of counting ways, minimize cost. dp[i] = min cost to land ON step i = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). Top is index n; dp[n] = min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]) (no cost beyond top).

clarifying_questions.question: Cost is paid when stepping ON, then you can jump 1 or 2?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. So dp[i] includes the cost AT i.

clarifying_questions.question: Top is at index n (one past last)?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — 'reach the top' means past the array. No cost beyond.

clarifying_questions.question: Can start at index 0 or 1?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — base cases dp[0] = cost[0], dp[1] = cost[1].

clarifying_questions.question: Can costs be zero?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Algorithm handles.

clarifying_questions.question: Recurrence vs iterative?
clarifying_questions.why: Iterative bottom-up — same as Climbing Stairs but with cost added.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). Final answer: min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]). Roll with two variables.

edge_cases.details: Min(cost[0], cost[1]).
Pay every other or take 2-jumps.
Total 0.
Algorithm correctly avoids.
Initial dp values handle it.

common_mistakes: Off-by-one — confusion about 'top' position.
Allocating O(n) — O(1) with two vars works.
Forgetting the final min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]) — you can jump from either of the last two.

faang.introduction: Climbing Stairs with cost. Tests the iterative-DP-O(1)-space pattern.
faang.introduction_context: Common phone screen. Warm-up before harder DP.
really_testing: Adapting Climbing Stairs recurrence to a min-cost variant.

rubric.criteria: Codes in 3-5 min, O(1) space.
Solves with array; refactors to O(1).
Off-by-one on top handling.
Can't derive recurrence.

playbook: Restate: 'min cost to reach top, pay when stepping'.
Recurrence: 'dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2])'.
Code: a, b = cost[0], cost[1]. for i in range(2, len(cost)): a, b = b, cost[i] + min(a, b). return min(a, b).
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 3-5 min.
dont_do: Don't off-by-one the 'top'.
Don't allocate full O(n).

approaches.name: Iterative DP, O(1) Space
approaches.explanation: Rolling two variables. a = min cost to reach step i-2, b = min cost to reach step i-1. For each new step, compute its min cost and shift. Final answer is min of the last two (you can jump from either to the top).

title: House Robber II
summary: Same as House Robber, but houses are arranged in a CIRCLE — house 0 and house n-1 are also adjacent. Return the maximum money you can rob.

examples.explanation: Can't rob both 2 (index 0) and 2 (index 2) — they're adjacent in the circle. So just rob 3.

starter_code:
def rob(nums):
    # Circular adjacency means either skip first OR skip last.
    # Run linear House Robber on nums[1:] and nums[:-1]. Return the larger.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Key insight: in a circle, the first and last house can't both be robbed. So consider two cases: (1) skip first → linear House Robber on nums[1:], (2) skip last → linear House Robber on nums[:-1]. Answer is the max of the two.

clarifying_questions.question: Adjacency in a circle?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — house n-1 is adjacent to house 0 AND to house n-2.

clarifying_questions.question: Single house — does the circle rule apply?
clarifying_questions.why: Trivially: rob it. The 'two slices' approach handles it (nums[1:] is empty, nums[:-1] is empty; max of 0, nums[0] is nums[0]).

clarifying_questions.question: Can I solve in one pass with state?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — track whether you robbed house 0 as state. But the two-slice approach is cleaner.

clarifying_questions.question: Why two slices and not three or more?
clarifying_questions.why: Only the first-last connection is the new constraint. Skipping one of them removes it. Linear works on the remaining.

clarifying_questions.question: Edge case n=1?
clarifying_questions.why: Both slices empty; result should be nums[0]. Handle as a separate case.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Reduce to House Robber I twice. Once excluding house 0, once excluding house n-1. The max of the two is the answer. The recurrence inside each linear case is dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]).

edge_cases.details: Return nums[0]. Don't slice.
Return max(nums[0], nums[1]).
Return 0.
Algorithm handles correctly.

edge_cases.category: First and last are tiny vs middle peaks
edge_cases.details: Algorithm picks the bigger slice.

common_mistakes: Trying full linear House Robber without handling the circular wrap — counts first and last both.
Recursing without memoization.
Not handling n=1 separately.
Slicing wrong (nums[1:] vs nums[:-1] confusion).

faang.introduction: House Robber with circular constraint. Tests adapting a known DP.
faang.introduction_context: Direct follow-up at Amazon, Microsoft.

rubric.criteria: Two-slice idea in 3-4 min, codes in 7.
Solves correctly with one base-case fix.
Tries to extend the linear DP with extra state; gets confused.
Can't derive.

playbook: Restate: 'circular adjacency: first and last can't both be robbed'.
Reduction: 'two cases — skip first OR skip last, take max'.
Code: if len(nums) == 1: return nums[0]. def lin(arr): prev2, prev1 = 0, 0; for x in arr: prev2, prev1 = prev1, max(prev1, prev2 + x); return prev1. return max(lin(nums[1:]), lin(nums[:-1])).
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 5-8 min.
dont_do: Don't ignore the circular constraint.
Don't try complex single-pass DP with state — two slices is cleaner.
Don't forget single-house edge case.

approaches.name: Two-Slice Reduction
approaches.explanation: Reduce circular case to two linear cases. Run linear House Robber excluding either the first or the last house — that breaks the wrap-around constraint. Maximum of the two is the answer. O(n) time, O(1) space within each linear call.

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
summary: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest STRICTLY INCREASING subsequence. A subsequence preserves relative order but doesn't have to be contiguous.
starter_code:
def lengthOfLIS(nums):
    # O(n^2) DP: dp[i] = LIS ending at i.
    # O(n log n): patience sorting with binary search (bisect).
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Two approaches. (1) O(n²) DP: dp[i] = LIS length ending at i. For each i, scan j < i; if nums[j] < nums[i], dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). Answer: max(dp). (2) O(n log n) patience sort: maintain a 'tails' array where tails[k] = smallest possible tail of an increasing subsequence of length k+1. For each num, binary-search to replace (or append). tails length = LIS length.

clarifying_questions.question: Strictly increasing, or non-decreasing?
clarifying_questions.why: Strictly per spec. Duplicates don't extend. Use < (not ≤).

clarifying_questions.question: Subsequence (preserve order, not contiguous)?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Different from substring.

clarifying_questions.question: Return length or the sequence?
clarifying_questions.why: Just length per spec. Saves work.

clarifying_questions.question: O(n²) acceptable or do you want O(n log n)?
clarifying_questions.why: n ≤ 2500 → O(n²) = ~6 million, fine. But the patience-sort version is impressive.

clarifying_questions.question: Negatives or large values?
clarifying_questions.why: Algorithm is purely comparison-based. Range doesn't matter.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Start with O(n²) DP: for each i, find max LIS ending at i by scanning back. Answer is max over all dp[i]. For O(n log n): keep a 'tails' array — tails[k] is the smallest possible ending value for an LIS of length k+1. For each num, find the first tails[k] >= num (bisect_left) and replace. If num is larger than all tails, append. Final length of tails is LIS length.

edge_cases.details: LIS length 1.
LIS = whole array.
LIS = 1.
LIS = 1 (strict).
Strict comparison handles them — each duplicate doesn't extend.

common_mistakes: Using <= instead of < (allows duplicates to extend).
Confusing subsequence with substring (contiguous).
O(n²) DP that resets dp[i] to 1 every time — should remain 1 by default.
Patience sort with wrong bisect side — should be bisect_left for strict increase.
Returning the sequence instead of just length.

faang.introduction: Classic LIS. Often paired with the 'can you do it in O(n log n)?' follow-up.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Google, Meta. The O(n²) is the entry bar; O(n log n) is impressive.

rubric.criteria: O(n²) DP in 5 min, then O(n log n) via patience-sort. Walks through example.
O(n²) solid. Knows O(n log n) exists, can describe but maybe not code.
Wrong inequality. Or treats as substring.
Cannot derive DP.

playbook: Restate: 'longest STRICTLY increasing SUBSEQUENCE'.
Pitch O(n²) DP.
Code: dp = [1] * len(nums). for i in range(len(nums)): for j in range(i): if nums[j] < nums[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). return max(dp).
Pitch O(n log n): 'patience sort — tails[k] = smallest possible end of length-(k+1) LIS'.
Code: from bisect import bisect_left. tails = []. for x in nums: idx = bisect_left(tails, x). if idx == len(tails): tails.append(x). else: tails[idx] = x. return len(tails).
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-15 min. Follow-up: 'return the actual sequence' (track predecessors).
dont_do: Don't use ≤.
Don't treat as substring.
Don't claim tails IS the LIS — it's not; just the same length.
Don't bisect_right (would give non-decreasing version).

approaches.name: DP O(n²)
approaches.explanation: Classic 1D DP. dp[i] = length of LIS ending at index i. For each i, scan all earlier j; if nums[j] < nums[i], we can extend dp[j]. Initialize all dp[i] = 1 (subsequence of just nums[i]). Answer is max(dp). O(n²).

approaches.name: Patience Sort with Binary Search, O(n log n)
approaches.explanation: Patience-sort algorithm. Maintain 'tails' where tails[k] is the smallest possible ending value of any increasing subsequence of length k+1. For each new value, find its position via bisect_left (since strict, we replace equals). Append if it extends; otherwise replace to lower the tail. Length of tails is the LIS length. O(n log n).

title: Word Break
summary: Given a string s and a dictionary wordDict of words, return True if s can be segmented into a space-separated sequence of dictionary words. Each dictionary word may be used multiple times.
constraints: All words consist of lowercase English letters; wordDict contains unique words.

examples.explanation: No way to segment fully — 'catsandog' can start with cats/cat but neither leaves a valid suffix.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: 1D DP. dp[i] = True if s[:i] can be segmented. Base: dp[0] = True (empty string). Transition: dp[i] = True if there exists j < i such that dp[j] and s[j:i] is in wordDict. Use a set for O(1) lookup.

clarifying_questions.question: Words can be reused?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Each word may appear multiple times in the segmentation.

clarifying_questions.question: All same length, or variable?
clarifying_questions.why: Variable per spec (1 to 20 chars).

clarifying_questions.question: Case-sensitive match?
clarifying_questions.why: All lowercase per spec. No normalization needed.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty s — is that True?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec s.length >= 1, but dp[0] = True is the natural base case.

clarifying_questions.question: Why DP not greedy?
clarifying_questions.why: Greedy fails on 'catsandog' — taking 'cats' or 'cat' first is locally valid but both fail downstream. DP explores all splits.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Bottom-up DP. dp[0] = True. For each i from 1 to len(s): for each j from 0 to i-1: if dp[j] is True and s[j:i] is in dict, set dp[i] = True and break. Optimization: only try j values where i - j ≤ max_word_length.

edge_cases.details: Repeated single chars segment correctly.
dp[len(s)] stays False.
Skipped — j would be negative.
Algorithm naturally allows reuse.

edge_cases.category: Spec disallows empty s; defensive returns True
edge_cases.details: dp[0] = True base case.

common_mistakes: Greedy first-match — fails on backtracking-required inputs.
Recursion without memoization — exponential.
Iterating j from len(s) down — wastes work.
Using a list instead of set for word lookup — O(n) per check.
Off-by-one slicing (s[j:i] vs s[j:i+1]).

faang.introduction: String + DP. The 'can it be split into dictionary pieces' archetype.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Meta, Google. Often a follow-up: 'return all valid segmentations' (Word Break II).
really_testing: Building the recurrence: dp[i] = any(dp[j] and s[j:i] in dict).

rubric.criteria: DP in 8-10 min. Optimizes with max_len trim. Explains why greedy fails.
Memoization first; refactors to bottom-up correctly.
Greedy approach; can't fix when shown counterexample.
Cannot derive DP.

playbook: Restate: 'can s be split into dict words (reusable)?'
Pitch DP: 'dp[i] = True if s[:i] segments'.
Code: words = set(wordDict); n = len(s). dp = [False] * (n + 1); dp[0] = True. for i in range(1, n + 1): for j in range(i): if dp[j] and s[j:i] in words: dp[i] = True; break. return dp[n].
Walk 'leetcode': dp[0]=T. dp[4]=T (s[0:4]='leet'). dp[8]=T (dp[4]=T and s[4:8]='code'). ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min. Follow-up: Word Break II (return all segmentations).
dont_do: Don't greedy.
Don't use list for dict.
Don't recurse without memo.
Don't try every word in dict — try every split point.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up DP
approaches.time: O(n² * w) where w is avg word length comparison
approaches.explanation: dp[i] = True iff s[:i] can be segmented. dp[0] = True (empty string is trivially segmented). For each i, look back for a valid split: some j where dp[j] is True AND s[j:i] is a dictionary word. Set-based dict lookup is O(L) for word length L. Total work O(n² L).

title: Decode Ways
summary: A message containing letters from A-Z can be encoded by mapping 'A' = 1, 'B' = 2, ..., 'Z' = 26. Given a string s containing only digits, return the number of ways to decode it. '0' has no mapping; codes 10-26 are valid two-digit combinations.
constraints: s contains only digits and may have leading zeros.

examples.explanation: Leading zero — no valid decoding starts with '0'.

starter_code:
def numDecodings(s):
    # dp[i] = ways to decode s[:i]. Base dp[0] = 1, dp[1] = 1 if s[0] != '0' else 0.
    # Transition: add dp[i-1] if s[i-1] != '0'; add dp[i-2] if s[i-2:i] is in 10..26.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: 1D DP similar to Climbing Stairs. dp[i] = ways to decode s[:i]. From position i: (1) if s[i-1] is '1'-'9', dp[i] += dp[i-1] (single-digit decode); (2) if s[i-2:i] is in '10'..'26', dp[i] += dp[i-2] (two-digit decode). Handle '0' as a special case — can't stand alone.

clarifying_questions.question: Are leading zeros (like '06') ever valid?
clarifying_questions.why: No. '06' means starting with 0, which can't decode (no letter for 0). Spec returns 0.

clarifying_questions.question: Is '0' alone valid?
clarifying_questions.why: No — same reason.

clarifying_questions.question: Numbers above 26?
clarifying_questions.why: Only 1-26 are letters. So '27' = '2','7' (only one way). '32' = '3','2' (only one way).

clarifying_questions.question: Why is 0 the trouble case?
clarifying_questions.why: 0 can only appear as part of '10' or '20'. Standalone or in '30'-'90' it kills the decoding. Need explicit checks.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the string be very long?
clarifying_questions.why: 100 max per spec. DP O(n) handles easily.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Bottom-up DP. dp[0] = 1, dp[1] = 1 if s[0] != '0' else 0. For i from 2 to n: dp[i] = (dp[i-1] if s[i-1] != '0' else 0) + (dp[i-2] if 10 <= int(s[i-2:i]) <= 26 else 0). Return dp[n]. O(1) space possible with two rolling vars.

edge_cases.details: Return 0.
Only valid if part of '10' or '20'. Otherwise dp drops to 0.
Fibonacci-like growth (similar to Climbing Stairs).
Two-digit invalid → only single-digit decode counts.
Return 0.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to handle '0' specially.
Allowing '07', '08', etc., as valid two-digit codes (they're not — must be 10-26).
Counting '0' as a valid single-digit decoding.
Off-by-one on indices.
Recursion without memo — exponential.

faang.introduction: Classic 1D DP with tricky edge cases. The '0' handling separates strong from average candidates.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta, Google. The high frequency of '0' edge cases makes this a popular bug-hunt.

rubric.criteria: Codes in 8-12 min handling all '0' cases. Walks through '226' and '06'.
Solves correctly; misses one '0' case until tested.
Treats '0' as a valid decode; multiple '0' bugs.
Cannot derive DP.

playbook: Restate: 'ways to decode digit string into letters'.
Insight: 'Climbing Stairs DP with special handling for 0'.

Code: if not s or s[0] == '0': return 0. n = len(s). dp = [0] * (n + 1); dp[0], dp[1] = 1, 1. for i in range(2, n + 1): if s[i-1] != '0': dp[i] += dp[i-1]. two = int(s[i-2:i]); if 10 <= two <= 26: dp[i] += dp[i-2]. return dp[n].
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-15 min. Follow-up: Decode Ways II (with '*' wildcards).
dont_do: Don't allow '07' as two-digit.
Don't count '0' as single-digit.
Don't recurse without memo.
Don't off-by-one indices.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up DP
approaches.explanation: Variant of Climbing Stairs. dp[i] = ways to decode s[:i]. From each i, consider decoding the last 1 or 2 characters. '0' is invalid as single-digit, so guard. Two-digit codes only valid in [10, 26]. Sum the contributions. O(n) time.

title: Maximum Product Subarray
summary: Given an integer array nums, find a contiguous non-empty subarray that has the largest PRODUCT, and return that product.
constraints: The product of any prefix or suffix is guaranteed to fit in a 32-bit integer.
starter_code:
def maxProduct(nums):
    # Track both running max AND running min (negatives flip them).
    # At each step: candidates are nums[i], min*nums[i], max*nums[i]. Update both.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Kadane's twist: track BOTH running max product AND running min product. A negative number can flip min to max. At each step: new_max = max(nums[i], max*nums[i], min*nums[i]); new_min = min(nums[i], max*nums[i], min*nums[i]). Track the global maximum.

clarifying_questions.question: Subarray is contiguous?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Different from subsequence.

clarifying_questions.question: Can be a single element?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — minimum subarray length is 1.

clarifying_questions.question: Negatives possible?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. They're THE complication — a sequence with even count of negatives can have huge positive product.

clarifying_questions.question: What about zeros?
clarifying_questions.why: A zero resets the running product. Both max and min effectively restart from the next element.

clarifying_questions.question: Overflow?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, products fit in 32-bit. Python ints are unbounded, so no issue.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Kadane's algorithm doesn't directly work — multiplying flips signs. Solution: track running max AND running min simultaneously. At each new element: candidates are itself (start fresh), running_max * itself, running_min * itself. Take max of those for new max, min for new min. Update global answer with new max.

edge_cases.details: Return that element.
Product of whole array.
Splits problem; max could be left or right side.
Multiplies to positive — algorithm picks them up.
Best to use only some — algorithm explores subarrays.

common_mistakes: Plain Kadane — fails on negatives flipping.
Tracking only max — loses negative-pair-becomes-positive case.
Initializing max/min to 0 — wrong (single negative element should be max).
Forgetting to include nums[i] alone as a candidate (start fresh).
Not updating global max each step.

faang.introduction: Kadane's with a twist. Tests recognizing that tracking both min and max is necessary.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta, Google. The twin-state insight is the discriminator.
really_testing: Recognizing plain Kadane fails on negatives.
Tracking BOTH running max and running min.
Update order (don't overwrite max before using it in min calculation).
Edge cases: zeros, single elements, all negatives.

rubric.criteria: Twin-state idea immediately. Codes in 5-8 min.
Tries Kadane, recognizes failure on negatives, refactors.
Plain Kadane; can't fix when shown negative case.
Can't derive any DP.

playbook: Restate: 'max product subarray, contiguous'.
Insight: 'negatives flip sign — track both running max and running min'.

Code: result = max_so_far = min_so_far = nums[0]. for x in nums[1:]: candidates = (x, max_so_far * x, min_so_far * x). new_max = max(candidates); new_min = min(candidates). max_so_far, min_so_far = new_max, new_min. result = max(result, max_so_far). return result.
Walk [2,3,-2,4]: result=max=min=2. x=3: cands (3,6,6) → max=6, min=3. result=6. x=-2: cands (-2,-12,-6) → max=-2, min=-12. result=6. x=4: cands (4,-8,-48) → max=4, min=-48. result=6. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 6-10 min. Follow-up: Maximum Sum Subarray (Kadane's plain). Sliding window variants.
dont_do: Don't plain Kadane.
Don't update max before computing new min using old max.
Don't initialize to 0.
Don't forget single-element start.

approaches.name: Kadane's with Min and Max Tracking
approaches.code:
def maxProduct(nums):
    result = max_so_far = min_so_far = nums[0]
    for x in nums[1:]:
        # Tuple of candidates (must compute before overwriting either tracker)
        candidates = (x, max_so_far * x, min_so_far * x)
        new_max = max(candidates)
        new_min = min(candidates)
        max_so_far, min_so_far = new_max, new_min
        result = max(result, max_so_far)
    return result
approaches.explanation: Track both running max and running min. At each element, the three candidates are: start fresh (x alone), extend max by multiplying x, extend min by multiplying x. Update both trackers — and the global result with the new max. The min tracking captures the 'long negative streak flips to positive' case.

title: Longest Palindromic Substring
summary: Given a string s, return the longest palindromic substring of s.
constraints: s consists of digits and English letters.
starter_code:
def longestPalindrome(s):
    # Expand around each center. O(n^2) time, O(1) space.
    # 2n-1 centers: each character + each gap between adjacent characters.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Expand-around-center. For each of 2n-1 possible centers (each character, each gap), expand outward while characters match. Track the longest span seen. O(n²) time, O(1) extra space — simpler than DP and faster in practice.

clarifying_questions.question: Return any valid longest, or a specific one?
clarifying_questions.why: Any. If multiple ties exist, any is fine.

clarifying_questions.question: Odd-length and even-length palindromes both possible?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. That's why 2n-1 centers: n single-char centers (odd) + n-1 gap centers (even).

clarifying_questions.question: Manacher's algorithm — overkill?
clarifying_questions.why: O(n) but very tricky to code. Expand-around-center O(n²) is the bar; mention Manacher's as an optional follow-up.

clarifying_questions.question: Case-sensitive?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, no normalization needed. Match exactly.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty string?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints n >= 1, so trivially the empty case doesn't occur. Defensive return '' is cheap.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Expand-around-center. Helper: expand(l, r) — while in bounds and s[l] == s[r], decrement l, increment r. Returns s[l+1:r]. For each i: check expand(i, i) (odd center) and expand(i, i+1) (even center). Track the longest.

edge_cases.details: Trivially palindrome of length 1.
Entire string is palindrome.
Return any single character.
Return any.
Algorithm checks both via 2n-1 centers.

common_mistakes: Only checking odd centers (single-char) — misses 'bb', 'abba', etc.
Off-by-one in slicing (s[l+1:r] vs s[l:r+1]).
O(n³) brute force enumerating all substrings.
Trying DP without realizing the recurrence dp[i][j] = (s[i]==s[j]) and dp[i+1][j-1].
Forgetting to update the longest when current is bigger.

faang.introduction: Classic palindrome problem. Expand-around-center is THE expected solution.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta, Google. Often the first 'medium' in a string-heavy round.
really_testing: Handling both odd and even palindrome lengths.

rubric.criteria: Expand-around-center in 8-10 min. Both odd/even handled. Walks through example.
DP solution (O(n²)) or expand-around-center with one bug.
Misses even-length palindromes. Or O(n³) brute force.
Cannot articulate any sub-cubic approach.

playbook: Restate: 'longest palindromic substring'.
Insight: 'expand around each center, 2n-1 centers'.
Helper: def expand(l, r): while l >= 0 and r < len(s) and s[l] == s[r]: l -= 1; r += 1. return s[l+1:r].
Code: longest = ''. for i in range(len(s)): p1 = expand(i, i); p2 = expand(i, i+1). longest = max(longest, p1, p2, key=len). return longest.
Walk 'babad': i=0 expand(0,0)='b', expand(0,1)=''. i=1 expand(1,1)='aba'(b matches), expand(1,2)=''. longest='aba'. Continue. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min. Follow-up: Manacher's (O(n)) — usually only requested at the highest bar.
dont_do: Don't miss even-length palindromes.
Don't O(n³) brute force.
Don't try Manacher's first.
Don't off-by-one slicing.

approaches.name: Expand Around Center
approaches.explanation: For each of 2n-1 centers, expand outward while characters match. Each expansion takes O(n) in the worst case; total O(n²). O(1) extra space. Cleaner than the DP approach (O(n²) time, O(n²) space) and easier to reason about.

title: Palindromic Substrings
summary: Given a string s, return the COUNT of palindromic substrings in it. Substrings with different start or end positions are counted separately, even if they consist of the same characters.
constraints: s consists of lowercase English letters.

examples.explanation: 'a', 'b', 'c'. Each single char is a palindrome.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Same expand-around-center. For each of 2n-1 centers, every successful expansion (chars matching) is a new palindromic substring — count it. Sum across all centers.

clarifying_questions.question: Count substrings or distinct substrings?
clarifying_questions.why: Substrings — duplicates counted separately by position.

clarifying_questions.question: Single characters count?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — each is trivially a palindrome.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty substring?
clarifying_questions.why: Not counted. Spec implicitly assumes length >= 1.

clarifying_questions.question: Same expand-around-center as LPS, just count?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Almost identical code, but increment a counter instead of tracking length.

clarifying_questions.question: Can DP also solve this?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. dp[i][j] = True iff s[i:j+1] is palindrome. Total trues = answer. O(n²) time, O(n²) space.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Two helper expansions per center i: odd (i, i) and even (i, i+1). Each step that keeps l, r in bounds and s[l]==s[r] is one more palindromic substring — count it. Sum across all i.

edge_cases.details: 1 palindrome.
n + n-1 + ... + 1 = n(n+1)/2 palindromes.
Just n (single chars).
Algorithm counts each correctly.

common_mistakes: O(n³) brute force.
Forgetting even-length centers.
Off-by-one when counting.
Counting the same palindrome twice (e.g., 'aba' from center 'b' vs 'aa' from gap).
Subtracting 1 because 'single chars aren't palindromes' — they are.

faang.introduction: Counting variant of Longest Palindromic Substring. Same algorithm.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Microsoft. Often follows LPS in the same round.

rubric.criteria: Codes in 4-6 min. Reuses LPS pattern.
Works it out, minor bug fixed.
Misses even centers.
Brute force O(n³).

playbook: Restate: 'count palindromic substrings'.
Pitch: 'expand-around-center, count each successful expansion'.
Code: count = 0. def expand(l, r): nonlocal count. while l>=0 and r<len(s) and s[l]==s[r]: count+=1; l-=1; r+=1. for i in range(len(s)): expand(i, i); expand(i, i+1). return count.
Walk 'aaa': odd centers: 'a'(1), 'aaa'(2), 'a'(1) — wait need to count each step. expand(0,0): match → count=1. l=-1 stop. expand(0,1): s[0]==s[1] → count=2. l=-1 stop. Continue. Total 6 for 'aaa'. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 4-7 min.
dont_do: Don't O(n³).
Don't skip even centers.
Don't double-count via DP indices.
Don't think single chars don't count.

approaches.name: Expand Around Center
approaches.explanation: Same expand-around-center as Longest Palindromic Substring, but count every successful step instead of tracking length. Each expansion that finds a palindrome at the current (l, r) adds 1 to the count. O(n²) time, O(1) space.

title: Partition Equal Subset Sum
summary: Given an integer array nums, return True if you can partition the array into two subsets with equal sum. Each element must be in exactly one subset.

examples.explanation: Cannot split [1,2,3,5] into two equal-sum subsets.

starter_code:
def canPartition(nums):
    # Reduces to subset-sum: is there a subset summing to total/2?
    # 1D boolean DP of size target+1. Iterate nums; for each num, update dp from right to left.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Reduces to subset-sum: is there a subset of nums summing to total/2? If total is odd, immediately False. Otherwise, 0/1 knapsack DP. dp[j] = True iff some subset sums to j. Iterate nums; for each, update dp[j] = dp[j] or dp[j - num] (iterating j from target down to num to avoid double-counting).

clarifying_questions.question: Each element used at most once?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. 0/1 knapsack, not unbounded.

clarifying_questions.question: What if total is odd?
clarifying_questions.why: Can't split evenly. Return False immediately.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty subset allowed (sum = 0)?
clarifying_questions.why: Technically yes — but spec implies non-trivial split. dp[0] = True base case is fine.

clarifying_questions.question: Why iterate j right-to-left?
clarifying_questions.why: To avoid using the same num twice in the same iteration. Left-to-right would treat the problem as unbounded knapsack (reuse allowed).

clarifying_questions.question: Time complexity?
clarifying_questions.why: O(n * target) where target = sum/2. With n=200 and max sum = 200*100 = 20000, target=10000 → 2 million ops, fine.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Subset-sum reduction. Compute total; if odd, False. target = total // 2. dp = [False] * (target+1); dp[0] = True. For each num: for j from target down to num: dp[j] = dp[j] or dp[j-num]. Return dp[target]. O(n*target) time, O(target) space.

edge_cases.details: Can't split → False.
True. Each in its own subset.
False immediately.
True if length > 0. Spec says nums >= 1.
Pseudo-polynomial. With nums up to 100 and length 200, target up to 10000 → fine.

common_mistakes: Forgetting the odd-total early return.
Iterating j left-to-right — turns into unbounded knapsack.
Trying greedy or exhaustive search — exponential.
Off-by-one in range (j from target downto num-1 vs num).
Treating as a 2D DP unnecessarily — 1D rolling works.

faang.introduction: 0/1 knapsack subset-sum variant. Tests the DP iteration direction trick.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Microsoft. The 'iterate right-to-left' detail is the discriminator.

rubric.criteria: Reduces to subset-sum and codes 1D DP in 6-8 min. Explains direction.
Codes 2D DP. Refactors to 1D when prompted.
Greedy or exhaustive; doesn't see DP.
Can't articulate any algorithm.

playbook: Restate: 'partition into two equal-sum subsets'.
Reduction: 'subset summing to total/2'.
Pitch 1D knapsack DP.

Code: total = sum(nums). if total % 2: return False. target = total // 2. dp = [False] * (target + 1); dp[0] = True. for num in nums: for j in range(target, num - 1, -1): dp[j] = dp[j] or dp[j - num]. return dp[target].
Walk [1,5,11,5]: total=22, target=11. After processing nums: dp[11] = True (1+5+5). ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min. Follow-up: Last Stone Weight II (similar reduction).
dont_do: Don't iterate j left-to-right (becomes unbounded).
Don't forget odd-total exit.
Don't 2D when 1D suffices.
Don't brute force.

approaches.name: 1D Knapsack DP
approaches.explanation: Reduces to: is there a subset summing to total/2? If total is odd, impossible. Otherwise, classic 0/1 knapsack subset-sum DP. dp[j] = True iff some subset of seen elements sums to j. For each num, update dp from RIGHT to LEFT (so we don't reuse this num within the same iteration). O(n * target) time, O(target) space.

title: Unique Paths
summary: A robot is on an m × n grid at position (0, 0). It can only move right or down. Return the number of unique paths to reach (m-1, n-1).

examples.explanation: C(8, 2) = 28 paths from top-left to bottom-right of a 3x7 grid.

starter_code:
def uniquePaths(m, n):
    # dp[i][j] = paths from (0,0) to (i,j). dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1].
    # 1D rolling row suffices.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: 2D DP. dp[i][j] = paths to reach (i,j) = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. Base: dp[0][j] = dp[i][0] = 1 (only one path along the edges). Optimization: only need previous row, so 1D rolling row reduces space to O(n). Math: C(m+n-2, m-1) gives the closed form (combinatorics).

clarifying_questions.question: Only right and down moves?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. No diagonals, no backtracking.

clarifying_questions.question: Are there obstacles?
clarifying_questions.why: No in this problem (LC 62). Unique Paths II adds obstacles.

clarifying_questions.question: Big m or n?
clarifying_questions.why: ≤ 100. Answer up to 2 * 10^9 — fits in int.

clarifying_questions.question: Combinatorics shortcut?
clarifying_questions.why: C(m+n-2, m-1). Total moves = m+n-2; choose which m-1 are downward. Closed form is O(min(m,n)).

clarifying_questions.question: Why DP if combinatorics works?
clarifying_questions.why: Combinatorics needs factorial / large numbers. DP is simpler to code, generalizes to obstacles, and complexity is the same in practice.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: 2D DP with O(n) rolling row. Initialize row = [1] * n (first row's count). For each subsequent row i: row[j] += row[j-1] (cumulative sum trick — encodes dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]). After m-1 iterations, row[n-1] is the answer.

edge_cases.details: Already at destination. Return 1.
Only one path (all in one direction).
Symmetric — m and n swappable.
Answer up to 2*10^9. Python ints fine; Java/C++ need long.
Extension — set dp[i][j] = 0 at obstacles.

common_mistakes: Recursing without memo — exponential.
Allocating O(m*n) instead of O(n) rolling.
Off-by-one (m+n-1 vs m+n-2 in combinatorics).
Confusing m and n.
Initializing dp[0][0] = 0 instead of 1.

faang.introduction: 2D DP introduction. The simplest 'count paths on a grid' problem.
faang.introduction_context: Common phone screen at Amazon, Microsoft. The bar: solve in 5-7 min.

rubric.criteria: Codes 1D DP in 5 min. Mentions combinatorics.
2D DP works correctly.
Recursion without memo.
Cannot derive recurrence.

playbook: Restate: 'count paths from top-left to bottom-right, right/down only'.
Recurrence: 'dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]'.
Pitch 1D rolling: 'row[j] += row[j-1] over m-1 iterations'.
Code: row = [1] * n. for _ in range(m - 1): for j in range(1, n): row[j] += row[j-1]. return row[-1].
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 5-8 min. Follow-up: Unique Paths II (obstacles), Minimum Path Sum.
dont_do: Don't allocate O(m*n).
Don't recurse without memo.
Don't mix m and n.
Don't off-by-one combinatorics.

approaches.name: 1D Rolling DP
approaches.explanation: Roll a single row. Initially [1]*n (top row: one path each, only going right). For each subsequent row, update in place: row[j] += row[j-1] expresses dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] (old row[j]) + dp[i][j-1] (already updated row[j-1]). O(m*n) time, O(n) space.

approaches.name: Combinatorics
approaches.explanation: Total moves = (m-1) down + (n-1) right = m+n-2. Choose which m-1 of them are 'down' (or equivalently n-1 are 'right'). C(m+n-2, m-1). One-line closed form.

title: Longest Common Subsequence
summary: Given two strings text1 and text2, return the length of their longest common subsequence. If there is no common subsequence, return 0.
constraints: Both consist of lowercase English letters.

examples.explanation: 'ace' is a common subsequence of length 3.

starter_code:
def longestCommonSubsequence(text1, text2):
    # dp[i][j] = LCS of text1[:i] and text2[:j].
    # Recurrence: if chars match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1.
    # Else: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Classic 2D DP. dp[i][j] = LCS of text1[:i] and text2[:j]. If text1[i-1] == text2[j-1], dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1. Else dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Build table bottom-up. Final answer: dp[m][n]. Space can be reduced to O(min(m,n)) with rolling.

clarifying_questions.question: Subsequence preserves order, not contiguous?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — subsequence (gaps allowed), not substring.

clarifying_questions.question: Are we returning length or the actual sequence?
clarifying_questions.why: Length per spec. Building the sequence requires backtracking through dp.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty strings handled?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints, length >= 1. dp[0][_] = dp[_][0] = 0 handles empty as base case.

clarifying_questions.question: Case sensitivity?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, all lowercase. No normalization needed.

clarifying_questions.question: Can I do O(min(m,n)) space?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — only need previous row. Roll with two 1D arrays.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Bottom-up 2D DP. Build dp of size (m+1) x (n+1). For each cell, character match → diagonal + 1; no match → max of up or left. O(m*n) time and space. Roll to O(min(m,n)) space if asked.

edge_cases.details: LCS = length of either.
LCS = 0.
LCS = 1.
LCS = length of shorter.
LCS = 0. Base case.

common_mistakes: Recursion without memoization — exponential.
Treating as substring (contiguous) — different problem.
Off-by-one in dp indices (text1[i] vs text1[i-1]).
Confusing rows and columns.
Building O(m*n) when O(min(m,n)) suffices.

faang.introduction: Canonical string DP. The foundation for Edit Distance, Distinct Subsequences, and many others.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Google, Meta. The recurrence is famous; the interviewer wants to see you derive and code it cleanly.

rubric.criteria: Codes 2D DP in 8-10 min. Walks through example. Mentions O(n) space.
Correct, with one indexing bug fixed.
Treats as substring. Or buggy recurrence.
Cannot derive DP.

playbook: Restate: 'LCS length, preserve order, gaps allowed'.
Recurrence: 'match → diag+1; mismatch → max(up, left)'.

Code: m, n = len(text1), len(text2). dp = [[0]*(n+1) for _ in range(m+1)]. for i in range(1, m+1): for j in range(1, n+1): if text1[i-1] == text2[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1. else: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). return dp[m][n].
Walk 'abcde' / 'ace': dp[5][3] = 3. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-15 min. Follow-up: Edit Distance, Distinct Subsequences.
dont_do: Don't treat as substring.
Don't recurse without memo.
Don't off-by-one indices.
Don't allocate full 2D when 1D suffices (in production).

approaches.name: Bottom-Up 2D DP
approaches.explanation: Classic 2D DP. dp[i][j] = LCS of text1's first i chars and text2's first j chars. Match: extend the diagonal. Mismatch: best of skipping a char in either string. Base row/column is 0 (empty string vs anything = 0). O(m*n) time and space.

title: Coin Change II
summary: You are given an array coins representing distinct coin denominations and an integer amount. Return the NUMBER of distinct combinations that make up amount. You can use each coin an unlimited number of times. If amount is unreachable, return 0.
starter_code:
def change(amount, coins):
    # 1D DP. dp[i] = ways to make amount i.
    # Iterate coins (outer), amount (inner) to count COMBINATIONS (not permutations).
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: 1D DP, but order of loops matters. dp[i] = ways to make amount i. Initialize dp[0] = 1 (one way to make 0: empty combination). For each coin (outer loop), for each amount from coin to target (inner loop): dp[i] += dp[i - coin]. The outer-coin loop ensures combinations (order doesn't matter); inner-amount-first would count permutations.

clarifying_questions.question: Combinations or permutations?
clarifying_questions.why: Combinations per spec. {1,2} and {2,1} count as one. Loop order matters.

clarifying_questions.question: Coins reusable?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec — unbounded knapsack. So inner loop iterates left-to-right (reuse allowed).

clarifying_questions.question: amount = 0?
clarifying_questions.why: 1 way — the empty combination. dp[0] = 1 base case.

clarifying_questions.question: What if no combination reaches amount?
clarifying_questions.why: Return 0. dp[amount] stays 0.

clarifying_questions.question: Why coin outer / amount inner?
clarifying_questions.why: If you swap to amount outer / coin inner, you count permutations: each ordering becomes a separate count. Coin-outer locks ordering.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Unbounded knapsack count variant. dp[i] = number of combinations summing to i. Initialize dp[0]=1. For each coin: for each i from coin to amount: dp[i] += dp[i - coin]. Critical: outer loop is coins (ensures combinations).

edge_cases.details: Return 1.
Return 1.
Return 0.
Skipped — inner range starts at coin.

edge_cases.category: Multiple coins same value (out of spec)
edge_cases.details: Spec says distinct. Algorithm would still count correctly.

common_mistakes: Swapping loop order — counts permutations.
Inner loop right-to-left — turns into 0/1 knapsack (no reuse).
Forgetting dp[0] = 1.
Recursion without memo — exponential.
Trying to enumerate explicitly — exponential.

faang.introduction: Variant of Coin Change focused on counting. Loop order is the discriminator.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Microsoft. The 'why does loop order matter?' question separates strong from average.

rubric.criteria: Codes 1D DP in 8-10 min, explains coin-outer.
Solves correctly; might initially flip loop order before debugging.
Counts permutations and can't see why.
Cannot derive DP.

playbook: Restate: 'number of combinations summing to amount; coins reusable'.
Insight: 'unbounded knapsack count; coin-outer for combinations'.
Code: dp = [0] * (amount + 1); dp[0] = 1. for c in coins: for i in range(c, amount + 1): dp[i] += dp[i - c]. return dp[amount].
Walk amount=5 coins=[1,2,5]: after coin 1: dp = [1,1,1,1,1,1]. After coin 2: dp = [1,1,2,2,3,3]. After coin 5: dp = [1,1,2,2,3,4]. Return 4. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min. Follow-up: Combination Sum, Permutation Sum.
dont_do: Don't flip loop order.
Don't right-to-left inner.
Don't enumerate.
Don't recurse without memo.

approaches.name: 1D Unbounded Knapsack DP
approaches.explanation: Unbounded knapsack count variant. dp[i] = number of combinations summing to i. Outer loop iterates coins (ensures combinations, not permutations). Inner loop left-to-right (allows reuse — unbounded). dp[0] = 1 is the empty combination. O(amount * coins) time, O(amount) space.

title: Edit Distance
summary: Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of operations to convert word1 into word2. Operations: insert a character, delete a character, replace a character.
constraints: Both consist of lowercase English letters.
starter_code:
def minDistance(word1, word2):
    # dp[i][j] = edit distance between word1[:i] and word2[:j].
    # If chars match: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] (no op).
    # Else: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(insert: dp[i][j-1], delete: dp[i-1][j], replace: dp[i-1][j-1]).
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Levenshtein distance. dp[i][j] = edit distance between word1[:i] and word2[:j]. Base: dp[0][j] = j (insert j chars), dp[i][0] = i (delete i chars). Transition: if chars match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] (no cost). Else: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j-1] replace, dp[i-1][j] delete, dp[i][j-1] insert).

clarifying_questions.question: Operation costs — all equal (1)?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Generalization allows different costs, but baseline is uniform.

clarifying_questions.question: Both strings can be empty?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints, length >= 0. Empty → length = number of ops needed.

clarifying_questions.question: Mutate the input or create a new string?
clarifying_questions.why: We only count ops. Algorithm is index-based; doesn't actually build the target.

clarifying_questions.question: Order: which is the source and which is the target?
clarifying_questions.why: Doesn't matter — Levenshtein is symmetric. By convention word1 → word2.

clarifying_questions.question: Space optimization?
clarifying_questions.why: Only need previous row → O(min(m,n)) space. Useful for very long strings.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Classic 2D DP. Build (m+1) x (n+1) table. Fill first row/col with insert/delete counts. Iterate filling each cell: if chars equal, take diagonal; else, 1 + min of three neighbors (insert/delete/replace). Final answer in dp[m][n].

edge_cases.details: Need to insert n chars.
Need to delete m chars.
0 ops.
Replace each — n ops.
Insertions or deletions only.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to include 'replace' in the min — missing diagonal case.
Wrong base cases (dp[0][j] should be j, dp[i][0] should be i).
Off-by-one indexing (word1[i] vs word1[i-1]).
Recursion without memoization — exponential.
Not handling empty strings.

faang.introduction: The canonical 2D DP problem. Every interview prep guide mentions this.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft. The recurrence with three operations is the famous part.
really_testing: All three operations in the recurrence (insert, delete, replace).

rubric.criteria: Derives recurrence in 5 min, codes in 12-15 min. Walks through example.
Correct with one operation missed initially, then fixed.
Only insert/delete (missing replace) — gets wrong answer.
Cannot derive DP.

playbook: Restate: 'min ops (ins/del/repl) to turn word1 into word2'.
Pitch 2D DP.

Code: m, n = len(word1), len(word2). dp = [[0]*(n+1) for _ in range(m+1)]. for i in range(m+1): dp[i][0] = i. for j in range(n+1): dp[0][j] = j. for i in range(1, m+1): for j in range(1, n+1): if word1[i-1] == word2[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]. else: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). return dp[m][n].
Walk 'horse' / 'ros': end at dp[5][3] = 3. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 12-18 min. Follow-up: One Edit Distance (LC 161), Reconstruct the edits.
dont_do: Don't miss 'replace' in the min.
Don't recurse without memo.
Don't off-by-one base.
Don't allocate full 2D if O(n) space is asked.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up 2D DP
approaches.explanation: Classic Levenshtein DP. dp[i][j] = min edits to transform word1[:i] into word2[:j]. Base row/col reflects 'empty + j inserts' or 'i deletes + empty'. Match → diagonal (no cost). Mismatch → 1 + min of three neighbors (insert/delete/replace). O(m*n) time and space.

title: Target Sum
summary: You are given an integer array nums and an integer target. You want to assign '+' or '-' to each element so the sum equals target. Return the number of different ways to achieve this.
starter_code:
def findTargetSumWays(nums, target):
    # Memoization on (index, current_sum) OR reduce to subset-sum:
    # Sum_plus - Sum_minus = target. Sum_plus + Sum_minus = total.
    # => Sum_plus = (total + target) / 2. Count subsets summing to Sum_plus.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Two approaches. (1) Memoization on (index, current_sum) — explores all 2^n sign assignments. (2) Reduce to subset-sum: let S+ be the sum of '+' elements and S- the sum of '-' elements. S+ - S- = target, S+ + S- = total. So S+ = (total + target) / 2. Count subsets summing to that. Requires (total + target) be even and non-negative.

clarifying_questions.question: Each number must be assigned a sign (can't skip)?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Every nums[i] gets + or -.

clarifying_questions.question: Target can be negative?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec (-1000 ≤ target). Algorithm handles.

clarifying_questions.question: What if total + target is odd or negative?
clarifying_questions.why: 0 ways. The reduction requires (total + target) to be even AND non-negative.

clarifying_questions.question: Can numbers be zero?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Each zero doubles the count (sign doesn't change sum, but counts as a distinct assignment).

clarifying_questions.question: Memoization or DP — which?
clarifying_questions.why: Either. Memoization is cleaner to write; DP reduction is more elegant if you see it.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Brute force: 2^n sign assignments. With memoization on (index, current_sum), it reduces to O(n * sum) states. Subset-sum reduction: count subsets of nums summing to (total + target) / 2 — that's the count of distinct '+' assignments.

edge_cases.details: Each zero doubles the count (sign doesn't matter for sum but counts).
Either +num or -num. Count = 1 if matches target, else 0.
0 ways.
Exactly 1 way (all + or all -).
0 ways (reduction fails).

common_mistakes: Brute force without memo — 2^n, TLE on n=20.
Forgetting the (total + target) sign / parity check.
Confusion on subset-sum reduction.
Counting same state multiple times (memo key bug).
Off-by-one on the reduced subset target.

faang.introduction: Combinatorial DP. Tests both memoization and the optional subset-sum reduction.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Microsoft. The reduction trick impresses interviewers.
really_testing: Edge cases around zero, negative, parity.

rubric.criteria: Memoization in 8 min, subset-sum in 12 min, picks one and ships.
Memoization correct.
Brute force only.
Can't derive any solution.

playbook: Restate: 'count ways to assign +/- signs to reach target'.
Memoization approach: 'state = (i, current_sum); transitions = +nums[i] or -nums[i]'.
Code: from functools import lru_cache. @lru_cache(maxsize=None). def go(i, s): if i == len(nums): return 1 if s == target else 0. return go(i+1, s + nums[i]) + go(i+1, s - nums[i]). return go(0, 0).
Or reduction: total = sum(nums). if (total + target) % 2 != 0 or total < abs(target): return 0. P = (total + target) // 2. count subsets summing to P (knapsack).
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-15 min.
dont_do: Don't 2^n brute force.
Don't forget memo.
Don't ignore parity/sign in reduction.
Don't memo without including the relevant state.

approaches.name: Memoization on (index, sum)
approaches.explanation: Top-down DP with memoization. State: index in nums + running sum. At each index, two choices: add or subtract nums[i]. Memoize on the (index, sum) pair so we don't recompute. O(n * total) unique states; each transition O(1).

approaches.name: Subset-Sum Reduction
approaches.explanation: Elegant reduction. Let S+ = sum of '+' elements, S- = sum of '-' elements. We have S+ - S- = target AND S+ + S- = total. Solving: S+ = (total + target) / 2. So the problem becomes 'count subsets summing to S+' — a standard 0/1 knapsack subset-sum count. O(n * P) time, O(P) space.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown
summary: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on day i. Find the maximum profit you can achieve with the following restrictions: (1) you may complete as many transactions as you like (buy → sell), (2) after you sell, you cannot buy on the next day (1-day cooldown), (3) you can only hold ONE share at a time.

examples.explanation: Buy day 0 ($1), sell day 1 ($2). Cooldown day 2. Buy day 3 ($0), sell day 4 ($2). Profit (2-1) + (2-0) = 3.

starter_code:
def maxProfit(prices):
    # State machine: hold, sold, rest.
    # Transitions: hold = max(hold, rest - price); sold = hold + price; rest = max(rest, sold).
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: State machine DP with three states: hold (own a stock), sold (just sold today), rest (not holding, not on cooldown). Transitions: hold[i] = max(hold[i-1], rest[i-1] - price); sold[i] = hold[i-1] + price; rest[i] = max(rest[i-1], sold[i-1]). Answer is max(sold[n-1], rest[n-1]) — can't end in 'hold'.

clarifying_questions.question: How many transactions allowed?
clarifying_questions.why: Unlimited per spec. Just respect the cooldown between sell and next buy.

clarifying_questions.question: Cooldown duration — 1 day?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. After selling on day i, can buy earliest on day i+2.

clarifying_questions.question: Can hold multiple stocks?
clarifying_questions.why: No — only one at a time.

clarifying_questions.question: Negative prices?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, prices >= 0. Algorithm doesn't depend on positivity.

clarifying_questions.question: Can transactions overlap (buy before selling previous)?
clarifying_questions.why: No — must sell before buying again. Plus the cooldown.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: State machine DP. Three states per day: hold (owning a stock), sold (just sold today — can't buy tomorrow), rest (idle, no cooldown active). Buy: rest → hold (pay price). Sell: hold → sold (gain price). Rest: stay or transition from sold. Track each state's max profit; roll with three variables.

edge_cases.details: Can't profit. Return 0.
Buy then sell. Profit = diff.
Don't buy. Profit = 0.
No profit possible.
Cooldown limits how often we can capture them. Algorithm finds optimal.

common_mistakes: Greedy 'buy at every dip' — ignores cooldown.
Forgetting the cooldown — sold → buy next day is illegal.
Initial values wrong (hold = -prices[0], others 0).
Returning hold at the end (still owning a share).
Trying to enumerate transactions — exponential.

faang.introduction: State machine DP. The 'three states + transitions' pattern.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta. Variants like 'with fee' use the same skeleton.

rubric.criteria: States and transitions clear in 5 min, codes in 10 min.
Solves with arrays for each state; refactors to vars when prompted.
Greedy approach; can't handle cooldown.
Cannot derive state machine.

playbook: Restate: 'unlimited transactions, 1-day cooldown after selling'.
States: hold (own), sold (just sold), rest (idle).
Transitions: hold = max(hold, rest - p); sold = hold + p; rest = max(rest, sold).
Code: hold, sold, rest = -prices[0], 0, 0. for p in prices[1:]: hold, sold, rest = max(hold, rest - p), hold + p, max(rest, sold). return max(sold, rest).
Walk [1,2,3,0,2]: trace each day's three states; ends sold=3 or rest=3. Return 3. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-15 min. Follow-up: with transaction fee, with k transactions.
dont_do: Don't greedy.
Don't ignore cooldown.
Don't return hold (still owning).
Don't use full O(n*3) arrays — three vars suffice.

approaches.name: State Machine DP, O(1) Space
approaches.code:
def maxProfit(prices):
    if len(prices) <= 1:
        return 0
    # hold: best profit while currently holding a share
    # sold: best profit just sold today (cooldown tomorrow)
    # rest: best profit while idle (no cooldown active)
    hold = -prices[0]
    sold = 0
    rest = 0
    
    for p in prices[1:]:
        new_hold = max(hold, rest - p)   # keep holding OR buy today (from rest)
        new_sold = hold + p              # sell today (must have been holding)
        new_rest = max(rest, sold)       # stay idle OR rest after cooldown
        hold, sold, rest = new_hold, new_sold, new_rest
    
    return max(sold, rest)
approaches.explanation: State machine with three states tracked across days. hold = best profit while owning. sold = just sold today (next day is cooldown). rest = idle, free to buy. Transitions encode the rules. Roll with three variables for O(1) space. End in sold or rest (never hold).

title: Interleaving String
summary: Given strings s1, s2, and s3, return True if s3 is formed by an interleaving of s1 and s2. Interleaving means selecting characters from s1 and s2 (in their original order) to form s3.
constraints: s1, s2, and s3 consist of lowercase English letters.

examples.explanation: s3 can be formed by interleaving s1 and s2 (e.g., aa[d]b[bc]c[ac] picks from both).

starter_code:
def isInterleave(s1, s2, s3):
    # 2D DP. dp[i][j] = can s3[:i+j] be formed by interleaving s1[:i] and s2[:j]?
    # Transition: dp[i][j] = (dp[i-1][j] and s1[i-1] == s3[i+j-1]) or (dp[i][j-1] and s2[j-1] == s3[i+j-1]).
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Classic 2D DP. dp[i][j] = is s3[:i+j] formed by interleaving s1[:i] and s2[:j]? Base: dp[0][0] = True (empty interleave). Transition: dp[i][j] is True if either (a) dp[i-1][j] and s1[i-1] matches s3[i+j-1], or (b) dp[i][j-1] and s2[j-1] matches s3[i+j-1]. Answer: dp[len(s1)][len(s2)].

clarifying_questions.question: Length check first?
clarifying_questions.why: If len(s1) + len(s2) != len(s3), immediately False.

clarifying_questions.question: Characters preserve order within each source?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — that's the 'interleaving' definition.

clarifying_questions.question: Each char of s3 must come from EITHER s1 or s2?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Total chars = len(s1) + len(s2). Each consumed once.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty case?
