clarifying_questions.why: All empty → True. One empty → s3 must equal the other.

clarifying_questions.question: Can I do this greedy?
clarifying_questions.why: No — when both s1's and s2's current char match s3's current, you don't know which to consume. DP handles this branching.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: 2D DP. dp[i][j] tracks whether the FIRST i+j characters of s3 can be formed using first i of s1 and first j of s2. From either dp[i-1][j] (consumed s1's i-th char) or dp[i][j-1] (consumed s2's j-th char). O(m*n) time and space.

edge_cases.details: dp[0][0] = True.
Return False without DP.
Algorithm picks the working path.
DP explores both branches; if either works, True.

edge_cases.category: All chars consumed from one source
edge_cases.details: Walk only one side of the DP table.

common_mistakes: Greedy — fails when multiple choices look valid.
Forgetting the length check.
Off-by-one indexing (s3[i+j] vs s3[i+j-1]).
Treating as 'is s3 a supersequence of s1+s2' — different problem.
Recursion without memo — exponential.

faang.introduction: String interleaving DP. Common at Amazon, Google.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite question — recognizing the 2D DP and avoiding greedy is the key.

rubric.criteria: DP recurrence in 5 min, codes in 10 min.
Memoization version; converts to bottom-up correctly.
Greedy or buggy DP.
Cannot derive.

playbook: Restate: 'is s3 an interleaving of s1 and s2?'
Precheck: len(s1) + len(s2) == len(s3).
DP: 'dp[i][j] = first i+j chars of s3 formable from s1[:i] + s2[:j]'.

Code: if len(s1)+len(s2) != len(s3): return False. m, n = len(s1), len(s2). dp = [[False]*(n+1) for _ in range(m+1)]. dp[0][0] = True. for i in range(m+1): for j in range(n+1): k = i+j-1. if i>0: dp[i][j] |= dp[i-1][j] and s1[i-1]==s3[k]. if j>0: dp[i][j] |= dp[i][j-1] and s2[j-1]==s3[k]. return dp[m][n].
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-15 min.
dont_do: Don't greedy.
Don't skip length check.
Don't recurse without memo.
Don't off-by-one.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up 2D DP
approaches.explanation: dp[i][j] = True iff s3[:i+j] is an interleaving of s1[:i] and s2[:j]. For each cell, either (a) consume s1's i-th char (came from dp[i-1][j]), or (b) consume s2's j-th char (came from dp[i][j-1]). The matched character must equal s3 at the corresponding position. Length precheck saves time on mismatched inputs.

title: Distinct Subsequences
summary: Given two strings s and t, return the number of distinct subsequences of s that equal t.

examples.explanation: Three ways to extract 'rabbit' from 'rabbbit' (different b's used).
Five distinct ways to pick 'bag' from 'babgbag'.

starter_code:
def numDistinct(s, t):
    # dp[i][j] = number of ways s[:i] forms t[:j].
    # If s[i-1] == t[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + dp[i-1][j] (use this char OR skip it).
    # Else: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] (must skip this s char).
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Count-style 2D DP. dp[i][j] = number of distinct subsequences of s[:i] equal to t[:j]. Base: dp[i][0] = 1 for all i (empty t is always achievable). Transition: if s[i-1] == t[j-1], dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] (use this char) + dp[i-1][j] (skip it). Else: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] (must skip, can't match).

clarifying_questions.question: Distinct in what sense — by index choices?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Two subsequences are distinct if they pick different INDEX sets from s, even if they spell the same word.

clarifying_questions.question: Case-sensitive?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec doesn't normalize. Match exactly.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty t?
clarifying_questions.why: 1 way (empty subsequence). dp[i][0] = 1 for all i.

clarifying_questions.question: t longer than s?
clarifying_questions.why: 0 ways. Algorithm handles — dp stays 0.

clarifying_questions.question: Can I do space O(n)?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — only need previous row. Roll with one 1D array.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Count DP. For each (i, j): if chars match, you can either use s[i-1] (adding dp[i-1][j-1] ways) or skip it (dp[i-1][j]). If they don't match, you must skip — dp[i-1][j]. Sum the count, return dp[m][n].

edge_cases.details: Always 1 way.
0 ways.
1 way.
Each occurrence creates branching — what makes the count > 1.

common_mistakes: Confusion between subsequence and substring.
Wrong recurrence on mismatch (should NOT skip a t char).
Off-by-one indexing.
Not initializing dp[i][0] = 1.
Recursion without memo.

faang.introduction: Count-style 2D DP. Tests careful recurrence design.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Microsoft. Harder than LCS because of the count interpretation.

rubric.criteria: Recurrence in 5-8 min, codes in 12-15 min.
Solves correctly with one base-case fix.
Misses use-or-skip; only counts skips.
Can't derive.

playbook: Restate: 'count distinct subsequences of s equal to t'.
Recurrence: 'match → use+skip; mismatch → skip only'.

Code: m, n = len(s), len(t). dp = [[0]*(n+1) for _ in range(m+1)]. for i in range(m+1): dp[i][0] = 1. for i in range(1, m+1): for j in range(1, n+1): dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j]. if s[i-1] == t[j-1]: dp[i][j] += dp[i-1][j-1]. return dp[m][n].
Walk 'rabbbit'/'rabbit': dp[7][6] = 3. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 12-18 min.
dont_do: Don't only count skips — miss use case.
Don't init dp[i][0] = 0.
Don't substring.
Don't recurse without memo.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up 2D DP
approaches.explanation: Count DP. dp[i][j] counts distinct subsequences of s[:i] matching t[:j]. Always have the option to skip s's i-th char (dp[i-1][j]). If chars match, additionally we can use s's i-th to match t's j-th (dp[i-1][j-1]). Base dp[i][0] = 1 since empty t is trivially formable. O(m*n).

title: Longest Increasing Path in a Matrix
summary: Given an m × n matrix, find the length of the longest strictly increasing path. From each cell, you can move 4-directionally to an adjacent cell. You cannot revisit cells.
starter_code:
def longestIncreasingPath(matrix):
    # DFS with memoization. dp[r][c] = longest increasing path STARTING at (r, c).
    # Recurse into neighbors with strictly greater values.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: DFS + memoization. dp[r][c] = longest increasing path STARTING at (r, c). For each cell: dp[r][c] = 1 + max(dp[nr][nc]) over neighbors with strictly greater values. Memoize to avoid recomputation. Answer is the max over all cells.

clarifying_questions.question: Strictly increasing — duplicates don't extend?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Use strict > for the move check.

clarifying_questions.question: 4-directional?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec.

clarifying_questions.question: Path length counted in nodes or edges?
clarifying_questions.why: Nodes per spec. Single cell = length 1.

clarifying_questions.question: Why is this DP not just DFS?
clarifying_questions.why: Pure DFS revisits subpaths. Memoization makes it O(m*n) since each cell's longest-path-from-here is computed once.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the matrix have all same values?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Longest path = 1 (each cell alone).

interview_tips.think_out_loud: DFS with memoization. For each cell, find the longest STRICTLY INCREASING path starting there. Memoize since multiple starts converge to the same subpaths. Take the global max. The graph is acyclic (values strictly increase along edges), so no visited tracking needed — memoization is sufficient.

edge_cases.details: Length 1.
No strict increase possible. Length 1.
Length n.
Algorithm handles 4-directional only.
200x200=40k cells. O(m*n) memoized DFS handles easily.

common_mistakes: Pure DFS without memo — exponential.
Using <= instead of < — allows equal-value steps.
Tracking visited within DFS — unnecessary (strict increase prevents cycles).
Computing only from corners — must try every cell as start.
Counting in edges instead of nodes.

faang.introduction: DFS + memoization on a grid. The classic 'memoized DAG DFS' pattern.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Google, Microsoft. The recognition that the graph is acyclic is the key insight.
really_testing: Identifying the implicit DAG (strict-increase prevents cycles).

rubric.criteria: Memoized DFS in 10-12 min. Explains DAG-ness.
DFS with explicit visited; memoization added after prompting.
DFS without memo; can't see the optimization.
Cannot articulate DFS structure.

playbook: Restate: 'longest strictly increasing 4-directional path'.
Insight: 'strict-increase → DAG → memoized DFS works'.

Code: from functools import lru_cache. m, n = len(matrix), len(matrix[0]). @lru_cache(None). def dfs(r, c): best = 1. for dr,dc in [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]: nr,nc = r+dr,c+dc. if 0<=nr<m and 0<=nc<n and matrix[nr][nc] > matrix[r][c]: best = max(best, 1 + dfs(nr, nc)). return best. return max(dfs(r, c) for r in range(m) for c in range(n)).
Walk 3x3 example: dfs from each cell, memoized; max over all is 4. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 12-18 min. Follow-up: count all paths.
dont_do: Don't pure DFS without memo.
Don't use <= for moves.
Don't only DFS from corners.
Don't track visited set — strict increase prevents revisits.

approaches.name: DFS with Memoization
approaches.explanation: DFS computes longest increasing path STARTING at each cell. Memoization caches each cell's result, so each cell's DFS is computed exactly once. The strict-greater condition ensures no cycles — the implicit graph is a DAG. Final answer is the max over all starting cells. O(m*n).

title: Burst Balloons
summary: You are given n balloons in a row, each with a number nums[i] painted on it. When you burst the i-th balloon, you earn nums[i-1] * nums[i] * nums[i+1] coins. After bursting, the gap closes. Return the maximum coins. (Out-of-range neighbors are treated as 1.)

examples.explanation: Burst 1 first: 1*1*5 = 5. Burst 5: 1*5*1 = 5. Total 10. Or burst 5 first: 1*5*1 = 5; burst 1: 1*1*1 = 1. Total 6. So burst 1 first = 10.

starter_code:
def maxCoins(nums):
    # Reverse the thinking: instead of choosing which to burst FIRST,
    # decide which is the LAST one to burst in each subinterval (i, j).
    # dp[i][j] = max coins from bursting all balloons strictly between i and j.
    # Pad with 1s on both ends to simplify boundary cases.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: The trick: instead of choosing which to burst FIRST, choose which is the LAST to burst in each subinterval. With the 'last' fixed, its neighbors are well-defined (the original boundary balloons, not modified by previous bursts). Pad nums with [1] on both ends. dp[i][j] = max coins from bursting all balloons in OPEN interval (i, j). For each k in (i, j) as the LAST balloon to burst there, dp[i][j] = max over k of dp[i][k] + dp[k][j] + nums[i] * nums[k] * nums[j].

clarifying_questions.question: What about boundaries — what's outside the array?
clarifying_questions.why: Treat as 1. So padding with [1] simplifies. The first and last 'burst' multiplies by 1 on the missing side.

clarifying_questions.question: Burst order matters how?
clarifying_questions.why: Bursting changes the neighbors. So greedy fails — you can't just pick max each step.

clarifying_questions.question: Why 'last to burst' not 'first to burst'?
clarifying_questions.why: Last-to-burst has stable neighbors (the original ones at the open-interval endpoints). First-to-burst's neighbors depend on what's already gone — much harder to track.

clarifying_questions.question: Time complexity expected?
clarifying_questions.why: O(n³). For each (i, j, k) where i ≤ k ≤ j. With n=300, that's ~27 million — fast enough.

clarifying_questions.question: Is this a classical 'interval DP'?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — it's the textbook interval DP problem along with Matrix Chain Multiplication.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Interval DP. Pad nums with 1 on both ends. Build dp[i][j] for open intervals (i, j). For length len from 2 (so j - i = 2, one ball between) to n+1, iterate i: j = i + len. For each k between i and j: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i][j], dp[i][k] + dp[k][j] + nums[i] * nums[k] * nums[j]). Answer: dp[0][n+1].

edge_cases.details: Just nums[0] * 1 * 1 = nums[0].
Bursting in any order. Algorithm finds optimal.
Zero balloons may be best to burst last (or never matter — their contribution at burst time is 0).
Return 0. Defensive check.
Two orderings — algorithm tries both.

common_mistakes: Greedy max each step — fails.
DP on 'first to burst' — neighbors change as you burst, hard to track.
Forgetting to pad with 1s — off-by-one boundary issues.
Indexing inclusive when open intervals are exclusive (off-by-one in k loops).
Recursion without memoization — O(2^n).

faang.introduction: Interval DP. The 'reverse the thinking' trick is the entire test.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Google, Meta. Famous as one of the harder DPs — most candidates struggle without seeing it before.

rubric.criteria: Inversion in 3-5 min. Codes in 15 min. Walks through small example.
Stuck on 'first to burst', refactors after hint.
Stays stuck on greedy or 'first-to-burst' DP.
Cannot derive.

playbook: Restate: 'burst all balloons; coins from each burst depend on current neighbors'.
Key insight: 'pick LAST to burst — its neighbors at burst time are the open-interval endpoints'.
Padding: nums = [1] + nums + [1].

Code: nums = [1] + nums + [1]. n = len(nums). dp = [[0]*n for _ in range(n)]. for length in range(2, n): for i in range(n - length): j = i + length. for k in range(i+1, j): dp[i][j] = max(dp[i][j], dp[i][k] + dp[k][j] + nums[i]*nums[k]*nums[j]). return dp[0][n-1].
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 15-22 min.
dont_do: Don't greedy.
Don't 'first-to-burst' DP.
Don't skip the padding.
Don't recurse without memo.

approaches.name: Interval DP, Last-to-Burst
approaches.code:
def maxCoins(nums):
    # Pad with 1s on both ends to simplify boundary multiplication
    nums = [1] + nums + [1]
    n = len(nums)
    
    # dp[i][j] = max coins from bursting balloons in OPEN interval (i, j)
    dp = [[0] * n for _ in range(n)]
    
    # length is the gap between the open-interval endpoints
    for length in range(2, n):
        for i in range(n - length):
            j = i + length
            for k in range(i + 1, j):
                # k is the LAST balloon to burst in (i, j)
                dp[i][j] = max(
                    dp[i][j],
                    dp[i][k] + dp[k][j] + nums[i] * nums[k] * nums[j]
                )
    return dp[0][n - 1]
approaches.explanation: Interval DP with the key inversion: instead of choosing which balloon to burst FIRST, choose which to burst LAST in each open interval. When k bursts last in (i, j), its neighbors are exactly nums[i] and nums[j] (the boundary balloons of the interval haven't been touched yet by definition). dp[i][k] and dp[k][j] are the optimal subproblems on the left and right halves. O(n³) — three nested loops over n.

title: Regular Expression Matching
summary: Given an input string s and a pattern p, implement regular expression matching with support for '.' (matches any single character) and '*' (matches zero or more of the preceding element). The match must cover the ENTIRE input string.
constraints: s contains only lowercase English letters.
p contains only lowercase English letters, '.', and '*'.
It is guaranteed that for each '*', there is a previous valid character.

examples.explanation: '.*' matches zero or more of any character.

starter_code:
def isMatch(s, p):
    # dp[i][j] = does s[:i] match p[:j].
    # Case: p[j-1] == '*' → either zero (dp[i][j-2]) or one+ (dp[i-1][j] if char matches).
    # Case: p[j-1] == s[i-1] or '.' → dp[i-1][j-1].
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: 2D DP. dp[i][j] = does s[:i] match p[:j]? Base: dp[0][0] = True. Cases for dp[i][j] depend on p[j-1]: (1) If p[j-1] is a literal/'.' AND matches s[i-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]. (2) If p[j-1] is '*': either zero occurrences (dp[i][j-2]) or one+ occurrences (s[i-1] matches p[j-2] AND dp[i-1][j]).

clarifying_questions.question: What characters does '.' match?
clarifying_questions.why: Any SINGLE character, not zero. '.' alone matches exactly one char.

clarifying_questions.question: What does '*' modify?
clarifying_questions.why: The preceding element (single char or '.'). '*' alone is invalid; spec guarantees it's preceded by a valid element.

clarifying_questions.question: Must match the ENTIRE s?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. dp[len(s)][len(p)] is the answer.

clarifying_questions.question: Anchored at start?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — implicit. No partial matches.

clarifying_questions.question: Common gotchas?
clarifying_questions.why: Empty s matching a*: True. Empty p matching anything but empty s: False. '*' zero-occurrence case is the trickiest.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Build a 2D DP table of size (|s|+1) x (|p|+1). dp[0][0] = True (empty matches empty). For empty s: dp[0][j] is True iff pattern up to j vanishes (e.g., 'a*b*'). For general (i, j): handle the cases above. The '*' two-branch logic (zero or one+ occurrences) is the core complexity.

edge_cases.details: True.
Pattern can collapse — dp[0][j] propagates only if p[j-1] == '*'.
True.
Zero occurrences keeps it alive.
Greedy — matches anything.

common_mistakes: Treating '*' as 'one or more' instead of 'zero or more'.
Forgetting the dp[0][j] initialization (pattern can collapse).
Off-by-one indexing (s[i] vs s[i-1]).
Mismatched logic order — must check '*' before checking literal match.
Recursing without memoization.

faang.introduction: Hard string DP. THE pattern matching problem.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Google. Tests whether you can derive a multi-case recurrence cleanly.

rubric.criteria: Cases enumerated in 5 min, codes in 15-18 min. Walks through tricky case.
Memoization first; refactors to bottom-up.
Wrong '*' semantics; misses zero-occurrence case.
Cannot derive recurrence.

playbook: Restate: 'regex matching with . and *'.
Enumerate cases for p[j-1]: literal/dot vs '*'.
For '*': zero (dp[i][j-2]) or one+ (s[i-1] matches p[j-2] and dp[i-1][j]).

Code: m, n = len(s), len(p). dp = [[False]*(n+1) for _ in range(m+1)]. dp[0][0] = True. for j in range(1, n+1): if p[j-1] == '*': dp[0][j] = dp[0][j-2]. for i in range(1, m+1): for j in range(1, n+1): if p[j-1] == '*': dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-2] or (dp[i-1][j] and (p[j-2] == s[i-1] or p[j-2] == '.')). else: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] and (p[j-1] == s[i-1] or p[j-1] == '.'). return dp[m][n].
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 15-25 min. Follow-up: extend to '+' or '?'.
dont_do: Don't treat '*' as one or more.
Don't skip dp[0][j] init.
Don't recurse without memo.
Don't forget the dot-matches-any-single-char rule.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up 2D DP
approaches.explanation: 2D DP. dp[i][j] = does s[:i] match p[:j]? '*' is the tricky case: it allows zero occurrences (collapse, jump j by 2) OR one+ occurrences (if current pair matches, extend from dp[i-1][j]). The base row dp[0][j] handles patterns that can match empty s (a*b*c* etc). O(m*n) time and space.

title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
summary: Design a data structure that supports the following operations on a set of strings: insert(word), search(word) — returns True iff word is in the trie, startsWith(prefix) — returns True iff any word starts with prefix.
constraints: word and prefix consist only of lowercase English letters.

examples.input: ops: insert('apple'), search('apple'), search('app'), startsWith('app'), insert('app'), search('app')
examples.explanation: After insert 'apple': search 'apple' True; search 'app' False (no terminal); startsWith 'app' True. After insert 'app': search 'app' True.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Trie with 26-ary tree (or dict-based). Each node has children dict and an `is_end` flag marking end of an inserted word. Insert: walk down, creating nodes; mark end. Search: walk; verify is_end at final. StartsWith: walk; just verify traversal succeeded.

clarifying_questions.question: Lowercase English letters only?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. Could use 26-element array for speed; dict is fine and simpler.

clarifying_questions.question: Words can be prefixes of other words?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. 'app' and 'apple' both insertable; is_end flag distinguishes them.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty string?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints, length >= 1. Defensive code can mark root as is_end if empty inserted.

clarifying_questions.question: Trie vs hash set?
clarifying_questions.why: Hash set supports insert and search but NOT startsWith efficiently. Trie wins for prefix operations.

clarifying_questions.question: Memory concerns?
clarifying_questions.why: Dict-of-dicts is fine for 30k operations × 2000 char words. Array-based would be slightly faster but uses more memory.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Simple Trie. Each node: children (dict) + is_end (bool). Insert walks chars, creating missing children, marks end at the final node. Search walks chars; returns False if path breaks; True if path completes AND last node is_end. startsWith is like search but doesn't require is_end.

edge_cases.details: Just mark intermediate node as is_end.
Extend the trie. Both words coexist.
Path breaks → False.
Path breaks → False.
Standard handling — one node child of root.

common_mistakes: Forgetting is_end flag — can't distinguish 'apple' from 'app' insertions.
Confusing search with startsWith.
Using a hash set — fails on startsWith.
Off-by-one walking the trie.
Memory bloat with 26-element arrays at each node — fine but dict is cleaner.

faang.introduction: Trie design classic. Foundation for many prefix-based problems.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Google, Meta. Quick if you've seen tries; struggle otherwise.
really_testing: Knowing trie's tradeoff vs hash set.
Clean code with proper helper methods.

rubric.criteria: Codes all three methods in 8-10 min.
Correct with one method bug fixed.
Tries hash set; can't fix startsWith.
Can't articulate trie structure.

playbook: Restate: 'insert, search (exact), startsWith (prefix)'.
Pitch trie with dict-based children + is_end flag.
Code Trie class with __init__, insert, search, startsWith.
insert: node = self. for ch in word: if ch not in node.children: node.children[ch] = TrieNode(). node = node.children[ch]. node.is_end = True.
search: walk; if any char missing → False. End: return node.is_end.
startsWith: walk; if any char missing → False. End: return True (regardless of is_end).
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min. Follow-up: Design Add and Search Words (LC 211), Word Search II (LC 212).
dont_do: Don't use hash set.
Don't omit is_end.
Don't confuse search and startsWith.
Don't allocate 26 children per node unless asked for speed.

approaches.name: Dict-Based Trie
approaches.time: O(m) per operation where m = word/prefix length
approaches.explanation: Standard dict-based Trie. Each node has a children dict and is_end flag. _walk is a helper that traverses the trie returning the final node (or None if any char missing). search uses _walk + is_end check; startsWith just uses _walk. Each operation is O(m) where m is the input length.

title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
summary: Design a data structure that supports adding words and searching them. search(word) may contain '.' which matches any single letter. The data structure must support both efficiently.
constraints: addWord/search consist of lowercase English letters and '.' for search.

examples.input: ops: addWord('bad'), addWord('dad'), addWord('mad'), search('pad'), search('bad'), search('.ad'), search('b..')

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Trie + DFS for wildcards. addWord is identical to Trie's insert. search becomes a recursive DFS: for each '.', try every child; otherwise walk a single child if present.

clarifying_questions.question: Wildcard is just '.'?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. No '*' or character classes.

clarifying_questions.question: Word length bound?
clarifying_questions.why: ≤ 25. Recursion depth fine.

clarifying_questions.question: DFS or BFS for wildcards?
clarifying_questions.why: DFS — natural fit. BFS works but more state.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Trie with DFS-search. On '.', try every child node recursively. On a letter, follow that child if it exists. Return True iff some recursion path completes at is_end.

edge_cases.details: Matches any word of that length.
DFS exhausts string before reaching is_end → False.
False.

common_mistakes: Iterative search — doesn't handle wildcards.
Forgetting is_end check at recursion's end.
Not branching on '.' at every level.
Recursion without proper base case (string end + is_end).

faang.introduction: Trie + DFS variant. Tests adapting standard trie to handle wildcards.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Google.

rubric.criteria: Codes both in 10-12 min.
Correct with minor bug.
Misses wildcard branching.
Can't extend trie.

playbook: Trie skeleton.
search: recursive DFS with (node, index). If index == len, return node.is_end. If word[i] == '.': any(dfs(child, i+1)). Else: dfs(children.get(word[i]), i+1).
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-15 min.
dont_do: Don't iterate when wildcards present.
Don't forget is_end at terminator.

approaches.name: Trie with Recursive DFS Search
approaches.time: O(M) per add, O(26^k * M) worst case per search (k = wildcards)
approaches.explanation: Trie addWord is standard. search uses recursive DFS — at '.', try every child. At a letter, follow it if present. Return is_end at the string's end.

title: Word Search II
summary: Given an m × n board of characters and a list of words, return all words from the list that can be constructed by adjacent (4-directional) board cells, where each cell is used at most once per word. Output may be in any order.
constraints: All words are unique lowercase English letters.
starter_code:
def findWords(board, words):
    # Build a Trie of words. DFS from each board cell, walking trie + board simultaneously.
    # Collect words at each terminal trie node hit. Sort output for deterministic order.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Build a Trie of all words. DFS from each cell on the board, walking the Trie in lockstep. When we hit a Trie node with end=True, record that word (deduplicated). Mark cells visited (e.g., temporarily). The Trie lets us prune impossible paths early.

clarifying_questions.question: 4-directional?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes.

clarifying_questions.question: Each cell once per word, but reusable across words?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes.

clarifying_questions.question: Return order?
clarifying_questions.why: Any. Sort for determinism.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Naive: DFS per word — O(words * m*n*4^len). Trie: DFS once per cell; Trie tracks all words simultaneously. Each cell's DFS walks the Trie; matches found at end-nodes; remove found words from Trie to prune.

edge_cases.details: Empty list.
Just the letter.
Skipped naturally.

common_mistakes: Per-word DFS — too slow.
Not marking visited (cycles within one word).
Forgetting to unmark on backtrack.
Duplicates in output.

faang.introduction: Trie + DFS backtracking on a grid. Combines two patterns elegantly.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Google, Meta.

rubric.criteria: Trie + DFS in 15-20 min. Mentions pruning.
Solves correctly but doesn't optimize.
Per-word DFS only; TLE.
Can't structure.

playbook: Build Trie of all words.
DFS from each cell: walk Trie + board. At end-node, record word.
Mark visited (mutate to '#' or use set), restore after.
Sort output for determinism.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 15-22 min.
dont_do: Don't per-word DFS.
Don't forget backtracking.
Don't duplicate matches.

approaches.name: Trie + DFS
approaches.explanation: Build a Trie of all target words. From each board cell, DFS walking the Trie and board in lockstep. When we reach a Trie terminal node (marked with '$'), record the word. Mutate board cells to '#' during recursion to prevent cycles; restore on backtrack. Sort the final set for deterministic output.

title: Jump Game
summary: You are given an integer array nums. You start at index 0. Each element represents the maximum jump length at that position. Return True if you can reach the last index.

examples.explanation: Jump 1 step from 0 to 1, then 3 steps to last.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Greedy. Track the furthest index reachable so far. Walk left to right; if current index > furthest, we're stuck. Otherwise update furthest = max(furthest, i + nums[i]). If furthest reaches or exceeds n-1, return True.

clarifying_questions.question: Start at index 0?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes.

clarifying_questions.question: Reach exactly n-1 or beyond?
clarifying_questions.why: Either — covering n-1 is enough.

clarifying_questions.question: Negative jumps?
clarifying_questions.why: No — non-negative per spec.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Naive: BFS/DFS from each cell — O(n²). Greedy: single pass tracking max_reach. If max_reach ever falls behind current index, return False.

edge_cases.details: Already at last index — True.
Stuck at index 0 unless n=1.
Reach the end in one move.
Always reachable.

common_mistakes: Recursion without memo — O(2^n).
DP O(n²) — works but suboptimal.
Off-by-one (n-1 vs n).
Missing the early-exit when i > max_reach.

faang.introduction: Greedy classic. The single-pass max-reach trick.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta.

rubric.criteria: Greedy in 3-5 min.
DP first; refactors to greedy.
Exponential recursion.
Can't derive.

playbook: Restate: 'reach last index from 0'.
Greedy: 'max_reach = 0. for i, x in enumerate(nums): if i > max_reach: return False. max_reach = max(max_reach, i + x). if max_reach >= n-1: return True. return True.'
Walk [2,3,1,1,4]: reaches end. ✓
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 4-7 min.
dont_do: Don't recurse.
Don't over-engineer with DP.

approaches.name: Greedy Max-Reach
approaches.explanation: Greedy. max_reach tracks the furthest index we can possibly reach. If we ever step past max_reach, we're stuck. Otherwise update max_reach with each jump option. Early-exit if max_reach reaches end.

title: Jump Game II
summary: Given nums of non-negative ints, you start at 0; nums[i] is max jump from i. Return MIN number of jumps to reach last index. You're guaranteed to be able to reach it.
starter_code:
def jump(nums):
    # Greedy BFS-like. Track current jump's reach and the farthest reachable from THIS jump.
    # When we step past current reach, take another jump (= use the farthest seen).
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Greedy 'BFS by levels'. Track current_end (the furthest index in the current jump's reach) and farthest (the best reach we've seen scanning this level). When i hits current_end, we must take another jump → set current_end = farthest, increment jumps.

clarifying_questions.question: Guaranteed reachable?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec. No need to return -1.

clarifying_questions.question: Min jumps?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Greedy gives optimal here.

clarifying_questions.question: Index 0 counts as 'taken' jump or not?
clarifying_questions.why: Not — 0 jumps if already at last index.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Imagine BFS where each level is one jump. Level 0 covers indices [0, nums[0]]. Level 1 extends to max(i + nums[i]) over level 0. Repeat until reaching n-1.

edge_cases.details: 0 jumps.
1 jump.

edge_cases.category: All zeros after big first jump
edge_cases.details: Depends on whether first jump covers.

common_mistakes: O(n²) DP — works but slow.
Forgetting to skip when already past last index.

faang.introduction: Greedy with BFS-like levels.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta.

rubric.criteria: Greedy in 6-10 min.
DP O(n²) version; refactors when prompted.
Recursion only.
Can't derive.

playbook: jumps=0; current_end=0; farthest=0. for i in range(n-1): farthest=max(farthest, i+nums[i]). if i==current_end: jumps+=1; current_end=farthest. return jumps.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 6-10 min.
dont_do: Don't iterate to n (only to n-1).
Don't forget to update current_end.

approaches.name: Greedy BFS-by-Levels
approaches.explanation: Greedy. As you scan, track the farthest you could reach within the current jump's range. When you exhaust the current range (i == current_end), you must take another jump — set current_end to farthest. O(n).

title: Gas Station
summary: n gas stations on a circular route. gas[i] is the amount of gas at station i. cost[i] is gas needed to travel from i to i+1. Return the starting station index to complete one circuit, or -1 if impossible.
starter_code:
def canCompleteCircuit(gas, cost):
    # If total gas >= total cost, a solution exists.
    # Greedy: when tank goes negative, restart from the next station.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Two insights: (1) if sum(gas) < sum(cost), impossible. (2) When the running tank dips below 0 at some i, NO station in [start..i] can be the start — restart from i+1. This makes it O(n).

clarifying_questions.question: Unique answer if exists?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec.

clarifying_questions.question: Circular travel?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — wrap around.

clarifying_questions.question: Why does the restart trick work?
clarifying_questions.why: If we hit -1 at index i starting from start, every j in [start..i] would also fail (they have even less tank). So restart from i+1.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: If total gas < total cost, return -1. Else find start: walk; maintain tank = +gas[i] - cost[i]. When tank < 0, restart start = i+1, reset tank.

edge_cases.details: Works iff gas[0] >= cost[0].
sum(gas) < sum(cost) → -1.
Algorithm finds valid start.

common_mistakes: O(n²) try-every-start.
Forgetting the impossibility check.
Resetting start to i instead of i+1.

faang.introduction: Greedy with restart trick.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Bloomberg.

rubric.criteria: Both insights in 5-8 min.
O(n²); refactors with hint.
Bug in restart logic.
Brute force only.

playbook: if sum(gas) < sum(cost): return -1.
tank=0; start=0. for i: tank += gas[i]-cost[i]. if tank<0: tank=0; start=i+1. return start.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 6-10 min.
dont_do: Don't try every start.
Don't forget impossibility check.

approaches.name: Greedy with Restart
approaches.explanation: If total gas < total cost, no solution. Otherwise walk once. When tank dips negative, every start from old start through current i is impossible → restart from i+1. Total fuel non-negative guarantees a valid start exists.

title: Hand of Straights
summary: Given an integer array hand and an integer groupSize, return True if you can rearrange hand into groups of groupSize CONSECUTIVE integers.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: If len(hand) % groupSize != 0, False. Use a Counter. Iterate cards in sorted order. For each card with positive count, form a group of groupSize consecutive — decrement each. If any number missing, False.

clarifying_questions.question: Consecutive integers (n, n+1, ..., n+k-1)?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec.

clarifying_questions.question: Each card used once?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — partition.

clarifying_questions.question: Counter or sorted list?
clarifying_questions.why: Counter for lookups; sorted iteration via sorted(set(hand)).

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Greedy: smallest unused card must start a group. Form that group, decrement each. Repeat. If any required card is missing or count 0, return False.

edge_cases.details: Always True.
False.
Groups can skip gaps if each group is consecutive.

common_mistakes: Sorting hand directly and iterating — works but slow.
Forgetting the divisibility check.
Not skipping zero-count cards.

faang.introduction: Greedy with hash count.
faang.introduction_context: Common at Amazon, Bloomberg.

rubric.criteria: Greedy + Counter in 8 min.
Solves with sort + linear scan.
Tries to enumerate groups.
Can't derive.

playbook: if len(hand) % gs != 0: return False.
count = Counter(hand). for card in sorted(count): while count[card] > 0: for k in range(gs): if count[card+k] < count[card]+... actually iterate: take group starting at card, decrement each.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min.
dont_do: Don't skip divisibility check.
Don't iterate without sort.

approaches.name: Counter + Greedy Sorted
approaches.explanation: Sort unique cards ascending. For each, if count > 0, it must be the start of `count` distinct groups — verify each of [card, card+1, ..., card+gs-1] has at least `count` remaining, then subtract `count` from each. Linear in unique values × groupSize.

title: Merge Triplets to Form Target Triplet
summary: Given triplets and a target triplet, you may take any pair of triplets [a,b,c] and [d,e,f] and update them both to [max(a,d), max(b,e), max(c,f)]. Return True if you can reach the target.
starter_code:
def mergeTriplets(triplets, target):
    # Filter to triplets where every component is <= target's corresponding.
    # Check that for each of 3 positions, at least one kept triplet matches target.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Two key insights: (1) any triplet with a component > target[i] is unusable — it would push the merged result above target. (2) Among USABLE triplets, we just need to find one matching target[0], one matching target[1], one matching target[2] (could be the same triplet).

clarifying_questions.question: Multiple merges OK?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — any number.

clarifying_questions.question: Order matters?
clarifying_questions.why: No — merging is commutative on max.

clarifying_questions.question: Can one triplet satisfy all three positions?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — that's the simplest case.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Filter useful triplets (none > target). Among them, accumulate which positions matched. If all three positions reached, True.

edge_cases.details: Trivially True.
Some position unreachable → False.
Algorithm finds them by tracking positions.

common_mistakes: Trying to enumerate merges.
Not filtering triplets > target.
Confusing 'all 3' vs 'at least 3'.

faang.introduction: Greedy filter + track.
faang.introduction_context: Amazon, Microsoft.

rubric.criteria: Filter+track in 6 min.
Correct after one bug fixed.
Misses filtering.
Can't derive.

playbook: good = set(). for t in triplets: if all(t[i] <= target[i] for i in range(3)): for i in range(3): if t[i] == target[i]: good.add(i). return len(good) == 3.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 6-10 min.
dont_do: Don't enumerate merges.
Don't keep triplets with components > target.

approaches.name: Filter + Position Tracking
approaches.explanation: Skip any triplet that exceeds target in any component (would poison the max merge). For usable triplets, track which target positions they match. If all 3 positions covered, the target is reachable.

title: Partition Labels
summary: You are given a string s. Partition s into as many parts as possible so that each letter appears in at most one part. Return the lengths of each part.
constraints: s consists of lowercase English letters.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Precompute the LAST index of each character. Walk s; maintain current partition's [start, end]. For each i, extend end = max(end, last[s[i]]). When i == end, record the partition length and reset.

clarifying_questions.question: Each letter in only one part?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec — non-overlapping intervals of occurrences.

clarifying_questions.question: Maximize part count?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — as many parts as possible.

clarifying_questions.question: Output type?
clarifying_questions.why: List of part lengths.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: For partition starting at start, must include every occurrence of every letter that appears in [start, ...]. So end = max last[s[i]] for i in current partition. When i reaches end, close it.

edge_cases.details: One partition of size 1.
n partitions of size 1.
1 partition of size n.

common_mistakes: Not pre-computing last positions.
Forgetting to reset start.
Off-by-one on partition length.

faang.introduction: Greedy interval-merge classic.
faang.introduction_context: Amazon, Meta.

rubric.criteria: Codes in 5-8 min.
Solves correctly with one bug.
Can't track partition end.
Can't derive.

playbook: last = {ch: i for i, ch in enumerate(s)}. start, end = 0, 0. result = []. for i, ch in enumerate(s): end = max(end, last[ch]). if i == end: result.append(end-start+1); start = i+1. return result.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 5-8 min.
dont_do: Don't iterate looking forward.
Don't forget to update start.

approaches.name: Last-Occurrence + Greedy
approaches.explanation: Precompute the last index of each character. As we scan, the current partition must extend to at least the last occurrence of every character we've seen. When i reaches end, the partition is complete.

title: Valid Parenthesis String
summary: Given a string s containing '(', ')', and '*', return True if s is valid. '*' can be treated as '(', ')', or empty.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Track [min_open, max_open]: the range of possible open-paren counts. '(' increments both. ')' decrements both. '*' decrements min, increments max. If max < 0, impossible. If min < 0, clamp to 0 (any prefix making min < 0 was unbalanced — but we treat * as ')' in those). At end, min == 0 means we can balance.

clarifying_questions.question: * flexible — any of 3?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per spec.

clarifying_questions.question: Order matters?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — must be balanced at every prefix.

clarifying_questions.question: Stack approach?
clarifying_questions.why: Works but more complex. Greedy two-counter is cleaner.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Two counters track the range of possible open-counts under any * interpretation. Greedy: ensure max never goes negative; clamp min to 0 (handle unbalanceable but possibly-OK prefixes). At end, min == 0 must hold.

edge_cases.details: True — pair them as () or empty.
max goes negative early.
False.

title: Insert Interval
summary: Given a sorted list of non-overlapping intervals and a new interval, insert it in the right place and merge if necessary.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Three phases: (1) Copy intervals ending before newInterval starts. (2) Merge while there's overlap with newInterval (expand its boundaries). (3) Copy remaining intervals.

clarifying_questions.question: Input sorted?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — by start.

clarifying_questions.question: Output sorted with no overlaps?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes.

clarifying_questions.question: Touching intervals merge?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — [1,3] and [3,5] merge to [1,5].

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Single pass with 3 phases. Phase 1: intervals fully before new. Phase 2: overlap — extend new's bounds. Phase 3: intervals fully after.

edge_cases.details: Just return [newInterval].
Append newInterval after.
Prepend newInterval.
Single merged interval.

common_mistakes: O(n log n) sort+merge — works but ignores pre-sorted property.
Off-by-one in phase 2 loop.

faang.introduction: Interval insertion classic.
faang.introduction_context: Onsite at Amazon, Meta.

rubric.criteria: 3-phase in 8-10 min.
Sort+merge solution; correct.
Off-by-one bugs.
Can't derive.

playbook: result = []. i = 0. n = len(intervals).
while i < n and intervals[i][1] < newInterval[0]: result.append(intervals[i]); i += 1.
while i < n and intervals[i][0] <= newInterval[1]: newInterval[0] = min(newInterval[0], intervals[i][0]); newInterval[1] = max(newInterval[1], intervals[i][1]); i += 1.
result.append(newInterval).
while i < n: result.append(intervals[i]); i += 1.
return result.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min.
dont_do: Don't sort if already sorted.
Don't forget the merge expansion.

approaches.name: Three-Phase Pass
approaches.code:
def insert(intervals, newInterval):
    result = []
    i = 0
    n = len(intervals)
    
    # Phase 1: intervals entirely before newInterval
    while i < n and intervals[i][1] < newInterval[0]:
        result.append(intervals[i])
        i += 1
    
    # Phase 2: merge overlapping
    while i < n and intervals[i][0] <= newInterval[1]:
        newInterval = [min(newInterval[0], intervals[i][0]), max(newInterval[1], intervals[i][1])]
        i += 1
    result.append(newInterval)
    
    # Phase 3: intervals entirely after
    while i < n:
        result.append(intervals[i])
        i += 1
    
    return result
approaches.explanation: Linear three-phase scan exploiting the sorted input. Phase 1 copies non-overlapping prefix. Phase 2 expands newInterval to absorb all overlaps. Phase 3 copies the rest. O(n).

title: Non-overlapping Intervals

playbook: Sort intervals by start.

result = [-1]*len(queries). heap = []. j = 0.

while j < n and intervals[j][0] <= q: l, r = intervals[j]; heapq.heappush(heap, (r-l+1, r)); j += 1.

approaches.name: Offline Sort + Min-Heap
approaches.code:
import heapq

def minInterval(intervals, queries):
    intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
    qs = sorted((q, i) for i, q in enumerate(queries))
    result = [-1] * len(queries)
    heap = []  # (length, end)
    j = 0
    for q, idx in qs:
        while j < len(intervals) and intervals[j][0] <= q:
            l, r = intervals[j]
            heapq.heappush(heap, (r - l + 1, r))
            j += 1
        while heap and heap[0][1] < q:
            heapq.heappop(heap)
        if heap:
            result[idx] = heap[0][0]
    return result
approaches.explanation: Offline. Sort intervals by start and queries by value (keeping original indices). For each query, push all intervals starting at-or-before it into a min-heap keyed by length. Pop intervals whose end is before the query (no longer covering). Top = shortest covering interval.

title: Spiral Matrix

Walk down; done.
Bounds shrink at different rates.

Forgetting to check bounds after each direction.

playbook: top, bottom = 0, len(matrix)-1. left, right = 0, len(matrix[0])-1.

for c in range(left, right+1): result.append(matrix[top][c]). top += 1.
for r in range(top, bottom+1): result.append(matrix[r][right]). right -= 1.
if top <= bottom: for c in range(right, left-1, -1): result.append(matrix[bottom][c]). bottom -= 1.
if left <= right: for r in range(bottom, top-1, -1): result.append(matrix[r][left]). left += 1.

title: Set Matrix Zeroes

Track separately whether row 0 or col 0 originally had a 0. Pass 2: based on markers, zero each cell. Pass 3: zero row 0/col 0 if flagged.

clarifying_questions.why: Spec says int range; can't use a sentinel value safely.
